original_prompt:
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-a2
Selected app slug: cleanline

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# cleanline

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

cleanline

## Category

Graphics & Design

## One-Sentence Promise

Photographs a hand-drawn sketch and traces it to clean, editable vector paths (SVG/PDF) via on-device Vision.

## Target User

Illustrators/print-on-demand sellers who sketch on paper but need vector output.

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 6 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 2 Food & Drink apps, 2 Graphics & Design apps, and 2 News apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 12 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 6, and build all 6 to production quality.

phase_discussions.title: Prompt Contract
phase_discussions.output:
All three points actually landed this round. Codex accepted the scope fence and the Vision-isn't-magic framing as settled, and I explicitly confirmed the scope fence too, so that one's closed. Codex made the case that full in-app Bezier/anchor editing would blow the MVP scope and drift toward the "desktop-class editor" non-goal, arguing the honest reading of "editable" is real vector paths that are editable downstream, with in-app cleanup limited to crop/perspective/threshold/simplify/delete-fragment tools. I pushed back some but ended up narrowing my own ask instead of holding the line on full node editing — landing on select/delete-path, simplify-strength slider, merge-paths, and undo/redo as the MVP in-app editing bar, with full anchor-point dragging explicitly deferred to a later pro tier (which doubles as an honest monetization hook). Codex hasn't confirmed that exact list yet, but it's a direct answer to what they raised, not a new fight — it looks like a landing point rather than an open dispute.

I also supplied the concrete "clean trace" quality bar Codex didn't answer directly last round: no orphan fragments under ~3pt, continuous strokes stay continuous, bounded node counts (so paths are actually drag-editable), opens cleanly in Illustrator/Affinity, and passes a human side-by-side resemblance check. And I put a testability condition on Codex's "capture rescue" pitch — it only counts as a real differentiator if it's proven against named conditions (e.g. degrees of skew, shadow coverage), with anything outside that producing an honest retake-photo error rather than a silent garbage trace, rather than just being marketing language.

Nothing here is a live disagreement anymore — it's Codex's direction plus my specifics, and my one revision (narrowing the editing ask) moved toward Codex's position rather than away from it. Gemini still unavailable.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**cleanline execution contract, locked for this phase:**

- **Scope fence:** This phase sequence builds one app — cleanline — end to end. The parent portfolio's 12-ideas/pick-6, cross-app uniqueness enforcement, Jira/Notion backfill, and combined-transcript instructions are inherited upstream constraints (respect uniqueness, keep it a separate project folder), not tasks this app's phases re-run. No later phase should regenerate concepts for cleanline or build portfolio-level artifacts as part of this app's output.

- **Hard requirements:** A real SwiftUI, local-first iOS MVP: capture or import a paper sketch photo, run it through a deterministic on-device processing pipeline (perspective correction, contrast/threshold isolation, noise reduction, contour extraction via Vision, path simplification/curve fitting — Vision alone is explicitly not sufficient and is named as an architectural assumption, not a late discovery), preview the traced result, do meaningful in-app cleanup, and export valid SVG/PDF. Empty/loading/success/error states everywhere the flow can reach them. Accessibility, privacy, local persistence, and testing are in scope, not stretch goals. Architecture isolates capture / preprocessing / vectorization / editing / export / persistence so cloud sync or iPad support can be added later without a rewrite.

- **"Editable" is defined concretely for MVP:** users get real in-app editing — select and delete a stray path, adjust simplify/smooth strength (global or per-path, re-runs curve fitting), merge selected paths, and undo/redo across those actions. Full anchor-point/Bezier-handle dragging is an explicit MVP non-goal, reserved as a credible future paid "pro editing" tier. Exported paths must be genuinely modifiable in downstream tools (Illustrator/Affinity), not a flattened image.

- **Clean-trace quality bar (the acceptance test later phases build and QA against):** no orphan fragments under ~3pt at export scale, continuous source strokes stay continuous, bounded node counts per curve (so paths are actually drag-editable downstream), the exported SVG opens without errors in a mainstream vector tool, and it passes a human side-by-side resemblance check against the source sketch. Tested against a small fixture set: a simple icon sketch, a lettering sketch, a closed-shape illustration, and a messy-but-recoverable notebook photo.

- **Capture-rescue is the differentiator, but only if provably bounded:** perspective correction, shadow/background cleanup, and contrast isolation are the "better than a scan app + desktop cleanup" pitch, but the contract requires this be validated against named conditions (e.g., degrees of skew, percent of frame in shadow) — outside those bounds the app must give an honest "retake photo" error, never a silently garbage trace.

- **Non-goals:** desktop-class Illustrator replacement, full node-by-node vector editing in MVP, generic AI art/style-transfer, server-side tracing, solving color-illustration vectorization in v1, and any portfolio-management/storefront features.

- **Monetization:** the core promise (photograph → usable vector export) must work free. Premium lives in higher-fidelity tracing, batch processing, cloud sync/backup, and advanced pro editing (including future full node editing) — never gating basic SVG/PDF export.

- **Decision rule for conflicts:** when scope tensions come up, protect trace/export quality and the honesty of the promise over feature breadth; when the parent portfolio and child spec seem to disagree, default to the portfolio's local-first/cloud-ready floor requirement and name it as an assumption rather than silently dropping it.

My execution contract for cleanline is: this app is not a vague “art utility”; it is a local-first iPhone app that takes a photographed paper sketch, extracts linework on-device, and exports genuinely usable vector output. The assignment is to build a working SwiftUI MVP now, but the quality bar is still production-oriented: the MVP has to do one valuable job end to end, reliably, privately, and with export quality that an illustrator or print seller can actually use.

Hard requirements:
- It must be its own separate project/folder for the portfolio batch, distinct from the other five apps.
- It must be a SwiftUI iOS app with real local-first behavior, not a prototype deck or mock flow.
- The core workflow must work on device: capture/import sketch, process image, trace into editable vector paths, preview result, and export SVG/PDF.
- The app must use Apple-first frameworks and deterministic local state unless a dependency materially reduces risk.
- It must serve illustrators and print-on-demand sellers specifically, so output quality, cleanup controls, and export usability matter more than generic “AI art” novelty.
- It must include empty, loading, success, and error states anywhere the workflow can reach them.
- Accessibility, privacy, persistence, and testing are required product scope, not stretch goals.
- It must have a credible monetization path tied to real value, likely premium export controls, batch processing, higher-fidelity tracing presets, or commercial-use workflow features.
- It must be architected so cloud sync/collaboration could be added later without rewriting the tracing/export core.

Non-goals for this MVP:
- Not a desktop-class Illustrator replacement.
- Not freeform vector editing with full pen-tool, layers, typography, or complex node-by-node editing parity.
- Not a generic AI image generator, style-transfer app, or text-to-art tool.
- Not dependent on server-side tracing; privacy and local-first are part of the product thesis.
- Not trying to solve color illustration vectorization perfectly in v1 if that weakens the core black-and-line sketch use case.
- Not a portfolio-management or storefront app for sellers; the value is trace-to-vector conversion, not commerce ops.

The output shape later phases must preserve is a narrow but sharp product: one clear hero flow, a premium design system, local image/document storage, a tracing engine abstraction, export pipeline, settings/presets, and enough onboarding/help to make the promise believable. If later phases expand scope, they should expand around the tracing workflow, not away from it.

Production-ready here means more than “builds and shows screens.” It means the app can be installed, a user can import or photograph a sketch, understand what is happening, adjust a small set of meaningful controls, inspect the traced result, export files that open correctly in standard vector workflows, and recover from common failures like poor lighting, low-contrast sketches, unsupported images, or export errors. It also means privacy copy is honest, local storage is deliberate, permissions are explained, core flows are tested, and obvious paper cuts are handled. For this app, production-readiness does not require feature breadth; it requires trustworthy output and a polished primary loop.

Decision rules for later phases:
- When scope tradeoffs appear, protect output quality and clarity of the main workflow before adding extra tools.
- Favor the narrowest user promise that can be fulfilled exceptionally well: clean line-art vectorization from paper sketches.
- Any feature that does not improve capture quality, trace quality, editability, export quality, or repeat use by illustrators is probably out of scope.
- If a technique improves novelty but weakens privacy, determinism, speed, or reliability, reject it.
- If Vision-only tracing cannot produce commercially useful vectors by itself, the implementation can add deterministic image-processing stages, but must keep the on-device/local-first promise intact.
- SVG/PDF export is not optional decoration; it is the commercial payload.
- “Editable vector” should be interpreted honestly: paths that can be opened and modified in downstream tools, not just a flattened image inside a wrapper file.
- Monetization decisions should align to pro workflow leverage, not lock up basic correctness.
- In design, premium should mean focused, calm, and confidence-building for creators, not visually loud.
- In architecture, isolate capture, preprocessing, vectorization, export, and persistence so later cloud or iPad expansion is possible without rewriting the app.

The boldest direction is to position cleanline as the fastest way to get from notebook sketch to sellable vector asset on an iPhone, with a surprisingly strong “capture rescue” layer: perspective correction, background cleanup, contrast isolation, and line simplification before tracing. That makes the app materially better than a generic scan app plus desktop cleanup, and it fits the target user’s real pain. The biggest objection to this direction is technical: “editable vector paths via on-device Vision” can easily collapse into low-quality auto-trace if the tracing stack is not strong enough. The final phase output must preserve that risk explicitly and refuse to fake success with pretty screenshots. If the exported vectors are not genuinely usable, the app is missing its reason to exist.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

---

Now my read on this, coming at it looking for what breaks.

First, a scope trap that I want flagged before anyone builds anything: this phase's file is scoped to a single child app — cleanline — but the prompt it's built from is the *parent portfolio* prompt, which talks about 12 candidate ideas, choosing 6, Jira/Notion backfill, parallel app efforts, combined transcripts. That parent-level content is not this phase's job. cleanline has already been selected — the "generate 12, pick 6" instruction is closed business from an earlier phase of the portfolio run, not something this phase re-litigates. If a later phase for this child project starts second-guessing whether cleanline should have been picked, or tries to regenerate candidate ideas, that's scope creep into a decision the parent process already made. The contract needs to say explicitly: for this child project, the app is fixed as cleanline; portfolio-level instructions (idea generation, selection, cross-app uniqueness enforcement, Jira/Notion, combined transcript file) are inherited constraints to respect, not tasks to redo here.

Second, the core technical claim in the one-sentence promise is the single biggest risk in the whole project and I don't think it should be accepted at face value: "traces sketch to clean, editable vector paths via on-device Vision." Vision framework (VNDetectContoursRequest, VNDetectRectanglesRequest, etc.) gives you contour detection and rectangle/edge detection — it does not give you a hand-drawn sketch turned into artist-clean, closed, editable Bezier paths comparable to Illustrator's Image Trace or Adobe's vectorization. Real sketches have broken linework, overlapping strokes, shading, paper texture, shadows from the phone camera, and non-uniform lighting. A naive contour trace on that input produces a noisy mess of tiny disconnected paths, not "clean" vectors. This needs to be named as an assumption now, not discovered during build: the MVP's vectorization pipeline is a custom algorithm (perspective correction → binarization/adaptive threshold → noise reduction → contour extraction via Vision → path simplification/smoothing, e.g. Douglas-Peucker + curve fitting) built on top of Vision primitives, not something Vision does for you out of the box. If we let a later phase assume Vision "just does" vectorization, the build phase will hit this wall late, which is expensive. I'd rather the architecture phase commit explicitly to a bounded first version of this pipeline (e.g., single dominant line color, decent lighting, reasonably high contrast paper) and treat "handles messy shading/multiple pens/poor lighting" as an explicit non-goal for MVP, with a fallback/manual-cleanup UI (adjustable threshold slider, eraser/merge tools on the traced paths) as the safety net when auto-trace is imperfect — because it will be imperfect often.

Third, "editable vector paths (SVG/PDF)" is doing a lot of work. Editable to what degree? If a user can only view/export the SVG but not actually manipulate individual anchor points/curves inside the app, that's not "editable," that's "exportable." I'd treat true in-app Bezier-path editing (drag anchor points, adjust curve handles, delete/merge segments) as a hard requirement implied by "editable," not optional polish — otherwise the one-sentence promise is false advertising. This has real scope: it means we need a path data model, a canvas renderer, hit-testing on path segments/anchors, undo/redo, and it needs empty/loading/success/error states like everything else. If time-boxing forces a cut, the decision rule should be: cut sophistication of the auto-trace before cutting the ability to manually edit the resulting paths — because manual editing is the escape hatch that makes an imperfect trace still commercially useful to an illustrator.

Fourth, monetization: requirement 5 demands "genuine value, never cosmetics-only" subscription. For a vectorization tool, the honest premium wall is around export formats, resolution/complexity limits, and batch/cloud-ready features — not filters. I'd flag the risk that a lazy design pass tries to gate "SVG export" itself behind paywall while leaving PDF export free, which would gut the core promise for free users and read as bait-and-switch. Decision rule I want on record: the core promise (photograph → get *some* usable vector output) must work free; monetization should live in higher-fidelity tracing, batch processing, cloud sync/backup, or advanced editing tools, not in blocking the basic value prop.

Fifth, production-readiness. "0 to production-ready" for a camera+Vision+vector-editing app is a nontrivial bar. I want it defined concretely, not left vague: camera permission handling with a real denied/restricted state (not a crash or blank screen), photo library import fallback for users without live camera access (simulator testing, iPad without camera, permission denied), handling of low-quality/blurry/no-edges-detected input with a real error state and retry path, local persistence of projects/traces (Core Data or file-based, survives app relaunch), an actual export flow to Files/Share sheet that produces valid SVG/PDF files (this needs to be verified by opening the exported file in another app, not just "code compiles"), accessibility (VoiceOver on canvas editing is genuinely hard — decide now whether canvas interactions get real accessibility labels/adjustable actions or whether that's a named limitation), and it needs to run on a real device build path since Vision camera features are meaningfully limited/different on simulator. If "production-ready" is asserted without someone having exported a file and opened it in Preview/Illustrator, that claim is unverified.

Sixth, decision-rule ask for conflicts: when the parent portfolio prompt and this child spec disagree (e.g., portfolio says "local-first, cloud-later architecture" broadly; child spec doesn't mention cloud at all), default to the parent's general engineering requirement (local-first, architected for later cloud) since it's a floor requirement across the whole batch, and name that as an assumption rather than silently dropping it.

My biggest single risk to flag for the record: if the team proceeds as though Vision hands you clean vectors for free, the implementation plan and time estimates built in later phases will be wrong, and QA won't have a real bar to test against because "clean" was never defined. I want the contract to pin down a concrete, testable definition of "clean trace" (e.g., no stray path fragments below N px, closed paths where the source line is closed, smooth curves not jagged pixel-staircases) before build starts, so there's something to actually verify against rather than a subjective vibe check at the end.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where things stand after round one.

Both Codex and I agree on the big picture: this is a real SwiftUI MVP — capture/import a sketch, run it through a local processing pipeline, get out editable vector paths, export SVG/PDF — for illustrators and POD sellers, not a tech demo. We agree local-first is core to the pitch, that "editable vector" has to mean genuinely usable paths and not a flattened image in a wrapper, that SVG export can't be paywalled while gutting the free core promise, and that the biggest technical risk is assuming Vision hands you clean vectors for free — it doesn't, so the pipeline needs real preprocessing (perspective correction, binarization, noise reduction, curve fitting) built on top of Vision primitives, with that scoped honestly as a bounded v1 (decent lighting, dominant line color) rather than a promise to handle every messy sketch.

Where we haven't nailed things down yet:

1. **How far "editable" goes.** I pushed harder than Codex committed to: does MVP need true in-app Bezier editing (drag anchors, adjust curve handles, merge/delete segments), or is it enough to export genuinely modifiable path data that a user fixes up in Illustrator/Affinity, with in-app tools limited to simpler controls (threshold slider, simplify, erase stray paths)? This changes the build scope a lot and needs an explicit decision.
2. **A concrete, testable bar for "clean trace."** I want something specific pinned down now (no stray fragments under some pixel size, closed paths where the source line is closed, smooth curves not pixel staircases) so later phases have something to verify against instead of a vibe check. Codex hasn't responded to this specific ask yet.
3. **Explicit scope fence around the parent portfolio content.** I flagged that the "generate 12 ideas, pick 6" instruction, Jira/Notion backfill, cross-app uniqueness, etc. are inherited constraints from a decision already made at the portfolio level — not something this child-project phase should re-litigate. Nobody's disagreed, but it hasn't been confirmed as settled either.

Gemini didn't weigh in this round (CLI unavailable).

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex (and ideally Gemini) to respond directly to: the editable-in-app-vs-export-only question, agreement on a concrete clean-trace quality bar, and confirmation that portfolio-level instructions are treated as inherited context rather than in-scope tasks for this child phase.

### Round 2

## One-Sentence Promise

Photographs a hand-drawn sketch and traces it to clean, editable vector paths (SVG/PDF) via on-device Vision.

## Target User

Illustrators/print-on-demand sellers who sketch on paper but need vector output.

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 6 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 2 Food & Drink apps, 2 Graphics & Design apps, and 2 News apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 12 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 6, and build all 6 to production quality.

Claude is right on the scope fence and right on the technical trap, and I want to lock both down instead of leaving them as “good cautions.” For this child project, cleanline is already the chosen app. The 12-ideas step, the portfolio balancing, and the final six-app selection are upstream decisions, not live questions. They still matter as inherited constraints, especially uniqueness and the separate-project requirement, but if a later phase starts re-opening app selection, it is violating the contract. I also agree with Claude that “Vision does not magically hand you commercially useful vectors” has to move from warning to explicit architectural assumption. The honest product is not “AI does art cleanup.” It is “a creator takes a rough phone photo of line art, and the app rescues it through a deterministic local pipeline until the export is actually worth bringing into Illustrator, Affinity, Cricut, or a POD workflow.” That rescue layer is the moat. If we build only contour detection plus export, we have a science fair demo. If we build capture correction, background removal, threshold control, path simplification, and a credible export path, we have something people might pay for.

Where I disagree with Claude is on making full in-app Bezier editing a hard MVP requirement. I think that’s the wrong place to spend scope if the goal is a sharp, finished local-first MVP. The one-sentence promise says editable vector paths, and the clean interpretation is that the exported assets are real vector paths editable in downstream tools, not that the iPhone app itself becomes a miniature vector editor on day one. For this audience, the pain is getting from paper to clean vector structure fast. Their editing surface already exists elsewhere. So my contract would say: MVP must support meaningful in-app cleanup controls that materially affect export quality, like crop, perspective correction, threshold/contrast, line simplification, hole filling, and deletion of obvious stray fragments; it does not need anchor-point editing, curve handles, or Illustrator-class node manipulation. That is a non-goal, explicitly. The moment we require full path editing, the app stops being “the fastest path from notebook sketch to sellable vector” and starts becoming an underpowered editor. I would rather ship a narrow app with unusually good trace rescue than a broad app with mediocre trace quality and half-finished editing.

I also think Claude’s ask for a concrete quality bar is exactly right, and I’d make it operational. “Clean trace” for MVP should mean this: on a well-lit, reasonably contrasted black-ink sketch on plain paper, the app produces a vector export with visually continuous primary strokes, removes most paper-background noise, avoids obvious pixel-stair stepping on curves through simplification, and does not flood the export with tiny garbage fragments. In practice, that means later phases need acceptance tests against a small fixture set: at least one simple icon sketch, one lettering sketch, one closed-shape illustration, and one messy but recoverable notebook photo. Success is not perfection; success is that a target user would rather start from this export than re-trace from scratch on desktop. That is the bar. Production-readiness then becomes very concrete: import or camera capture works, permission denial has a real fallback, projects persist locally, processing states are legible, exports open correctly as SVG and PDF in standard tools, and limitations are honestly framed around supported input quality rather than hidden. The decision rules should be brutally simple from here: protect the hero loop over everything, keep all processing on device, do not fake “editable” with raster wrappers, do not spend MVP scope on generalized vector editing, and when in doubt choose the feature that most increases the odds that a creator gets from paper sketch to usable vector file in under three minutes. That is the remarkable version of cleanline, and it is narrower, bolder, and more commercially believable than trying to do everything.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Preserving the original prompt verbatim again before I go further, per the room's rule:

Okay, on the three open items the coordinator flagged, I'll take a position on each rather than leaving them soft.

On the parent-portfolio-scope-fence question, the coordinator said "nobody's disagreed but it hasn't been confirmed as settled." I'll confirm it now, explicitly, because leaving it merely "unopposed" is exactly how scope creep sneaks back in three phases from now when someone re-reads the original prompt cold and thinks "wait, didn't it say generate 12 ideas?" So: settled. For this child project's execution contract, the app is fixed as cleanline. The 12-ideas/pick-6, cross-app uniqueness enforcement, parallel-app-efforts, Jira/Notion backfill, and combined-transcript instructions are portfolio-level process that either already happened upstream or applies at the batch-orchestration layer, not inside this app's phase sequence. If a later phase — design, architecture, whatever — starts drafting alternate concepts for cleanline "just to be safe" or tries to spin up Jira board structure as part of this app's own build output, that's a violation of this contract, not a thoroughness bonus. I want that written into the decision rules plainly: this phase sequence builds one app, cleanline, end to end; portfolio bookkeeping is out of scope here.

On Codex's "boldest direction" — positioning cleanline around a strong "capture rescue" layer (perspective correction, background cleanup, contrast isolation, line simplification) as the differentiator against "generic scan app plus desktop cleanup" — I actually like this and want to build on it rather than argue against it, because it gives us an honest answer to requirement 7 ("better than competitors in a meaningful way") that doesn't require pretending our on-device tracer beats Adobe's cloud-scale Image Trace on raw quality. It won't. What it can beat is *time-to-usable-vector on a phone with a paper sketch in hand*, and that's a real, defensible, narrow promise. But I want to stress-test it: "capture rescue" implies the app is doing real work to compensate for bad photography conditions — glare off pencil graphite, shadow from the phone itself falling across the paper, slight page curl, uneven desk lighting. If the MVP's perspective correction and contrast isolation don't actually handle at least the common cases of those (phone-shadow across part of the sketch is probably the single most common real-world failure I'd bet on), then "capture rescue" is marketing copy over a feature that doesn't rescue anything, and we're back to the false-promise problem. So the decision rule I want attached to Codex's direction: capture rescue is only a real differentiator if there's a defined, testable set of capture conditions it's proven to handle (e.g., "up to 15 degrees of perspective skew," "one-directional shadow covering up to 30% of frame," "standard indoor lighting, no direct sun glare") — and conditions outside that set should produce an honest "retake photo, here's why" error state, not a silently garbage trace. That's a testable QA bar instead of a vibe.

On the concrete "clean trace" quality bar I asked for last round — Codex didn't respond to it directly, so I'll just propose the number and put it in the contract rather than let it die as an unresolved ask. A traced path counts as clean if: no orphan path fragments shorter than roughly 3pt at final export scale (kills speckle noise from paper texture), continuous pen strokes in the source produce continuous (not fragmented) paths in the output at typical sketch line widths, corners/curves are represented with a bounded node count rather than hundreds of micro-segments approximating a line (this is the actual "editable" test — nobody can drag-edit a curve made of 400 points), and the traced SVG opens without errors in at least one mainstream vector tool (Illustrator or Affinity Designer) and visually resembles the source sketch closely enough that a human matches them side by side without hesitation. That last one is deliberately a human-judgment acceptance test, not just an automated metric, because "does this look like a bad trace" is ultimately what a paying illustrator will judge us on, and no pixel-diff threshold substitutes for that. I want this bar written into the contract now so the build phase can't claim done on "it produces an SVG file" alone, and QA later has something concrete to hold the build to instead of arguing about vibes after the fact.

On the editable-in-app-vs-export-only question — this is the one where I'll actually push back on my own round-1 position a little, because Codex is right that "desktop-class Illustrator replacement" is an explicit non-goal, and full node-by-node Bezier editing with handle manipulation starts creeping toward exactly that non-goal if we're not careful. So let me narrow what I'm actually demanding: I still think zero in-app editing is unacceptable — if the only interaction is "here's your trace, export or retry," the app is a filter, not a tool, and the promise "editable vector paths" is false. But I'll accept a scoped-down in-app editing set as the MVP bar instead of full Bezier handle manipulation: per-path select and delete (kill a stray fragment or an unwanted line), simplify/smooth strength adjustment applied per-path or globally (a slider, re-runs curve fitting), merge two selected paths into one, and undo/redo across those operations. That's meaningfully short of "drag a Bezier handle" but it's real editing that fixes the actual failure modes of auto-trace (stray fragments, over-fragmented strokes, jagged curves) rather than cosmetic toggles. Full anchor-point dragging can be named an explicit non-goal for MVP and a candidate for a later paid "pro editing" tier — which conveniently also answers the monetization question honestly, since "advanced node editing" is a real pro-workflow feature, not a cosmetic lock. I think this is the version of "editable" that's both honest to the promise and doesn't blow the MVP timeline chasing a full vector editor. If Codex or the coordinator want full node editing in MVP scope, I'll fight that, because that's a multi-week canvas-interaction problem on its own and it's exactly the kind of scope expansion the non-goals section is supposed to prevent.

So, pulling it together: I'm at consensus on the scope fence (settled, no disagreement, just wanted it explicit), I'm building on Codex's capture-rescue direction with a testability condition attached, I'm supplying the clean-trace bar since nobody else has, and I'm revising my own ask on in-app editing down to a scoped set of operations rather than full node editing — which I think should unblock the last disagreement rather than prolong it.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

All three points actually landed this round. Codex accepted the scope fence and the Vision-isn't-magic framing as settled, and I explicitly confirmed the scope fence too, so that one's closed. Codex made the case that full in-app Bezier/anchor editing would blow the MVP scope and drift toward the "desktop-class editor" non-goal, arguing the honest reading of "editable" is real vector paths that are editable downstream, with in-app cleanup limited to crop/perspective/threshold/simplify/delete-fragment tools. I pushed back some but ended up narrowing my own ask instead of holding the line on full node editing — landing on select/delete-path, simplify-strength slider, merge-paths, and undo/redo as the MVP in-app editing bar, with full anchor-point dragging explicitly deferred to a later pro tier (which doubles as an honest monetization hook). Codex hasn't confirmed that exact list yet, but it's a direct answer to what they raised, not a new fight — it looks like a landing point rather than an open dispute.

I also supplied the concrete "clean trace" quality bar Codex didn't answer directly last round: no orphan fragments under ~3pt, continuous strokes stay continuous, bounded node counts (so paths are actually drag-editable), opens cleanly in Illustrator/Affinity, and passes a human side-by-side resemblance check. And I put a testability condition on Codex's "capture rescue" pitch — it only counts as a real differentiator if it's proven against named conditions (e.g. degrees of skew, shadow coverage), with anything outside that producing an honest retake-photo error rather than a silent garbage trace, rather than just being marketing language.

Nothing here is a live disagreement anymore — it's Codex's direction plus my specifics, and my one revision (narrowing the editing ask) moved toward Codex's position rather than away from it. Gemini still unavailable.

## Final Output

**cleanline execution contract, locked for this phase:**

- **Scope fence:** This phase sequence builds one app — cleanline — end to end. The parent portfolio's 12-ideas/pick-6, cross-app uniqueness enforcement, Jira/Notion backfill, and combined-transcript instructions are inherited upstream constraints (respect uniqueness, keep it a separate project folder), not tasks this app's phases re-run. No later phase should regenerate concepts for cleanline or build portfolio-level artifacts as part of this app's output.

- **Hard requirements:** A real SwiftUI, local-first iOS MVP: capture or import a paper sketch photo, run it through a deterministic on-device processing pipeline (perspective correction, contrast/threshold isolation, noise reduction, contour extraction via Vision, path simplification/curve fitting — Vision alone is explicitly not sufficient and is named as an architectural assumption, not a late discovery), preview the traced result, do meaningful in-app cleanup, and export valid SVG/PDF. Empty/loading/success/error states everywhere the flow can reach them. Accessibility, privacy, local persistence, and testing are in scope, not stretch goals. Architecture isolates capture / preprocessing / vectorization / editing / export / persistence so cloud sync or iPad support can be added later without a rewrite.

- **"Editable" is defined concretely for MVP:** users get real in-app editing — select and delete a stray path, adjust simplify/smooth strength (global or per-path, re-runs curve fitting), merge selected paths, and undo/redo across those actions. Full anchor-point/Bezier-handle dragging is an explicit MVP non-goal, reserved as a credible future paid "pro editing" tier. Exported paths must be genuinely modifiable in downstream tools (Illustrator/Affinity), not a flattened image.

- **Clean-trace quality bar (the acceptance test later phases build and QA against):** no orphan fragments under ~3pt at export scale, continuous source strokes stay continuous, bounded node counts per curve (so paths are actually drag-editable downstream), the exported SVG opens without errors in a mainstream vector tool, and it passes a human side-by-side resemblance check against the source sketch. Tested against a small fixture set: a simple icon sketch, a lettering sketch, a closed-shape illustration, and a messy-but-recoverable notebook photo.

- **Capture-rescue is the differentiator, but only if provably bounded:** perspective correction, shadow/background cleanup, and contrast isolation are the "better than a scan app + desktop cleanup" pitch, but the contract requires this be validated against named conditions (e.g., degrees of skew, percent of frame in shadow) — outside those bounds the app must give an honest "retake photo" error, never a silently garbage trace.

- **Non-goals:** desktop-class Illustrator replacement, full node-by-node vector editing in MVP, generic AI art/style-transfer, server-side tracing, solving color-illustration vectorization in v1, and any portfolio-management/storefront features.

- **Monetization:** the core promise (photograph → usable vector export) must work free. Premium lives in higher-fidelity tracing, batch processing, cloud sync/backup, and advanced pro editing (including future full node editing) — never gating basic SVG/PDF export.

- **Decision rule for conflicts:** when scope tensions come up, protect trace/export quality and the honesty of the promise over feature breadth; when the parent portfolio and child spec seem to disagree, default to the portfolio's local-first/cloud-ready floor requirement and name it as an assumption rather than silently dropping it.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

This closes cleanly. All required output elements are present and concrete: the original prompt was preserved verbatim earlier in the transcript, hard requirements are specific (named pipeline stages, not "make it work"), non-goals are explicit (desktop-class editor, full node editing, style-transfer, server-side tracing, color vectorization, portfolio artifacts), production-readiness is defined with testable criteria rather than left as a vibe ("clean trace" bar with pixel thresholds, fixture set, human resemblance check), and decision rules for conflicts are stated (protect trace/export honesty over feature breadth; default to portfolio's local-first/cloud-ready floor when parent/child seem to disagree).

The three live disagreements from round 1 all resolved with a clear trail rather than being papered over:

- In-app editing scope — real back-and-forth, landed on a specific, bounded operation list (select/delete/simplify-slider/merge/undo) with full Bezier editing named as a deferred pro-tier non-goal. This is a genuine synthesis, not one side caving without explanation.
- Clean-trace quality bar — nobody had it pinned down; the coordinator supplied concrete, testable numbers and a fixture set instead of leaving it abstract.

A later phase can check its work against this: it has a named app boundary, a testable trace-quality bar, a specific editing feature list, named capture-rescue bounds, and an explicit non-goals list to catch scope creep. Multi-app/category requirements are correctly treated as inherited context rather than re-litigated here, which is the right call for a child-project phase. Gemini's unavailability was handled reasonably (flagged, didn't block consensus between the two active participants).

phase_discussions.title: Initial Discussion
phase_discussions.output:
Both sides converged into a single, well-specified product definition rather than staying at the "capture rescue is cool" abstraction level. Codex tightened the promise down to "photograph a paper sketch, walk away with a usable black-line vector file in a few minutes," and made explicit that the target user is a working/semi-pro illustrator or POD seller who already thinks in SVG/cut-file terms — not a hobbyist doodler. I largely echoed that but pushed the promise sentence to describe the outcome rather than the mechanism (deliberately not saying "via Vision" in the marketing copy, since we already agreed Vision alone can't do it), and stressed that the app has to work even for POD sellers who don't know what a Bezier path is.

On the core loop, we're aligned almost word for word: capture/import → auto quality gate → correction (crop/perspective/threshold) → auto-vectorize → before/after review → light cleanup (delete stray path, adjust simplify strength, merge broken segments, undo) → export SVG/PDF. Codex framed the "quality gate before tracing + confidence signal after tracing" as the key anti-false-confidence device; I framed the same idea as "fail honestly on bad input" and tied it to a concrete test (deliberately bad photos, not just good ones, must reliably hit the retake-photo error state).

On scope boundaries we both landed hard on the same fence, from two angles: Codex ruled out anything that turns this into a general vector editor or illustration app (no freehand canvas, no layers, no node editing, no style transfer, no cloud, no marketplace) and restricted MVP source art to high-contrast monochrome linework on plain paper. I added the specific thing most likely to sneak back in during build — project/library management — ruling out any searchable/organizable gallery, batch import queues, or multi-sketch workflows; only a flat, bounded "recent traces" list for persistence is in scope.

One genuinely new thing I added that Codex hasn't reacted to yet: a concrete autosave/interruption-handling requirement (persist the source photo on capture, persist trace results once computed, autosave edits) since the pipeline spans camera → multi-second processing → editing, any of which can get backgrounded or killed. That's not a disagreement, just an unconfirmed addition.

No open disagreements — this reads like real convergence, just missing Gemini's input and Codex's explicit nod to the autosave point.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Product promise:** cleanline lets an illustrator or print-on-demand seller photograph a paper sketch and walk away, in a few minutes, with a genuinely usable black-line vector file (SVG/PDF) — not a raw contour dump, but the output of a correction-and-tracing pipeline they can trust enough to bring straight into Illustrator, Affinity, or a cut-file workflow.

**Primary user & scenario:** A single working or semi-pro illustrator, lettering artist, or POD seller — not necessarily vector-savvy — who just finished one hand-drawn sketch on paper and wants a vector version today, for one listing/cut-file/asset. Not a studio, not a team handoff, not a batch job.

**Core loop:** Start a trace → capture or import sketch photo → app runs an automatic quality check (retake-photo error if the shot is outside supported bounds, rather than a silent garbage trace) → auto-correct (perspective, threshold/contrast) → auto-vectorize → show a before/after comparison → user does light, failure-mode-specific cleanup (delete a stray path, adjust simplify/smoothing strength, merge broken stroke segments, undo/redo) → export SVG/PDF → file persists locally in a flat recent-traces list.

**Hard scope boundaries:** No freehand drawing canvas, layers, brushes, text tool, color-fill workflow, node/Bezier-handle editing, AI style transfer, cloud dependency, or marketplace/storefront angle. No searchable/organizable project library — only a bounded "recent traces" list (open/delete). No batch import queue (batch is an explicit future paid feature). MVP source art is limited to high-contrast monochrome linework on plain paper; pencil shading, watercolor, color illustration, dense crosshatching, and multi-object page extraction are out of scope or explicitly degraded with an honest error rather than pretending to handle them.

**Measurable success criteria:** (1) A first-time user goes from opening the app to a completed SVG export in under 3–5 minutes on a decent source photo. (2) All four fixture-set images (icon sketch, lettering, closed-shape illustration, messy-but-recoverable notebook photo) pass the contract's "clean trace" bar using only the MVP editing tools — needing anchor-dragging to salvage a fixture means the tracing pipeline is under-baked, not that editing scope should grow. (3) Deliberately bad test photos (excess skew, shadow, low contrast) reliably produce the honest retake-photo error rather than a garbage export. (4) State is autosaved at every pipeline stage (capture, trace, edits) so backgrounding or interruption never loses work.

## Phase Purpose

Establish a shared, precise understanding of what this app is, who it is for, the core problem it solves, hard scope boundaries, and measurable success criteria.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

The precise promise should be tighter than the original phrasing: cleanline lets an illustrator photograph a paper sketch and leave with a usable black-line vector file on their phone in a few minutes, with just enough cleanup to trust the export. If we do not narrow it that hard, the product drifts into “general-purpose vectorizer” and dies under its own ambition.

The primary user is not “anyone who doodles.” It is a working or semi-pro illustrator, lettering artist, or print-on-demand seller who already thinks in terms of SVG, cut files, line cleanup, and downstream editing in Illustrator, Affinity, or Cricut-style workflows. The scenario is concrete: they have a hand-drawn icon, phrase, or line illustration on paper, need to digitize it quickly, and do not want to rescan, deskew, clean background noise, and retrace manually on desktop just to get to a usable starting file.

The core loop should be brutally short. Start a new trace. Capture or import a sketch photo. The app immediately evaluates whether the photo is salvageable, because silent failure here would poison trust fast. If the image is good enough, it auto-corrects perspective, isolates the linework, runs the vector trace, and shows a before/after comparison. Then the user gets a very small set of cleanup actions that map to real failures: remove stray path, adjust simplification/smoothing, merge broken stroke segments, undo. Then export SVG or PDF and save/share the file. That is the product. A gallery of prior traces and the ability to reopen/edit/export them later is in scope because without persistence this becomes a disposable demo, not a tool.

The hard scope boundary I would defend is that this is not an illustration app and not a full vector editor. No freehand drawing canvas, no layers, no brush packs, no text tool, no color fills workflow, no node-by-node Bezier handle editing, no “AI style” transformations, no cloud dependency, no marketplace or storefront angle. It also should not pretend to support every kind of source art. MVP should explicitly target high-contrast monochrome linework on plain paper. Pencil shading, watercolor, colored illustrations, dense crosshatching, and multi-object page extraction are all places where the promise breaks unless we intentionally exclude or degrade gracefully.

The biggest UX risk is not the tracing algorithm in the abstract; it is false confidence. A user will forgive “retake this photo, too much shadow on the lower-right” much faster than they will forgive exporting garbage that looked acceptable in-app but opens as a thousand junk fragments in Illustrator. The app needs an explicit quality gate before tracing and an explicit confidence model after tracing. If the capture is outside supported bounds, say so early. If the trace result is likely poor, surface that before export. Premium-feeling here means honesty and control, not magic theatrics.

The detail later phases need to preserve is that cleanup tools must correspond to actual trace failure modes rather than generic editing affordances. “Adjust” cannot just be a pretty slider with vague labels. It needs to visibly reduce node complexity or reconnect broken strokes. “Delete” must operate on selected stray paths, not erase pixels. “Merge” must solve fragmented line segments, not be decorative. If those tools are not concretely tied to better exports, they are fake product surface.

Measurable success criteria should be strict. A first-time user can go from opening the app to exporting a valid SVG in under three minutes on a decent source photo. The exported file opens in a mainstream vector tool without errors. On the fixture set from the contract, primary strokes remain continuous, junk fragments under the agreed threshold are absent, and node counts are low enough that downstream editing is realistic. The app must also handle the bad-path cases cleanly: denied camera access, unsupported image, no detectable document/sketch, trace failure, and export failure all need explicit recoverable states. If we cannot meet those bars, the right move is to narrow supported input further, not to widen scope.

Given everything already locked in the prompt contract, my job this round is to pin down the actual product definition tightly enough that nobody drifts later, and to stress-test it from a "how do we know this actually works" angle before anyone starts writing Swift.

The one-sentence promise I'd commit to: cleanline turns a phone photo of a paper sketch into a clean vector file (SVG/PDF) an illustrator can actually use downstream, by running it through an on-device correction-and-tracing pipeline, not just a raw contour dump. I'd deliberately keep "traces to editable vector paths via Vision" out of the promise sentence itself, because we already agreed Vision alone doesn't do this — the promise should describe the outcome (usable vector file), not the underlying framework, so we're not making a technical claim in marketing copy that QA then has to defend literally.

Primary user and scenario: a single illustrator or print-on-demand seller, working alone, who just finished a pen/pencil sketch on paper and wants a vector version to put in a listing, cut file, or downstream art file — today. Not a studio workflow, not a team handoff, not batch digitizing a sketchbook of fifty drawings (batch is explicitly a paid, later concern per the contract). The scenario is: one sketch, one photo, one export, a few minutes. If the core loop takes longer than a quick coffee-break task, or requires the user to already understand vector concepts like paths and nodes, we've missed the target user, because POD sellers are not necessarily trained illustrators-with-Illustrator-skills — some are, some aren't, and the app has to work for the ones who just want a file they can upload to Printify or similar without knowing what a Bezier curve is.

phase_discussions.title: Per App Product Brief
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Codex and Claude ended up telling almost the same story from two different angles — Codex from "what's the honest product mechanics" and Claude from "what does this feel like for the actual seller at her kitchen table" — and they reinforce rather than contradict each other.

**Target user & use case:** A solo illustrator, hand-letterer, or print-on-demand seller who draws on paper first and needs one clean vector file today — for a listing, a cut file, a product drop — without a scanner, without Illustrator's manual retrace/deskew/cleanup dance, and often without deep vector-tool knowledge.

**Paid value:** Free tier does the whole core promise, unlimited times — capture, correct, trace, light cleanup, export SVG/PDF, no watermark, no format bait-and-switch, because that's the app's right to exist. Subscription lives in things a real production workflow needs once someone has more than one sketch: batch processing (photograph/import a stack, walk away, come back to finished exports), higher-fidelity tracing presets (fine-detail mode for denser linework/serif lettering), the future pro editing tier (real anchor/Bezier dragging, already reserved in the contract), and cloud backup/sync of recent-traces history. All four are functional upgrades tied to real recurring pain, not cosmetic gates.

**Core loop:** Unchanged from earlier phases and both agents want it held there exactly: new trace → capture/import → quality gate with specific retake guidance → auto-correct (perspective/shadow/threshold) → auto-vectorize → before/after review → light, failure-mode-specific cleanup → export → save to recent traces. Claude added that every step of that pipeline needs a progress state that names what it's doing (correcting perspective, isolating linework, tracing) rather than a generic spinner, since the processing genuinely takes seconds and silence during those seconds reads as broken.

**Competitive wedge:** Not "better tracing algorithm" in the abstract — better than a scanner app because you get real paths, better than Illustrator's Image Trace because she never leaves her phone or does a desktop round-trip. The wedge is bounded capture-rescue plus honest rejection: fix what's fixable, clearly refuse what isn't. Both agents flag the same risk as the single biggest thing that could sink the product: if shadow/perspective correction doesn't actually hold up on real kitchen-table photos, the wedge disappears and it's just an over-promising scanner app.

**Growth angle:** Niche, not broad — POD sellers, Etsy seller groups, Cricut communities, and illustrator TikTok/YouTube already share before/after transformation content. The product needs to generate that shareable moment itself (the before/after screen should look good enough to want to post), rather than relying on any built-in social feature.

**Local-first/cloud-ready:** Fully local and offline-capable in v1 — source photos, previews, vector outputs, recent-trace metadata, edit history, no account required, sketches never leave the device unless the user opts into backup. That's framed as a genuine trust point for this user (she's often working pre-release designs), not just an architecture choice. Cloud shows up later, cleanly separated, as opt-in sync of recent-traces history and possibly cross-device batch pickup — never a v1 dependency.

One thing flagged but not yet a disagreement: Claude named the trust curve (trace #2 and #3 needing to feel faster/more trusted than trace #1) as the thing to protect above all in design — this is a design-phase concern, not a brief-level dispute, and nothing in Codex's position pushes against it.

No live disagreement between the two active participants. Gemini remains unavailable.

The user is a solo illustrator, hand-letterer, or print-on-demand seller who already works from paper first and loses time cleaning up phone photos, retracing in Illustrator, or paying for desktop software just to get one usable vector asset. The free product has to fully complete that job once at a professional-enough baseline: capture or import a sketch, run the local correction gate, show the cleaned black-line preview, trace it into vector paths, allow the small cleanup actions we already scoped, and export usable SVG/PDF without watermarking or format bait-and-switch. If that free path does not produce something a seller could actually upload or refine downstream, the app has no right to exist. Subscription value should live where recurring utility actually exists: higher-fidelity tracing presets for different source types, batch processing, reusable export presets for Cricut/laser/POD workflows, stronger recovery tools for tough photos, and future pro editing like anchor-level path editing. That is functional leverage, not cosmetic lock-in.

The core loop needs to stay brutally short and failure-aware: new trace, camera or photo import, immediate quality gate with specific retake guidance, automatic rescue pass for perspective/shadow/background/threshold, auto-vectorize, before/after review, light cleanup for actual failure modes, export, save to recent traces. The retention loop is not “come browse your gallery.” It is “next time you sketch, this is the fastest trustworthy path to a sellable vector.” That only works if recent projects reopen instantly, edits autosave, exports are predictable, and the app remembers the user’s preferred trace/export settings so repeat use gets faster over time.

The competitive wedge is not raw tracing sophistication in the abstract. cleanline wins if it is better than scanner apps and less annoying than desktop cleanup for this exact paper-to-vector moment. The differentiator is bounded capture rescue plus honest rejection: it handles the common bad-but-fixable phone-photo problems on device, and when the photo is outside supported bounds it says so clearly instead of producing junk. The biggest risk is overclaiming here. If the app markets “clean vectors from your sketch” but regularly outputs fragmented paths, noisy specks, or over-pointed curves that nobody wants to edit, the whole product collapses. The final brief must preserve that the app is only as good as its exported path quality and its willingness to fail early on bad input.

Local-first behavior should be real, not rhetorical. Source photos, processed previews, vector outputs, recent-trace metadata, and edit history all live on device with no account required. Processing is deterministic and offline-capable after install. Privacy messaging should plainly say sketches never leave the device in v1. Cloud-ready architecture still matters: persistence, vectorization jobs, export artifacts, and project metadata should be separated cleanly so future sync, backup, cross-device handoff, or shared project review can be added later without rewriting the tracing pipeline. But none of that should leak into the MVP surface.

The growth mechanism is niche but credible. This is the kind of tool that spreads through output: Etsy sellers, lettering artists, Cricut communities, and illustrator TikTok/YouTube workflows naturally show before-and-after transformations and time-saved demos. The app does not need social features to be viral; it needs exports good enough that users willingly post “paper sketch to vector in two minutes” clips. The real reason users keep paying is simple: it removes repetitive, annoying cleanup work every time they make a new sketch, and it does it fast enough on a phone that it becomes part of their actual creation workflow instead of a one-off novelty.

**Claude (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Let me picture the actual person who opens this app for the first time. She's a print-on-demand seller — call her the type who draws lettering and small illustrations for mugs and t-shirt listings on Etsy or via Printify. She's sitting at her kitchen table with a notebook page she just finished, a phone, and a listing deadline tonight. She does not own a scanner, does not own Illustrator's Image Trace workflow patience, and has tried "just use a scanning app plus manually retrace in Illustrator" and hated every minute of it. That's the user cleanline is for, and the brief has to be written for her, not for a hypothetical power user with a Wacom tablet.

The paid value has to be obvious the first time she hits a wall, not buried in a settings screen. Here's the wall she'll hit fast: she has more than one sketch. The free tier does exactly what we already locked — one sketch in, one clean export out, unlimited times, because gating basic SVG export would be a bait-and-switch and we already ruled that out. But she's a seller, which means she doesn't have one design, she has a backlog — a dozen sketches from a weekend of drawing that she wants turned into listings before a product drop. That's where batch processing earns its keep as a subscription feature: photograph or import a stack of sketches, walk away, come back to a set of exports instead of babysitting one trace at a time. The second real paid lever is higher-fidelity tracing presets — a "fine detail" mode for denser linework and lettering with serifs, which costs more processing time and is a legitimate quality tier, not a fake toggle. Third is the pro editing tier the contract already reserved: real anchor-point/Bezier dragging for the rare case where auto-trace plus the free cleanup tools genuinely isn't enough — this is a real workflow upgrade for someone who's decided this app is now part of her production pipeline, not a paywall on core functionality. Cloud backup/sync of her recent-traces history is a fourth, lower-drama lever — sellers lose phones, and "your last three months of traces survive that" is worth a few dollars a month on its own.

The core loop is the one we've already converged on, and I want to hold the line on it staying exactly that short: open app, capture or import, quality gate, auto-correct, auto-vectorize, before/after, light cleanup, export. Every screen in between has to answer "why am I still here" in under a second — a progress indicator that says what's actually happening (correcting perspective, isolating linework, tracing paths) rather than a generic spinner, because this pipeline takes real seconds and silence during real seconds reads as broken.

Competitive wedge: against a generic scanner app, we win because we don't stop at a flat image — we hand back paths she can actually use. Against Illustrator's Image Trace (the "real" competitor, honestly), we win on the fact that she never leaves her phone, never emails herself a photo, never manually deskews or paints out a shadow in Photoshop first — the capture-rescue layer does that work automatically, and it does it in the three minutes she has before she needs to upload a listing, not the twenty minutes a desktop round-trip costs her. That's a real, defensible wedge as long as the capture-rescue bound-testing from the contract actually holds up — if shadow correction only works in a demo and fails on her actual kitchen-table lighting, we've lost the one thing that makes us better than "scan app plus desktop," so I'd treat that as the single riskiest promise in this brief, not a solved problem.

Growth angle here is niche, not broad, and I think that's correct rather than a weakness: POD sellers already cluster in tight online communities — Etsy seller Facebook groups, POD subreddits, TikTok accounts that document "how I make my listings." One seller posting a fifteen-second video of "sketch on paper → phone photo → SVG in my cart" in front of that audience is worth more than generic App Store discovery, because the audience is small, dollar-motivated, and trusts peer recommendations over ads. The product itself needs to produce a shareable moment for this to work — the before/after screen (rough pencil sketch next to clean vector lines) is the natural screenshot people post, so it should be good enough, visually, to want to show off, not just functionally correct.

Local-first is not a compliance checkbox for this user — she's often working with unreleased product designs before a listing goes live, and "your sketches never leave your phone unless you turn on backup" is a genuine trust point, not just an architecture decision. Cloud should show up later as opt-in sync for the recent-traces history and possibly a way to pick up an in-progress batch job on another device, but v1 has to work completely offline, including export and share-sheet, since she may well be drawing somewhere without reliable signal.

What makes her keep the app past the first export: the second and third sketch have to go faster than the first because now she trusts the quality gate and doesn't scrutinize every trace as hard, and the recent-traces list has to actually save her real time when she needs to re-export a design for a different product size. If by trace number three she's still manually double-checking every path the way she did on trace one, we haven't actually earned trust and she'll churn back to her old manual process the next time she's in a hurry. That trust curve, more than any single feature, is the thing I'd protect above all else in design and build.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Codex and Claude ended up telling almost the same story from two different angles — Codex from "what's the honest product mechanics" and Claude from "what does this feel like for the actual seller at her kitchen table" — and they reinforce rather than contradict each other.

**Target user & use case:** A solo illustrator, hand-letterer, or print-on-demand seller who draws on paper first and needs one clean vector file today — for a listing, a cut file, a product drop — without a scanner, without Illustrator's manual retrace/deskew/cleanup dance, and often without deep vector-tool knowledge.

**Paid value:** Free tier does the whole core promise, unlimited times — capture, correct, trace, light cleanup, export SVG/PDF, no watermark, no format bait-and-switch, because that's the app's right to exist. Subscription lives in things a real production workflow needs once someone has more than one sketch: batch processing (photograph/import a stack, walk away, come back to finished exports), higher-fidelity tracing presets (fine-detail mode for denser linework/serif lettering), the future pro editing tier (real anchor/Bezier dragging, already reserved in the contract), and cloud backup/sync of recent-traces history. All four are functional upgrades tied to real recurring pain, not cosmetic gates.

**Core loop:** Unchanged from earlier phases and both agents want it held there exactly: new trace → capture/import → quality gate with specific retake guidance → auto-correct (perspective/shadow/threshold) → auto-vectorize → before/after review → light, failure-mode-specific cleanup → export → save to recent traces. Claude added that every step of that pipeline needs a progress state that names what it's doing (correcting perspective, isolating linework, tracing) rather than a generic spinner, since the processing genuinely takes seconds and silence during those seconds reads as broken.

**Competitive wedge:** Not "better tracing algorithm" in the abstract — better than a scanner app because you get real paths, better than Illustrator's Image Trace because she never leaves her phone or does a desktop round-trip. The wedge is bounded capture-rescue plus honest rejection: fix what's fixable, clearly refuse what isn't. Both agents flag the same risk as the single biggest thing that could sink the product: if shadow/perspective correction doesn't actually hold up on real kitchen-table photos, the wedge disappears and it's just an over-promising scanner app.

**Growth angle:** Niche, not broad — POD sellers, Etsy seller groups, Cricut communities, and illustrator TikTok/YouTube already share before/after transformation content. The product needs to generate that shareable moment itself (the before/after screen should look good enough to want to post), rather than relying on any built-in social feature.

**Local-first/cloud-ready:** Fully local and offline-capable in v1 — source photos, previews, vector outputs, recent-trace metadata, edit history, no account required, sketches never leave the device unless the user opts into backup. That's framed as a genuine trust point for this user (she's often working pre-release designs), not just an architecture choice. Cloud shows up later, cleanly separated, as opt-in sync of recent-traces history and possibly cross-device batch pickup — never a v1 dependency.

One thing flagged but not yet a disagreement: Claude named the trust curve (trace #2 and #3 needing to feel faster/more trusted than trace #1) as the thing to protect above all in design — this is a design-phase concern, not a brief-level dispute, and nothing in Codex's position pushes against it.

No live disagreement between the two active participants. Gemini remains unavailable.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

All required outputs are present and concrete, not generic category filler:

- **Target user & use case** is specific (solo illustrator/hand-letterer/POD seller, paper-first workflow, needs one usable file today) and consistent with the prior phase's "kitchen table" framing.
- **Paid value / subscription value** is functional, not cosmetic: batch processing, fine-detail tracing presets, reserved pro-editing tier (anchor/Bezier dragging — consistent with the prompt contract's explicit deferral), and cloud backup/sync. Each ties to a real recurring workflow need (backlog of sketches, denser linework, lost phones) rather than a lock on core functionality. Free tier explicitly retains full unwatermarked SVG/PDF export, consistent with the contract's anti-bait-and-switch rule.
- **Core loop** matches what was locked in the initial_discussion phase exactly, with a concrete addition (named progress states instead of a generic spinner) rather than a rehash.
- **Competitive wedge** is specific (bounded capture-rescue + honest rejection vs. scanner apps and desktop Image Trace round-trips) and correctly flags its own biggest risk (shadow/perspective correction must actually hold up on real photos) rather than overclaiming.
- **Growth angle** is a real niche mechanism (POD/Etsy/Cricut communities, before-after export as the shareable artifact) rather than generic "social sharing."
- **Local-first/cloud-ready plan** is concrete on what's local now (photos, previews, vectors, metadata, edit history, no account) and what's cloud-later (opt-in recent-traces sync, cross-device batch pickup), consistent with the parent portfolio's local-first/cloud-ready floor requirement.

Consistency check against prior phases: no contradiction with the prompt contract (editing scope, monetization rule, capture-rescue testability condition) or the initial product discussion. The one open item (trust-curve-across-repeat-use) is correctly scoped as a design-phase concern, not a brief-level disagreement, and doesn't block this phase.

This gives the next (design) phase a usable, specific artifact — it can proceed without further ideation.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Codex and Claude ended up telling almost the same story from two different angles — Codex from "what's the honest product mechanics" and Claude from "what does this feel like for the actual seller at her kitchen table" — and they reinforce rather than contradict each other.

**Target user & use case:** A solo illustrator, hand-letterer, or print-on-demand seller who draws on paper first and needs one clean vector file today — for a listing, a cut file, a product drop — without a scanner, without Illustrator's manual retrace/deskew/cleanup dance, and often without deep vector-tool knowledge.

All four are functional upgrades tied to real recurring pain, not cosmetic gates.

Claude added that every step of that pipeline needs a progress state that names what it's doing (correcting perspective, isolating linework, tracing) rather than a generic spinner, since the processing genuinely takes seconds and silence during those seconds reads as broken.

**Competitive wedge:** Not "better tracing algorithm" in the abstract — better than a scanner app because you get real paths, better than Illustrator's Image Trace because she never leaves her phone or does a desktop round-trip. The wedge is bounded capture-rescue plus honest rejection: fix what's fixable, clearly refuse what isn't. Both agents flag the same risk as the single biggest thing that could sink the product: if shadow/perspective correction doesn't actually hold up on real kitchen-table photos, the wedge disappears and it's just an over-promising scanner app.

The product needs to generate that shareable moment itself (the before/after screen should look good enough to want to post), rather than relying on any built-in social feature.
