phase_discussions.title: Detailed Discussion
phase_discussions.output:
Good news — the two things I flagged as genuinely open both got resolved with real give-and-take, not just one side caving.

On monetization, Codex came around independently to exactly the position Claude was arguing: no live StoreKit in this MVP. Building a purchase flow for batch/fine-detail/pro-editing/cloud-sync when none of those exist yet would be the same overclaiming sin the app is designed to avoid in its own tracing pipeline. Landing spot: free loop ships complete and unlocked, settings has an honest non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" note, zero StoreKit code, zero disabled toggles in the main workflow.

On storage, this wasn't "pick a side" — it was a genuine synthesis. Claude proposed keeping the raw capture only transiently (through the correction/review step, so the "inspectable, reversible" requirement is real), then discarding it and keeping the corrected working image + vector data forever until manual delete. Codex arrived at essentially the same shape from the other direction — "persist a normalized working original, not the raw camera file" — with the added detail that orientation-correction, downsampling, and metadata-stripping should all happen at that same normalization step. Both agree: no silent eviction, manual delete only, and EXIF/location/timestamp get stripped at ingest rather than lingering in a retained asset (which matters once cloud sync shows up later).

Codex also explicitly signed off on the rest of Claude's round-1 batch — PHPicker over full library access, EXIF-orientation handling as product correctness not just plumbing, the VoiceOver list-fallback for path cleanup — and drew one useful new boundary: dark pencil is in-scope only when the quality gate judges contrast sufficient, blue ballpoint is explicitly excluded rather than left to silently underperform.

Claude added a few things this round Codex hasn't directly reacted to, but they're extensions of the same honesty principle both sides already hold, not new fights: splitting "contrast too low" into a separate "sketch too faint to trace — darken your lines or go over it in pen" reason (since that's a drawing problem, not a photo problem, and no retake fixes it); making the quality-gate rejection reasons a fixed named vocabulary rather than free-form strings, so repeat failures feel recognizable instead of random; and naming live in-camera framing guidance (à la VisionKit's document scanner) as a requirement direction for architecture to weigh, rather than defaulting by accident to post-capture-only rejection.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- MVP supports one artifact class: high-contrast monochrome linework (black ink, dark marker, dark pencil when contrast is sufficient) on plain paper, one sketch per job. Blue ballpoint, shaded graphite, watercolor, multicolor illustration, and multi-object pages are explicitly out of scope, not silently degraded.
- Capture/import via live camera or PHPickerViewController (not full photo-library access) — reduces permission friction and strengthens the local-first privacy claim.
- Every pipeline stage is inspectable: user can view original, corrected/normalized preview, and traced vector preview, with undo/redo across edits.
- Quality-gate rejections use a fixed, named vocabulary (e.g., skew-too-high, shadow-coverage-too-high, blur-too-high, contrast-too-low, sketch-too-faint, no-sketch-region-detected, ruled-paper-interference), each with specific, consistent copy — not free-formed per attempt — so repeat failures are recognizable rather than random.
- "Sketch too faint to trace" is a distinct rejection reason from generic low-contrast/lighting problems, since faint pencil pressure is a drawing problem no retake fixes.
- Architecture should treat live, in-camera framing guidance (real-time edge/skew feedback before the shot is taken, in the spirit of VisionKit's document scanner) as a requirement direction to evaluate, not default to post-capture-only rejection — the implementation choice (custom vs. VisionKit-based) is deferred to the architecture phase.
- No live StoreKit subscription in this MVP. The complete free loop (capture, correct, trace, cleanup, export) ships fully unlocked. A non-purchasing, honestly-worded "Pro — coming soon" entry in settings describes planned batch/fine-detail/pro-editing/cloud-sync features. No paywall code, no disabled premium toggles in the main workflow.
- Storage/retention: the raw full-resolution capture is transient, kept only through the correction/review step. Once the user proceeds past review, the app discards the raw capture and persists a normalized working image (orientation-corrected, downsampled, EXIF/location/timestamp stripped at this point) plus the vector result — retained indefinitely in the recent-traces list until manual delete. No silent LRU eviction.

**Edge cases:**
- Camera permission denied → import path (PHPicker) must remain fully usable; app must not degrade to a dead end.
- App backgrounded/killed mid-processing → resume from the last saved checkpoint (source or normalized image), not from a partially-run Vision request; never silently lose the source.
- Export destination/share-sheet failure → traced file remains available in recent traces so the user isn't forced to rerun the pipeline.
- HEIC/EXIF orientation must be respected at ingest, before any correction — a silently rotated/mirrored result destroys trust instantly.
- Ruled/grid notebook paper must be distinguished from foreground sketch lines in preprocessing, since this is one of the four committed acceptance fixtures.
- Lettering vs. closed-shape illustrations fail differently (stroke continuity/corner fidelity vs. closed loops not being accidentally filled) — both need to be covered by the acceptance bar, not just one.
- Faint/light pencil source material is a distinct failure mode from bad photo conditions and needs its own honest rejection message.
- VoiceOver users can't precision-tap a small stray vector fragment on a canvas — cleanup needs an accessible list-based fallback (select/delete/merge by name, not just by touch).
- Simplify-strength slider dragging must be debounced and operate on a downsampled preview (full-res only on commit) to avoid visible lag/thermal throttling on older supported devices.

**Data and privacy implications:**
- Sketches never leave the device by default; no account required in MVP.
- No analytics SDK, no phone-home crash reporter, no network entitlement usage in this build — the local-first claim must be auditable, not just asserted in copy.
- EXIF metadata (location, timestamp, device model) is stripped at ingest, not just at export, so nothing sensitive is sitting in retained assets waiting to leak once cloud sync is added later.
- Camera/photo-library usage-description strings must be specific and true, not boilerplate.
- Recent-traces list is unbounded in count with manual delete only — no background/automatic deletion of a user's work.

**Risk register:**
- Capture-rescue overclaim: app markets itself as forgiving but only performs on near-ideal photos. Mitigation: named, testable support envelope plus fixture-based QA using deliberately imperfect photos.
- Vector quality overclaim: technically valid SVG that's still unusable (too many fragments/nodes). Mitigation: the existing clean-trace bar plus real downstream-open tests in a mainstream vector tool.
- Editing-scope mismatch: cleanup tools too weak for real failure modes while the app doesn't fail early enough to stop bad traces. Mitigation: keep cleanup tools mapped to actual failure classes; reject inputs that would need desktop-class editing.
- Trust decay across repeat use: same sketch succeeding once and failing later without explanation, or vague/inconsistent error messages. Mitigation: fixed rejection-reason vocabulary with consistent copy, faster/more predictable repeat use, remembered settings.
- Silent quality miss within "supported" bounds (e.g., a pencil sketch that's technically accepted but underperforms): mitigation is explicit contrast-sufficiency judgment at the gate rather than a blanket "pencil is fine" assumption.
- Half-built monetization risk avoided by decision above (no live paywall for non-existent features).
- Storage bloat / silent data loss risk avoided by the normalized-working-copy retention policy (bounded storage, no silent eviction).

**Final assumptions:**
- iPhone-first (not iPad-first) for MVP.
- Fully offline-capable after install; no server-side processing.
- One-sketch-at-a-time jobs; only a flat, bounded (by delete-only, not count) recent-traces list — no project/library management.
- Black-line output only; color-aware tracing is out of scope.
- Free tier ships the complete, unwatermarked core loop; no StoreKit/subscription code in MVP, only an informational "Pro — coming soon" settings entry.
- Autosave checkpoints at capture, post-normalization/review, post-trace, and post-edit.
- Raw capture is transient (through review only); normalized working image + vector result are the durable, recoverable assets.
- Quality-gate rejection reasons form a fixed, named set with dedicated copy, including a distinct "sketch too faint" reason separate from photo-condition reasons.
- Live in-capture framing guidance is a direction for the architecture phase to design against, not a locked implementation choice.
- Device floor and precise perf/thermal targets are deferred to the architecture phase, but debounced/downsampled interactive previews are a locked requirement for any slider-driven re-processing.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# cleanline — Detailed Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 00:55:52._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-a2
Selected app slug: cleanline

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# cleanline

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

cleanline

## Category

Graphics & Design

## One-Sentence Promise

Photographs a hand-drawn sketch and traces it to clean, editable vector paths (SVG/PDF) via on-device Vision.

## Target User

Illustrators/print-on-demand sellers who sketch on paper but need vector output.

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 6 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 2 Food & Drink apps, 2 Graphics & Design apps, and 2 News apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 12 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 6, and build all 6 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

The main job of this phase is to remove any ambiguity that would later turn into bad design decisions, dishonest marketing, or a brittle architecture. For cleanline, I think the right resolved requirement set is narrower and stricter than teams usually want, because this product only earns trust if it refuses to pretend. The user is not buying “computer vision.” She is buying confidence that a kitchen-table sketch can become a usable vector file on her phone without desktop cleanup theater. So the app should explicitly support one artifact class in MVP: high-contrast monochrome linework on plain paper, captured as a single sketch per job. That means pen, marker, dark pencil linework if contrast is sufficient, but not shaded graphite drawings, watercolor, multi-color illustration, collages, or pages with several unrelated drawings the user expects the app to separate automatically. The app should accept either live camera capture or photo import, immediately run a capture-quality gate, and either proceed with correction-and-trace or stop with a specific retake reason. “Specific” matters: too dark, too blurry, too skewed, too much shadow, no clear sketch region, or contrast too low. A generic “couldn’t process image” is not good enough for this user because it teaches her nothing and makes the app feel random.

I would lock in one more product requirement that earlier phases implied but did not fully operationalize: every transformation step needs to be inspectable, reversible, and autosaved. Not infinitely tweakable, but inspectable enough that the user can see what the app did to her sketch before trusting the export. Concretely, the user should be able to view original photo, corrected monochrome preview, and traced vector preview; adjust the few controls that correspond to actual failure modes; and undo/redo edits without fear of losing the good version. That matters because the emotional risk here is not just “bad output,” it is “I can’t tell whether the app ruined my art or helped it.” The app has to make its judgment legible.

The edge cases that matter most are the ones that strike this exact workflow, not generic app edge cases. If camera permission is denied, import must still keep the product usable; otherwise the app dies on first friction. If the user backgrounds the app during processing, the job must either resume safely or reopen into a recoverable pending/failed state with the source image preserved; losing the trace because iOS reclaimed memory would feel like betrayal for a five-minute workflow. If the export destination fails, the traced file still needs to remain locally available in recents so the user does not have to rerun the pipeline. If the input technically can be processed but falls outside the app’s quality bar, the app should prefer honest rejection over low-confidence output. That includes very faint pencil, ruled notebook paper overpowering the sketch, severe glare, clipped edges of the page, or dense crosshatching that will explode node counts. Another subtle edge case: lettering and closed-shape drawings do not fail the same way. Lettering users care more about stroke continuity and corner fidelity; closed-shape illustration users care more about loops staying closed and holes not being accidentally filled. The acceptance bar should preserve both classes explicitly.

Data and privacy implications are straightforward but important enough to state precisely. The product promise improves if the app can honestly say the sketch never leaves the device by default, no account required, no server trace, no cloud processing. That is not just a compliance statement; it is user value for someone working on unreleased designs. But local-first creates a data-retention decision that should be explicit, not accidental. The app needs a bounded recent-traces list, clear delete behavior, and a policy for whether originals are stored alongside processed previews and exports. My recommendation is: keep the original photo and derived assets by default until the user deletes the trace, because recoverability matters more than minimizing storage in MVP, but show that clearly. If later phases want privacy-sensitive users to be able to auto-delete originals after export, that should be a deliberate setting, not hidden behavior. Also, imported and captured images may contain EXIF metadata including location and time; unless there is a user benefit to preserving it, export artifacts should not propagate irrelevant photo metadata into the output.

The biggest product risk remains the same, but this phase should sharpen it: the app can easily feel competent on ideal inputs and collapse on normal ones. Normal means a kitchen light, slight page curl, slight hand shadow, imperfect framing, and a user who will not spend three attempts staging the paper like a product photographer. So the risk register should include capture-rescue overclaim, vector quality overclaim, and editing-scope mismatch. Capture-rescue overclaim means the app markets itself as forgiving but only succeeds on near-scanned inputs; mitigation is a named support envelope and fixture-based QA with deliberately imperfect photos. Vector quality overclaim means the app exports technically valid SVG that is still awful to use because it contains too many fragments or points; mitigation is the clean-trace bar already defined, plus explicit downstream-open tests in a real vector tool. Editing-scope mismatch means the cleanup tools are too weak to fix the predictable failure modes, yet the app also does not fail early enough to stop bad traces; mitigation is to keep the cleanup toolset tied to failure classes and reject inputs that would require desktop-class editing.

A quieter but serious risk is trust decay across repeat use. Trace one can feel magical and still fail as a product if trace two and three require the same paranoia. The app needs to get faster and more predictable with repetition, which means remembering prior settings, reopening recent traces instantly, and making error reasons consistent enough that the user learns how to feed it good input. If the quality gate feels arbitrary, or if the same sketch succeeds once and fails later without explanation, the user will stop trusting it even if the average output is decent.

The final assumptions I would lock are these. MVP assumes iPhone-first, not iPad-first. MVP assumes offline operation after install. MVP assumes one-sketch-at-a-time jobs and a bounded local recent list, not project organization. MVP assumes black-line output is the commercial payload; color-aware tracing is out. MVP assumes the free tier includes honest, usable SVG/PDF export and that paid value comes later from throughput, fidelity, and advanced editing, not from withholding the basic result. MVP assumes autosave at capture, post-trace, and post-edit checkpoints. MVP assumes the app will explicitly reject inputs outside its supported capture envelope instead of trying to salvage everything.

The best direction is still the same: make cleanline the fastest trustworthy path from paper sketch to usable vector on a phone. The biggest objection to that direction is that “trustworthy” is much harder than “functional,” and the team could fool itself with a demo that works on curated samples. The detail this phase output must preserve is that honesty is part of the feature set: specific retake guidance, bounded support claims, recoverable state, inspectable intermediate results, and exports that are truly worth opening downstream. If any of that gets watered down later, the app may still ship, but it will stop deserving its promise.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Let me walk through this from the angle of what could quietly break the promise we've already locked, rather than re-litigating the promise itself.

Starting with the thing nobody's pinned down yet: is a real StoreKit subscription actually in scope for this MVP build, or is "batch processing / fine-detail tracing / pro editing / cloud sync" purely an architectural placeholder we're reserving? The brief phase talked about these as the monetization story, but the execution contract only commits to a free core loop. If we quietly build an actual purchasable subscription with no real premium feature behind it yet, that's either vaporware or scope-creep pressure to half-build batch/fine-detail/cloud-sync just to justify the paywall. I'd resolve this now as an explicit assumption: MVP ships the complete free loop only, with an honestly-labeled "Pro (coming soon)" entry point in settings that explains what's coming and why, but no live purchase flow, no App Store Connect subscription product, no StoreKit code gating anything. That keeps monetization from becoming a shadow feature list this MVP has to half-implement.

On image handling specifically, there's a cluster of edge cases that the fixture-set testing won't surface unless we name them now. Notebook paper with ruled lines or a grid is one of our own four fixtures ("messy-but-recoverable notebook photo") — the threshold/contour pipeline needs an explicit strategy for distinguishing background paper lines from foreground sketch lines, not just contrast isolation, or that fixture will fail by tracing the ruled lines as extra paths. Photos from the modern camera stack can be 24–48MP HEIC with embedded orientation EXIF; if the pipeline reads pixel buffers without respecting orientation, corrected output silently rotates 90 degrees, which is a classic iOS bug that looks fine in the simulator (where captures are already upright) and breaks on device. I'd also flag ink color: the contract assumes "dominant line color," but real sketches are drawn in blue ballpoint or graphite pencil as often as black pen, and pencil in particular has low contrast against white paper and no true black — if the isolation step is hard-coded to detect dark-on-light in a way tuned only for black ink, pencil sketches (a very plausible real input) will underperform without ever hitting the honest retake-photo error, which is worse than failing — it's a silent quality miss inside supported bounds. That needs to either be tested explicitly or named as a further non-goal beyond what's already written.

On failure and interruption: we already agreed on autosave at every pipeline stage, but I want to name the specific recovery states that implies, because "autosave" alone doesn't tell you what happens when the user reopens after a kill mid-processing — does the app resume from the last completed stage, or discard the partial work and prompt to restart from the saved photo? I'd assume the latter (restart from photo, don't try to resume mid-Vision-request) since resuming a partially-run image pipeline deterministically is a much harder guarantee to make than "the source photo is safe." Separately, thermal/perf risk: running perspective correction, contour extraction, and curve fitting on a full-resolution modern photo, potentially re-triggered live as a user drags a simplify-strength slider, is real CPU work — on an older supported device this could cause visible lag or thermal throttling if the slider isn't debounced and downsampled-preview vs. full-res-final aren't separated. I'd want a documented device floor (something like iPhone with A12/iOS 17, matching what's realistic for Vision + on-device performance) rather than silently discovering during build that it's unusably slow on minimum supported hardware.

Storage is a data-lifecycle question the brief left open: does the app keep the full original capture forever, or downsample/discard after the corrected working image and vector output exist? With 48MP photos and a "recent traces" list that's meant to persist, storage can balloon fast, and nobody's defined retention. I'd propose: keep a downsampled working copy (sufficient for re-trace/re-edit) rather than the raw capture, and treat the recent-traces list as unbounded in count but with manual delete as the only cleanup mechanism for MVP — no silent LRU eviction, because silently deleting someone's design without them asking is a worse data-loss story than just letting storage grow and giving them a delete button.

On privacy specifically — since local-first here is a trust claim, not just an architecture choice — I'd treat "no analytics SDK, no crash reporter that phones home, no network entitement usage at all in this build" as a hard requirement to make the claim auditable, not just aspirational copy. It's easy for a build phase to add a third-party crash reporter for convenience and quietly contradict "your sketches never leave your device." Camera and photo-library usage-description strings also need to say something specific and true (not boilerplate) since generic descriptions are a common App Store review rejection reason, and we should decide now whether photo import uses the limited-photo-library picker (PHPickerViewController, which doesn't require library permission at all) versus full library access — I'd default to PHPicker specifically because it sidesteps a permission prompt entirely and is strictly better UX for a single-photo-import use case.

On accessibility, the one real risk is the before/after review and path-selection cleanup UI, which is inherently visual/spatial (tap a stray path to select it). A VoiceOver user can't reliably tap-select an arbitrary vector path on a canvas by touch. I'd want a fallback list-based selection mode (a plain accessible list of detected paths/fragments with a delete/merge action) as the accessible equivalent, named now as a requirement rather than left to "the canvas has accessibility labels," because canvas hit-testing accessibility is genuinely hard and usually gets skipped under time pressure unless it's an explicit acceptance item.

Last thing worth naming as a testing risk rather than a feature risk: the "human side-by-side resemblance check" in our clean-trace bar is inherently manual, not automatable. For it to mean anything at build/QA time, the four fixture images need to actually exist as checked-in test assets with a documented manual verification checklist — otherwise "passes human resemblance check" quietly becomes "looked fine to whoever built it," which is exactly the false-confidence failure mode we already said the app itself must avoid; the build process shouldn't be allowed to commit that same sin against itself.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Claude’s two open questions are the right ones, and I think we should resolve them in the strictest, least-self-deceiving way.

On monetization: I do not think a live StoreKit subscription belongs in this MVP at all. Not because subscriptions are bad, but because this phase has been obsessing, correctly, over honesty. Shipping a purchase flow for batch processing, fine-detail tracing, pro editing, or cloud sync when none of those are actually present at production value would be exactly the kind of overclaiming we keep warning against in the tracing experience itself. The clean product move is: ship the full free loop only, no paywall code, no StoreKit, no disabled premium toggles inside the main workflow. If we want a placeholder, keep it informational and low-pressure in settings: “Pro is planned for batch tracing, higher-fidelity modes, advanced path editing, and backup.” That preserves the monetization story without turning MVP into a fake commerce shell. I’d go further: if a feature is not real enough that a paying POD seller would feel immediate recurring value from it, it should not appear as a purchase option yet. This user is already skeptical of tools that promise magic and deliver friction; a half-built paywall would poison trust before the tracing engine even gets judged.

On storage, I’m siding with Claude’s instinct but with one adjustment. We should not keep the raw 48MP original forever by default. That’s engineering convenience masquerading as recoverability, and for a recent-traces utility it will create silent storage bloat fast. But I also don’t want us to throw away useful source fidelity too aggressively and discover later that retracing from a too-small derivative hurts quality. So the requirement I’d lock is: persist a normalized working original, not necessarily the raw camera file. In practice that means respect EXIF orientation immediately, flatten into an internal upright representation, downsample to a ceiling that is high enough for re-tracing and export-quality line recovery, strip irrelevant metadata, and keep that normalized source plus corrected preview plus vector result until the user deletes the trace. That gives us recoverability, predictable storage, and a cleaner privacy story. I agree with Claude that silent eviction is unacceptable in MVP. A bounded recent list means bounded in UI concept, not “we might delete your work in the background.” Manual delete only.

The other edge cases Claude raised should stop being “good notes” and become product requirements. PHPicker over broad photo-library permission is an easy yes because it reduces friction and strengthens the privacy claim without hurting the core loop. EXIF orientation handling is not an implementation detail here; it is part of product correctness, because a rotated or mirrored sketch destroys trust instantly. Ruled notebook lines need explicit treatment in the preprocessing story because one of our own acceptance fixtures depends on it; if we can’t suppress or ignore common ruled-line backgrounds reliably enough, then the “messy but recoverable notebook photo” fixture is dishonest. On ink/color coverage, I think we should be careful not to drift back into overbreadth. Black ink and dark marker are definitely in. Dark pencil is in only when the quality gate judges contrast sufficient. Blue ballpoint is not a guaranteed supported class for MVP; if it works, great, but I would rather exclude it explicitly than silently underperform on a very common input and call that success. And the accessibility point is real: a VoiceOver user cannot be asked to precision-tap a tiny stray vector fragment on a canvas. So yes, I think Claude is right that we need a list-based accessible fallback for path cleanup actions rather than pretending canvas accessibility is solved because elements have labels.

The bigger product principle tying all of this together is that cleanline should behave like a careful craft tool, not a flashy scanner toy. That means it stores enough to protect the user’s work but not so much that it becomes sloppy with their device storage or metadata; it talks about Pro plans without selling vapor; it uses the least-invasive permission path; and it only claims input classes it can actually handle with dignity. If we lock those decisions now, later design and architecture work gets simpler, not harder, because the app stops pretending to be broader than it is.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex's "keep the original photo and derived assets by default until the user deletes the trace" and my "downsample and discard the raw capture" aren't actually as far apart as the coordinator framed them — I think there's a real synthesis here rather than a pick-one. The question to ask is: recoverable *for what*? If a user reopens a trace three weeks later, what do they actually want to redo? Almost certainly they want to re-run vectorization with different settings, redo the cleanup edits, or re-export at a different size — not re-derive perspective correction from a raw 48MP capture. So my proposal: keep the raw original only transiently, through the correction/review step where the user can see and undo what perspective correction and threshold did — that's exactly the "inspectable, reversible" requirement Codex is (rightly) insisting on. Once the user proceeds past that review into vectorization, downsample and discard the full-res raw, and persist the corrected working image plus the vector data as the thing that lives in recent traces forever until manually deleted. That gives Codex's recoverability guarantee for the part of the pipeline that's actually recoverable in practice, without every saved trace silently costing 20+MB of raw capture that nobody will ever look at again. If someone finds a case where they genuinely need to re-crop from the original after confirming, that's a real but rare enough case that "just retake the photo" is an honest answer, not a betrayal.

That same discard-the-raw moment is also where I'd fold in Codex's EXIF point and push it further: strip EXIF (location, timestamp, device model) at ingest, not just at export. If we only strip it on export but keep it sitting in the retained internal asset, we've created a quiet privacy liability the moment cloud sync gets added later — someone's opted into backup for their recent-traces history and suddenly location data they never meant to keep is riding along. Strip it once, at the door, and there's nothing left to leak downstream by accident.

On the artifact-class scope Codex added — pen/marker/dark-pencil, not shaded graphite or multicolor — I agree, but I want to push on the pencil case specifically because it's not a clean yes/no like "no watercolor." A dark, heavily-pressed pencil line and a light, sketchy underdrawing pencil line are both "pencil," and only one of them is in scope. That's not a photography problem a retake fixes — no amount of better lighting turns a faint 2H pencil sketch into high-contrast linework, because the user drew it faintly. Which means "contrast too low" as a single generic retake reason is actually hiding two very different situations: bad photo conditions (fixable by retaking) versus faint source material (fixable only by redrawing darker or going over it with a pen). I think the quality gate needs to distinguish those in its messaging — something like a specific "sketch is too light to trace reliably — try darkening the lines or retracing with a pen" reason, separate from the generic lighting/shadow/skew retake reasons — because giving someone photography advice for a problem that's actually about their pencil pressure is exactly the kind of "generic error that teaches nothing" Codex is arguing against, just one level deeper than the point they already made.

That connects to the trust-decay risk both of us flagged from different angles — Codex called it out as "same sketch succeeds once and fails later without explanation," I'd tie the fix directly to implementation: this only works if the quality-gate reasons are a fixed, named enum (skew-too-high, shadow-coverage-too-high, blur-too-high, contrast-too-low, sketch-too-faint, no-sketch-region-detected, ruled-paper-interference) each with its own specific copy, not a generic string the pipeline free-forms per attempt. A fixed vocabulary is what makes errors learnable across repeat use — if the same underlying condition produces different-sounding messages on different days, the user can never build the mental model of "oh, it's the shadow thing again," and we've undermined the exact repeat-use trust curve the brief phase already said was the thing to protect above all else.

One more thing I want to put on the table that neither of us has raised yet: whether the capture-quality gate runs only after the photo is taken, or whether we lean on live framing guidance during capture — the same idea Apple's own document scanner (VNDocumentCameraViewController) already does, showing real-time edge detection and skew warnings while you're still holding the phone over the page, before you commit to a shot. If our gate is purely post-capture, every rejected photo costs a full retake cycle — take, wait for processing, get told "too much skew," try again. If we get live feedback during framing, a lot of retake-worthy photos never get taken in the first place, which directly improves the "3-5 minutes to export" success metric we already committed to and reduces how often a user ever sees the honest-rejection error at all. I don't think we need to settle the implementation here — that's an architecture-phase call, and there's a real question of whether to build custom live guidance or lean on VisionKit's existing scanner UI — but I want it named now as a requirement direction, because "capture quality gate" quietly defaulting to post-capture-only during build would be a real loss against our own success criteria, and it's the kind of thing that's much cheaper to decide now than to retrofit after the camera flow is built around a single "take photo, then evaluate" moment.

On the StoreKit question — Codex hasn't weighed in yet, so I'll hold my position and make the strongest case for it: building a live purchase flow with real StoreKit products for features that don't exist yet (batch, fine-detail tracing, pro editing, cloud sync) means either faking a subscription that unlocks nothing, or rushing half-built versions of those four features just to have something behind the paywall — and "ship a subscription for features that don't really work yet" is precisely the kind of dishonesty this whole product is defined against. I'd rather this phase lock in: MVP ships the complete free loop only, with a clearly-labeled non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" settings entry that's honest about what's planned and why, and zero StoreKit code in this build. If Codex disagrees I want to hear the actual argument, but I think this is the one where "genuine value, never cosmetics-only" monetization actually argues for waiting rather than building a placeholder paywall early.
