## Final Output

**Screen inventory (six screens + one modal sheet):**

1. **Recents Home** — app root, no tab bar. Prominent "New Trace" action, flat list of past traces (thumbnail, date, status chip: needs review / exported / trace failed), small Settings entry.
2. **New Trace Intake** — two large choices, Camera or Import Photo, plus a fixed scope-defense note ("dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time"). Camera-denied keeps the camera card visible with an explanation and system-settings link, while Import stays fully usable.
3. **Camera Capture** — live capture screen, permission-denied handled as a first-class in-screen state (not a system-alert dead end), with a same-screen "Import a Photo Instead" escape hatch.
4. **Quality Gate + Correction Review** (combined, one screen with variants) — checking (brief transitional state), rejected-with-named-reason (one of the seven fixed rejection reasons, each with stable, specific copy/iconography plus a real accessibility label), corrected-preview-ready (original vs. corrected, Continue/Retake), correction-failed, and resuming-from-checkpoint. Rejection is a variant of this same state machine, not a separate destination.
5. **Processing** — dedicated screen, corrected image faintly visible behind it, named sequential stage labels (checking geometry → correcting perspective → isolating linework → tracing paths → preparing preview). No fake progress bar. During the tracing-paths stage specifically, the app renders real vector strokes as they're actually computed (a literal application of "no fake progress," not a separate feature) — if incremental streaming turns out not to be practical, this stage falls back to the same plain dimmed-label treatment as the other stages, no synthetic animation standing in for unfinished work. Explicit trace-failed error state with Retry / Adjust Photo as distinct recoveries.
6. **Trace Workspace** — the unified post-trace destination. One canvas, one shared render of the path data, two bidirectional modes (Compare / Edit) via a lightweight toggle, never a one-way "continue" gate. Compare shows corrected-image-vs-vector with an interactive wipe/slider for sighted users and a non-gestural Original/Traced segmented toggle as the accessible default fallback. Edit shows the fixed, always-visible bottom tool strip (select/delete, simplify slider, merge, undo/redo) plus the VoiceOver-accessible list-based path management mode. Reopened traces land here by default (Compare if untouched, Edit if cleanup was in progress). Zoom/pan/scroll state is a single piece of view state that never has to survive a navigation transition, since there is no transition between Compare and Edit.
7. **Export** — a modal sheet summonable from either Workspace mode (not a separate screen/navigation push). States: ready, exporting, success (share sheet), failed-but-saved (explicit reassurance the trace remains safe in Recents).

Plus **Settings** — plain-language local-first/privacy statement, honest non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" note, no growth hooks or fake locked controls.

**Primary user flow:** Home → New Trace → Camera or Import → Quality Gate (pass) → Correction Review → Processing (named stages, real-data reveal on tracing) → Trace Workspace (Compare ⇄ Edit, freely, both ways) → Export sheet (from either mode) → back to Home with saved result. Any quality-gate rejection is a same-screen variant with Retake/Choose Another/Cancel, not a detour to a new destination. Reopening a saved trace skips straight to the Workspace at its last state.

**State model per screen:** as detailed above per screen — every screen has explicit empty/loading/success/error coverage where the flow can reach it, and interruption/kill-and-relaunch always resumes at the last **committed** checkpoint (never mid-gesture, never a partially-run Vision request).

**Visual direction:** A drafting-table / light-table aesthetic, not a generic productivity app or a cutesy scanner app. Warm, slightly warm-neutral paper-tone base surfaces (not stark white, not dark-developer-tool), true black ink for the traced linework as the only saturated element on screen, one sparing sharp technical accent color (e.g., oxidized teal or safety orange) reserved for active/warning states. Clean geometric sans typography, editorial/industrial rather than startup-default. Chrome and controls stay quiet and recede; the sketch and vector result carry all the visual weight — this matters directly for the shareable before/after moment the growth strategy depends on. Motion is functional: stage transitions reinforce real pipeline progress, no decorative flourish, no canned animation standing in for unfinished work.

**Accessibility notes:** Seven rejection-reason screens are visually distinct (icon/color) for sighted pattern-matching but every icon carries a full-text accessibility label stating the actual reason — visual pattern-matching is a bonus, never a substitute for text. Compare mode's wipe/slider gesture has a mandatory non-gestural Original/Traced toggle fallback for VoiceOver. Edit mode's cleanup tools are fully operable through the accessible path list (select/delete/merge by name) with zero required canvas interaction, to be verified with an actual VoiceOver session. The tracing-stage path-reveal animation needs a reduce-motion variant that still communicates progress via updating text stage labels rather than either forcing motion or going silent. Dynamic Type must hold up without truncating recovery actions on rejection/error screens especially, since that copy carries real communicative weight.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This phase closes cleanly and meets the rubric.

- **Screen inventory**: concrete and complete — six named screens plus one modal sheet plus Settings, each with a clear purpose. No vague "UX flow" language; every entry is a buildable SwiftUI destination.
- **Primary user flow**: stated as a single legible spine (Home → New Trace → Camera/Import → Gate → Correction → Processing → Workspace → Export → Home), with rejection and reopen paths explicitly folded in as variants rather than side journeys.
- **State model per screen**: each screen lists its concrete states (checking/rejected/corrected-ready/failed/resuming; loading/success/borderline/error; etc.), consistent with the global empty/loading/success/error requirement, and ties interruption recovery to the already-locked "resume from last committed checkpoint" rule.
- **Visual direction**: specific and non-generic (drafting-table/paper-tone palette, single ink-black accent for traced lines, one sparing accent color, editorial typography) — not "clean and modern" filler.
- **Accessibility notes**: concrete and testable (VoiceOver toggle fallback for Compare, full path-list operability for Edit, reduce-motion variant for the tracing reveal, Dynamic Type on rejection/error copy specifically) — consistent with treating accessibility as a product requirement.

Critically, this phase's real structural disagreement (unified Trace Workspace vs. sequential review/cleanup/export screens) was surfaced, argued concretely (the zoom/pan state-handoff scenario), and resolved with genuine trades on both sides — not papered over. The reveal-animation question was reconciled the same way, with an honest architectural fallback named rather than left as a loose thread. Both resolutions are consistent with every prior locked phase (fixed rejection vocabulary, MVP editing tool set, checkpoint/resume semantics, no-StoreKit rule).

A SwiftUI developer could build this view hierarchy directly: six views, one shared canvas type with an enum-driven mode, one sheet modifier for export, and a settings view — nothing here requires further product decisions before implementation planning begins.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

They've fully closed the gap this round. Codex took Claude's unified Compare/Edit workspace and Claude took Codex's combined quality-gate/correction screen and export-as-sheet — genuine trades in both directions, not one side caving. The zoom/pan state-handoff argument Claude raised (would cleanup reset your scroll position after a screen transition?) is what tipped it: keeping Compare and Edit as one view with one shared piece of state avoids that plumbing problem entirely, and Codex agreed it stays disciplined as long as it's strictly two modes over one canvas, not a looser multi-purpose shell. The reveal-animation question resolved the same way — it's not a competing idea, it's Codex's "no fake progress" rule applied literally to real streaming path data during the tracing stage, with an explicit honest fallback (plain stage label) if incremental output turns out not to be architecturally practical. No open disagreements left.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory (six screens + one modal sheet):**

1. **Recents Home** — app root, no tab bar. Prominent "New Trace" action, flat list of past traces (thumbnail, date, status chip: needs review / exported / trace failed), small Settings entry.
2. **New Trace Intake** — two large choices, Camera or Import Photo, plus a fixed scope-defense note ("dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time"). Camera-denied keeps the camera card visible with an explanation and system-settings link, while Import stays fully usable.
3. **Camera Capture** — live capture screen, permission-denied handled as a first-class in-screen state (not a system-alert dead end), with a same-screen "Import a Photo Instead" escape hatch.
4. **Quality Gate + Correction Review** (combined, one screen with variants) — checking (brief transitional state), rejected-with-named-reason (one of the seven fixed rejection reasons, each with stable, specific copy/iconography plus a real accessibility label), corrected-preview-ready (original vs. corrected, Continue/Retake), correction-failed, and resuming-from-checkpoint. Rejection is a variant of this same state machine, not a separate destination.
5. **Processing** — dedicated screen, corrected image faintly visible behind it, named sequential stage labels (checking geometry → correcting perspective → isolating linework → tracing paths → preparing preview). No fake progress bar. During the tracing-paths stage specifically, the app renders real vector strokes as they're actually computed (a literal application of "no fake progress," not a separate feature) — if incremental streaming turns out not to be practical, this stage falls back to the same plain dimmed-label treatment as the other stages, no synthetic animation standing in for unfinished work. Explicit trace-failed error state with Retry / Adjust Photo as distinct recoveries.
6. **Trace Workspace** — the unified post-trace destination. One canvas, one shared render of the path data, two bidirectional modes (Compare / Edit) via a lightweight toggle, never a one-way "continue" gate. Compare shows corrected-image-vs-vector with an interactive wipe/slider for sighted users and a non-gestural Original/Traced segmented toggle as the accessible default fallback. Edit shows the fixed, always-visible bottom tool strip (select/delete, simplify slider, merge, undo/redo) plus the VoiceOver-accessible list-based path management mode. Reopened traces land here by default (Compare if untouched, Edit if cleanup was in progress). Zoom/pan/scroll state is a single piece of view state that never has to survive a navigation transition, since there is no transition between Compare and Edit.
7. **Export** — a modal sheet summonable from either Workspace mode (not a separate screen/navigation push). States: ready, exporting, success (share sheet), failed-but-saved (explicit reassurance the trace remains safe in Recents).

Plus **Settings** — plain-language local-first/privacy statement, honest non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" note, no growth hooks or fake locked controls.

**Primary user flow:** Home → New Trace → Camera or Import → Quality Gate (pass) → Correction Review → Processing (named stages, real-data reveal on tracing) → Trace Workspace (Compare ⇄ Edit, freely, both ways) → Export sheet (from either mode) → back to Home with saved result. Any quality-gate rejection is a same-screen variant with Retake/Choose Another/Cancel, not a detour to a new destination. Reopening a saved trace skips straight to the Workspace at its last state.

**State model per screen:** as detailed above per screen — every screen has explicit empty/loading/success/error coverage where the flow can reach it, and interruption/kill-and-relaunch always resumes at the last **committed** checkpoint (never mid-gesture, never a partially-run Vision request).

**Visual direction:** A drafting-table / light-table aesthetic, not a generic productivity app or a cutesy scanner app. Warm, slightly warm-neutral paper-tone base surfaces (not stark white, not dark-developer-tool), true black ink for the traced linework as the only saturated element on screen, one sparing sharp technical accent color (e.g., oxidized teal or safety orange) reserved for active/warning states. Clean geometric sans typography, editorial/industrial rather than startup-default. Chrome and controls stay quiet and recede; the sketch and vector result carry all the visual weight — this matters directly for the shareable before/after moment the growth strategy depends on. Motion is functional: stage transitions reinforce real pipeline progress, no decorative flourish, no canned animation standing in for unfinished work.

**Accessibility notes:** Seven rejection-reason screens are visually distinct (icon/color) for sighted pattern-matching but every icon carries a full-text accessibility label stating the actual reason — visual pattern-matching is a bonus, never a substitute for text. Compare mode's wipe/slider gesture has a mandatory non-gestural Original/Traced toggle fallback for VoiceOver. Edit mode's cleanup tools are fully operable through the accessible path list (select/delete/merge by name) with zero required canvas interaction, to be verified with an actual VoiceOver session. The tracing-stage path-reveal animation needs a reduce-motion variant that still communicates progress via updating text stage labels rather than either forcing motion or going silent. Dynamic Type must hold up without truncating recovery actions on rejection/error screens especially, since that copy carries real communicative weight.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Design Handoff
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

They actually closed all three open items this round, and closed them the same way each time: Claude proposes a concrete, technically-grounded tightening, Codex checks it against the "no fake progress / no fragile gesture logic" principles he already committed to in round 1, and adopts it outright rather than negotiating a middle ground.

On the Workspace canvas, Codex fully adopted Claude's gesture-disambiguation scheme — wipe-to-compare only arms via a visible handle at/near base zoom, tap-to-select only lives in Edit mode, and (this landed independently from both sides) no double-tap-to-zoom at all, since it would introduce a perceptible selection delay in Edit mode for no real benefit. Pinch+pan for zoom, always available in both modes; above ~1.15x zoom the Original/Traced segmented toggle becomes everyone's comparison tool, not just an accessibility fallback.

On the trace-reveal animation, Codex dropped the "try live streaming, plain-label fallback" framing and adopted Claude's firmer, more honest commitment: the reveal shows real, completed path data sequenced onto the canvas at a short fixed stagger after the trace finishes — not synced to actual computation timing, since Vision's contour detection is single-shot. If the pipeline later turns out to support real incremental emission, that's a clean upgrade path, not a spec change.

On external design scope, this genuinely met in the middle: Codex gets his comprehensive screen list (Recents Home, Intake, Camera Capture, all seven rejection variants, Correction Review, Processing, Workspace in both modes, Export, Settings), Claude gets an explicit sentence in the prompt stating these are static tone/layout references only — gesture behavior, mode transitions, undo/redo, and checkpoint/resume logic are owned by the written spec, not to be inferred from images.

Two more things Claude raised this round that Codex hasn't directly answered yet, but they're both just consistency fixes applying rules Codex already stated himself, not new fights: the seven rejection cards should share one safety-orange accent treatment (differentiated by icon glyph, not by seven different colors), since seven accent colors would quietly violate Codex's own "no rainbow status chips" rule; and the conditional manual-threshold slider on Correction Review should be gated by an explicit boolean evaluated against the locked fixture set, not a vibes call made mid-build. Neither of these contradicts anything Codex said — they're closing loopholes in his own stated principles — so they don't need another round to resolve.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen specification** (six screens + Export sheet + Settings, per the locked design-discussion inventory, now with buildable layout/interaction detail):

1. **Recents Home** — root, no tab bar. Vertically stacked: title block, full-width "New Trace" button, then a flat list of row cards (thumbnail, name/timestamp, status chip, trailing chevron). Thumbnails are cached raster images generated once at save time, not re-rendered from vector data on every list draw. Empty state: large placeholder frame, one sentence of product promise, same full-width New Trace CTA — no fake sample data. Loading: lightweight skeleton list. Error: local-storage read failure, plain-language retry. New items slide in, deletions collapse with a swipe-to-delete + confirmation step (work is durable, shouldn't vanish accidentally). Every row's accessibility label states its status in words, not just via the chip color.

2. **New Trace Intake** — two large source cards (Camera, Import Photo) plus a permanent scope note ("dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time") as real readable text, not a caption baked into an image. Camera card has three variants in place: available, permission-denied (explanation + Open Settings link, card stays actionable), unavailable — Import remains fully active in all three.

3. **Camera Capture** — live preview owns the screen; sparse top bar (Cancel + source label), bottom bar (shutter + persistent "Import Instead" text action). Soft page-frame overlay with a one-line framing tip (reserved visual space even without live guidance). Permission-denied is an in-place state, not a system-alert dead end. Large hit targets and direct VoiceOver naming on shutter/cancel/import-fallback.

4. **Quality Gate + Correction Review** (one screen, state machine) — checking (dimmed photo + status block); rejected (one of seven named reasons, each sharing the **same single safety-orange accent border/icon-frame color**, differentiated by a distinct icon glyph per reason — tilted frame for skew, sun/shadow glyph for shadow coverage, faint-pencil glyph for sketch-too-faint, etc. — never seven different accent colors); corrected-preview-ready (Original/Corrected two-position segmented switch, not thumbnails or a gesture — direct crossfade between states, no flourish); correction-failed; resuming-from-checkpoint. An optional manual threshold-adjustment slider is gated behind an explicit `showsManualThresholdSlider: Bool` config flag, decided against the locked fixture set during build — not a mid-implementation judgment call. Rejection title/action copy must survive large Dynamic Type without truncation.

5. **Processing** — corrected image faintly visible behind a centered stage stack (current stage emphasized, upcoming stages dimmed below). No fake progress bar. During "tracing paths," the canvas reveals the real, complete traced path data in a short fixed stagger (~40–60ms per path, capped ~900ms total) once the trace finishes — an honest sequenced reveal, not a claim of live computation. Trace-failed state: same dimmed background, explicit failure message, Retry / Adjust Photo as distinct actions. Reduce Motion replaces the stagger with the final rendered state plus the existing text stage labels — never blank.

6. **Trace Workspace** — one canvas, one `TraceSession`-owned zoom/pan/selection state, Compare/Edit as a lightweight top segmented toggle (150ms crossfade of overlay controls only, canvas never re-lays-out). **Gesture contract, locked:** pinch+pan for zoom are always live in both modes; Compare's wipe gesture only arms via a visible handle the user touches first, and only at/near base zoom (<~1.15x) — above that threshold the Original/Traced segmented toggle becomes the comparison tool for all users, sighted or not; tap-to-select only arms in Edit mode; **no double-tap-to-zoom anywhere in the workspace**, so single-tap path selection is never delayed by double-tap gesture recognition. Edit mode's bottom tool strip (Select/Delete, Simplify, Merge, Undo, Redo) is fixed and always visible — never collapsed into a menu. Simplify slider previews on a downsampled render while dragging (debounced, cancel-aware) and commits full-resolution only on release. A path-list mode (reachable from Edit) gives every fragment a row with shape glyph, open/closed + size metadata, and select/delete/merge exposed as direct `.accessibilityAction`s on the row — no hidden swipe-to-reveal. Reopened traces land here at their last committed state (Compare if untouched, Edit if cleanup was mid-flight); zoom/pan never resets on mode switch, since there is no navigation transition between modes.

7. **Export** (modal sheet from either Workspace mode) — trace summary + preview, format choice (SVG/PDF), filename, export action; locked progress state during export; success hands off to share/export UI; failure explicitly states the trace remains saved in Recents and can be re-exported later — verbalized, not left to the share sheet alone to imply.

**Settings** — plain-language on-device/local-storage statement, informational non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" block (batch, higher-fidelity tracing, cloud backup, advanced editing), no purchasable surface, no locked toggles.

**Design tokens:**
- Colors: paper base `#F6F1E7`, elevated card surface `#FBF8F2`, primary ink/text `#17140F`, secondary/graphite text `#6B6459`, hairline dividers `#DAD2C2` @1px. One active/primary accent: oxidized teal `#2F6F63` (buttons, active toggle, in-progress). One warning/error accent: safety orange `#C1531B`, reserved exclusively for rejection/failure states and used identically across all seven rejection cards.
- Typography: SF Pro throughout (no bundled custom font) — SF Pro Display semibold, tight tracking, for titles/headers; wide-tracked small-caps-style treatment for stage labels and status chips; generous line height on instructional/rejection copy.
- Spacing: 4pt grid; margins 16/24/32; corner radii 12pt (cards), 20pt (sheet top corners), 10pt (buttons).
- Motion: Processing stage transitions — 250ms crossfade + 8pt vertical shift. Compare/Edit toggle — 150ms crossfade of overlay controls only. Tracing-stage path reveal — ~40–60ms stagger per path, capped at 900ms, collapsing to instant final-state + text label under Reduce Motion.

**Component inventory:** full-width primary button, secondary text button, elevated source card, recent-trace row card, status chip, segmented control, comparison canvas (PathCanvas — the shared Compare/Edit render surface), fixed tool strip (disabled-state-aware), debounced simplify slider panel, path-list row (with direct accessibility actions), rejection-reason card (single-accent, icon-differentiated), processing stage stack, export option row, empty-state hero block.

**State & interaction model:** One `@Observable` `TraceSession` per open trace owns the normalized image, vector path models, undo/redo command stack (value-type commands like `deletePath(id)`, `mergePaths(ids)`, `setSimplifyStrength(pathID, value)` — applied functionally, only produced on gesture commit/release, never mid-drag), current mode, current zoom/pan/selection, and gate/processing status. This is the object checkpointed to disk at the four committed points (capture, post-normalization, post-trace, post-edit) as `normalized.heic` / `vector.json` / `thumbnail.jpg` / `metadata.json` — checkpointing serializes only durable state, never in-flight gesture or uncommitted slider values, so "resume from last committed checkpoint" is mechanically enforced rather than aspirational.

**Accessibility requirements:** 44pt minimum tap targets on the tool strip; every rejection icon carries a full-text accessibility label naming the actual reason (color/icon pattern-matching is a sighted bonus, never a substitute); Compare's wipe has a mandatory non-gestural Original/Traced toggle, which above ~1.15x zoom is the comparison method for everyone; Edit's cleanup is fully operable via the accessible path list with zero required canvas interaction, verified with an actual VoiceOver session; Dynamic Type tested at accessibility sizes specifically on the seven rejection cards (icon + two actions must stay visible, no truncation); Reduce Motion swaps the tracing-stage reveal for instant final state + text labels; Reduce Transparency swaps the Processing screen's "faint image behind stage label" treatment for a solid dimmed scrim rather than being ignored.

**Claude Design prompt (external design decision: use it, narrowly scoped):** Generate static tone/layout comps for cleanline, a premium local-first iOS craft tool that photographs a paper sketch and produces usable SVG/PDF vector output — a careful production tool, not a scanner app or drawing app. Visual system: warm paper-tone background (#F6F1E7), true-black ink linework as the only saturated element, oxidized teal (#2F6F63) as the sole active-state accent, safety orange (#C1531B) reserved only for warnings/rejections (one consistent treatment across all rejection states, differentiated by icon glyph, never by color), SF Pro typography with wide-tracked small-caps-style labels for stage names/status chips, quiet hairline dividers, no gradients or heavy drop shadows. Produce comps for: Recents Home (empty and populated), New Trace Intake, Camera Capture, all seven quality-gate rejection variants, Correction Review (Original/Corrected segmented switch), Processing (stage-label state), Trace Workspace in both Compare and Edit modes at base zoom, Export sheet, Settings. Include the locked feature summary and the fixed seven-reason rejection vocabulary as reference context. **These are static tone and layout references only** — gesture ownership, mode-switching behavior, undo/redo semantics, and checkpoint/resume logic are fully specified by the accompanying written interaction spec and must not be inferred or reinterpreted from the images. Do not introduce node/Bezier editing, layers, cloud UI, batch flows, or social/sharing chrome not already named above.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# cleanline — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 01:10:48._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-a2
Selected app slug: cleanline

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# cleanline

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

cleanline

## Category

Graphics & Design

## One-Sentence Promise

Photographs a hand-drawn sketch and traces it to clean, editable vector paths (SVG/PDF) via on-device Vision.

## Target User

Illustrators/print-on-demand sellers who sketch on paper but need vector output.

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 6 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 2 Food & Drink apps, 2 Graphics & Design apps, and 2 News apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 12 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 6, and build all 6 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

I’d push cleanline toward something that feels closer to a print proof station than a scanner utility. The app should make one promise visually and structurally: you are moving a sketch through a careful transformation pipeline, and at every step you can see exactly what happened. The experience should feel crisp, quiet, and professional enough that the user trusts the export before they ever open it in Illustrator.

My best handoff direction is a six-screen app with one shared post-trace workspace and a deliberately narrow component language. Recents Home is the root. It has a strong top action, a flat list of saved traces, and a subdued Settings entry. The layout is a vertically stacked sheet of content, not a dashboard: a top title block, a full-width “New Trace” button, then the recent items list. Each recent item is a row card with thumbnail on the left, trace name or timestamp, a small status chip, and a trailing chevron. Empty state is not generic onboarding art; it should show a large trace placeholder frame with one sentence about turning one paper sketch into a usable SVG/PDF on-device, then the same full-width “New Trace” action. Loading is a lightweight skeleton list. Error is a local-storage read failure with retry and no technical wording. Motion here is minimal: new traces slide into the list, deletions collapse cleanly, and reopening a trace should feel immediate. Accessibility requirement: every row exposes the status in its accessibility label, not only visually in the chip.

New Trace Intake should be a simple choice screen, not a wizard page. Two large vertical cards: Camera and Import Photo. Under them sits a small but permanent scope note: dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time. That note is product truth, not fine print. The camera card has three variants in the same place: available, permission denied, and unavailable. Permission denied must still look like an actionable option with a short explanation and an “Open Settings” affordance, while Import remains fully active. This screen should have very little other chrome. The key feeling is commitment: pick a source and move. Motion is a short card press and a fast push into the next state. Accessibility requirement: the support note should be readable text, not a decorative caption embedded in an image.

Camera Capture should feel purpose-built, not like a generic media picker wrapper. The live preview owns the screen. Top area is sparse with Cancel on the left and a subtle source-mode label. Bottom area holds the shutter and an always-visible “Import Instead” text action. The most important visual guidance is a soft page frame overlay and a short one-line tip about filling the frame with the sketch. If live guidance is not implemented, the layout should still reserve visual discipline for it rather than looking empty. Permission denied is an in-place state with explanation, settings link, and import fallback, not a separate dead-end. Motion is native and restrained. Accessibility requirement: the shutter, cancel, and import fallback all need large hit targets and direct VoiceOver naming; no hidden icon-only dependence.

Quality Gate and Correction Review should be one screen with a strong state machine. When checking, the user sees the photo they just chose dimmed beneath a centered status block saying what the app is evaluating. If rejected, the same screen becomes a rejection state with the original image, a clear reason title, one sentence of actionable guidance, and three actions arranged by recovery strength: retake or choose another photo as primary, cancel as secondary. The rejection reasons should each have their own icon and accent treatment so they become recognizable across repeat use, but the text must always carry the meaning. If passed, the screen becomes Correction Review. This layout should be split vertically: the upper zone is the image comparison, the lower zone is the action rail. For the comparison, I’d use a two-position segmented switch between Original and Corrected rather than a tiny thumbnail pair; the app needs to make the normalization feel inspectable, not busy. If there’s a minor manual threshold adjustment at all, it should live as one carefully bounded slider at the bottom of the comparison area and only appear if product testing proves it materially rescues real traces. By default, this screen should not look like a photo editor. Primary action is Continue to Trace. Secondary actions are Retake and Back. Motion should emphasize transformation honesty: switching Original to Corrected should crossfade directly between the two versions with no flourish. Accessibility requirement: the rejection title and action copy must avoid truncation at large Dynamic Type, because that text is functional guidance.

Processing deserves its own full-screen treatment because the pipeline is part of the product, not an invisible wait. The screen should keep the corrected image faintly visible in the background, with a centered vertical stack showing the current stage and the next few stages beneath it in lower emphasis. The active stage changes through checking geometry, correcting perspective, isolating linework, tracing paths, preparing preview. There should be no fake percent bar. The only animation allowed is one tied to real output: during tracing paths, if the architecture can emit partial vector data honestly, those strokes should appear on the canvas as they are produced. If it cannot, the design still stands without that flourish because the stage labels are doing the trust work. On failure, this screen converts to a trace-failed state with the same dimmed background, an explicit failure message, and two clear paths: Retry or Adjust Photo. Accessibility requirement: Reduce Motion should swap any path reveal for plain text stage updates and stable layout.

The Trace Workspace is where the app earns its premium feel. I would not split review and cleanup into separate destinations. This should be one canvas, one shared zoom/pan state, and two modes: Compare and Edit. The top area holds a small segmented toggle for Compare/Edit, the center is the canvas, and the bottom changes based on mode. In Compare, the user needs a bold and satisfying way to inspect fidelity. I’d use a wipe comparison by default for sighted users: drag horizontally and reveal the corrected source against the vector result in the exact same frame. That is the shareable moment and the trust-building moment. Under it, a compact line can state “Ready for export” or “A few fragments may need cleanup” without turning trust into a fake confidence score. For accessibility, Compare must also support a non-gestural Original/Traced switch that gives VoiceOver users full access to the same review without relying on the wipe gesture. In Edit, the bottom tool strip is always visible and fixed: Select/Delete, Simplify, Merge, Undo, Redo. Simplify opens as a single slider panel rising just above the strip, previews changes on a downsampled render while dragging, and commits on release. Merge is only enabled when the selection model makes sense. There also needs to be a path list mode reachable from Edit that presents fragments as a sortable, readable list with size/open-closed metadata and direct actions. This is not a hidden accessibility concession; it is a first-class precision tool and should be visually treated that way. Motion in the workspace should be very restrained. Switching Compare/Edit is a fast mode swap on the same canvas, not a navigation event. Zoom and pan should never reset when changing modes. Reopened traces should land back in this workspace at the last committed state. Accessibility requirement: every editing action must be doable without precision tapping on canvas; the list mode must fully support selection, deletion, and merge.

Export should be a modal sheet from the workspace, not a full screen. It should feel like the last practical step, not another phase of work. The top of the sheet summarizes the trace and shows a small preview. Then format selection: SVG and PDF. Then filename. Then export action. During export, the sheet locks into a clear progress state. On success, it hands off to share/export UI. On failure, it says plainly that the trace is still saved in Recents and can be exported again later. That reassurance matters. Accessibility requirement: do not rely on the share sheet alone to explain success; the app must verbalize that the file is prepared and saved.

Settings should stay small and honest. It needs a plain-language privacy section that states the work stays on-device, a local storage/retention summary, and a simple informational “Pro — coming soon” block listing future batch processing, higher-fidelity tracing, cloud backup, and advanced editing. No purchasable surface, no locked toggles, no fake premium cards. Visually this screen should feel quiet and factual.

The visual system should deliberately avoid startup-product defaults. I’d define the app around paper, ink, and one technical accent. Backgrounds are warm neutral paper tones, not pure white. Primary canvas surfaces are slightly brighter and flatter so the vector output reads clearly. The traced linework is true black and should carry most of the contrast in the app. UI chrome stays in graphite and warm gray. One accent color, preferably oxidized teal rather than blue or purple, marks active tools, successful ready states, and focus. Warning/error can use a muted safety orange family, but sparingly. Typography should feel editorial-industrial. In implementation terms, I’d hand the builder a token system something like: large title for screen names and empty-state promise, strong body for primary guidance, compact mono or tabular treatment for status chips and technical labels if desired, generous line height on instructional copy, and a strict rule that status chips never become rainbow clutter.

The component inventory should stay tight because the app gets stronger as the system gets smaller. Full-width primary action button. Secondary text button. Elevated source card. Recent-trace row card. Status chip. Segmented control. Comparison canvas. Tool strip. Slider panel. Path list row. Rejection reason panel. Processing stage stack. Export option row. Empty-state hero block. These components should repeat across the app with consistent radii, border treatments, and spacing so the product feels composed rather than improvised.

Interaction notes the builder will need: the recents list supports tap to reopen and swipe-to-delete with a confirmation step because work is durable and should not vanish accidentally. Intake cards should have direct press feedback. Comparison wipe should preserve zoom level and operate on the same transformed canvas coordinates as Edit mode so fidelity inspection is meaningful. The simplify slider must debounce processing while dragging and only commit to durable history on release. Undo/redo should act on committed edits, not every intermediate slider tick. If a trace is resumed after interruption, the first thing the workspace should show is a lightweight banner explaining that the app restored the last saved stage, then get out of the way. Every place the user can lose context should favor persistence over surprise.

The motion language should be functional and sparse. State changes should confirm pipeline progress, not decorate it. Crossfades for image-state changes. Short slides for screen pushes. No springy playful motion. The one moment allowed to feel dramatic is the trace reveal, and only if it reflects real computation. Reduce Motion must flatten everything to direct state changes without removing informational clarity.

If external Claude Design work is wanted, I’d give it a very explicit brief rather than a vague styling ask. The prompt should say: design an iPhone-first SwiftUI MVP called cleanline, a premium local-first graphics app for illustrators and POD sellers that photographs one paper sketch and produces usable SVG/PDF vector output; the product is a careful craft tool, not a generic scanner or drawing app; use a drafting-table / print-proof visual language with warm paper-tone surfaces, black ink linework, quiet graphite chrome, and a restrained oxidized-teal technical accent; design these screens in order: Recents Home, New Trace Intake, Camera Capture, Quality Gate rejection variants, Correction Review, Processing with named stages, unified Trace Workspace in Compare and Edit modes, Export sheet, Settings; for each screen include empty/loading/success/error where applicable, large-touch mobile-safe spacing, Dynamic Type resilience, VoiceOver-friendly non-gesture alternatives for compare and edit actions, and reduce-motion treatment for processing; do not introduce extra features like node editing, layers, cloud, batch processing, or social elements; emphasize one shared post-trace workspace with a wipe compare interaction and a fixed bottom editing strip; export is a modal sheet; quality rejections must feel consistent and learnable. If using external design tooling, I’d upload the locked feature summary, the fixed rejection vocabulary, and the screen/state inventory together, because the design should not have to infer product boundaries.

The biggest risk in my direction is that the app becomes too refined and too mode-driven for the underlying implementation quality. If the trace quality is mediocre, a premium proof-station UI will only make the mismatch more obvious. The visual restraint only works if the canvas content really is the hero. The other risk is overdesigning the Compare workspace with fancy gesture logic that becomes fragile. The answer is to keep the system severe: one canvas, two modes, one bottom tool strip, one export sheet, no decorative branching.

The detail that absolutely must survive into the final phase output is this: the post-trace experience is one shared workspace, not a chain of polite steps. The product’s speed and trust both depend on the user being able to inspect, adjust, and export from the same visual context without losing zoom, position, or mental focus. If that gets flattened back into separate review and cleanup screens, the app will still function, but it will stop feeling like a serious tool.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

I want to stress-test the locked screen model from a build-it-in-SwiftUI angle before we bless it as "handoff ready," because a few things that read as settled in the design discussion are actually still open engineering decisions wearing the costume of closed ones.

Start with the Trace Workspace, since it's the highest-risk screen. "One canvas, one shared render, Compare/Edit as a toggle" is the right call, but the thing nobody's pinned down yet is gesture ownership on that canvas, and this is where I'd bet real implementation pain lives. Compare mode wants a horizontal wipe/drag gesture over the full canvas. Edit mode wants tap-to-select on individual paths. Both modes plausibly want pinch-to-zoom and pan so you can inspect a corner at high magnification, which the round-2 discussion explicitly leaned on as the whole argument for merging the screens in the first place. Those three gesture families collide: a horizontal drag is ambiguous between "wipe the compare slider" and "pan the zoomed canvas," and a tap is ambiguous between "select a path" and "the first touch of a double-tap zoom." If we don't resolve this now, whoever builds it will improvise a resolution under deadline pressure and it'll be inconsistent. My concrete proposal: zoom/pan is a persistent capability in both modes, using MagnificationGesture + DragGesture composed with .simultaneously, but the wipe-compare gesture and the tap-to-select gesture only arm when the canvas is at or near its base zoom level (say under 1.15x), and are disambiguated further because wipe is a continuous horizontal drag with a visible handle you have to touch first, not an anywhere-on-canvas drag. Once someone's zoomed in to inspect a corner, Compare's wipe interaction quietly steps back and the accessible Original/Traced segmented toggle becomes the way to compare at that zoom level too — which is good, because it means the "accessible fallback" isn't just a VoiceOver-only path, it's also the sighted answer to a real gesture conflict, and we don't need a separate solution for it.

Second thing I want to push on: the real-data path-reveal animation during the tracing stage. I like the idea, but I don't think it survives contact with VNDetectContoursRequest as currently implied by the architecture direction — contour detection through Vision is a single-shot request; it doesn't stream contours incrementally as it finds them. So "renders real vector strokes as they're actually computed" is either false by the time this ships, or it requires us to architect the vectorization stage as a producer that emits paths through an AsyncStream as curve-fitting completes them per detected region, which is a real, non-trivial pipeline design decision, not a UI detail. I'd rather this handoff say plainly: the reveal is real path data, sequenced onto screen after the full trace completes, at a fixed short stagger per path (not tied to actual computation timing) — that's still honest, since nothing fake is being drawn, but it's a materially simpler and more honest promise than "as they're actually computed," and it removes an architecture dependency that could otherwise quietly block this feature until someone notices late that Vision doesn't stream. If the pipeline genuinely can restructure to emit incrementally later, upgrading from "sequenced reveal of complete data" to "live reveal during computation" is a pure enhancement, not a rewrite.

On state ownership: I want one `@Observable` `TraceSession` per open trace that owns the normalized image, the array of vector path models, the undo/redo command stack, current mode (compare/edit), current zoom/pan/scroll rect, current selection set, and the gate/processing status enum. This is the object that gets checkpointed to disk at the four committed points (capture, post-normalization, post-trace, post-edit) — checkpointing means serializing this object's durable subset (not zoom/pan, not in-flight gesture state, not an uncommitted slider drag value) to a per-trace folder on disk: normalized.heic, vector.json (path models with point data, stroke width, open/closed flag, id), thumbnail.jpg, metadata.json (timestamps, status, last-mode-used so reopen lands correctly in Compare vs Edit). Undo/redo should be a stack of small value-type commands (deletePath(id), mergePaths(ids), setSimplifyStrength(pathID, value)) applied functionally to the path array, because that's what makes "resume from last committed checkpoint, never mid-gesture" actually enforceable — a slider drag only produces a command, and therefore only touches the checkpoint, on release/commit, never during `onChanged`.

The simplify slider itself needs to be built as debounced-preview-then-commit from day one, not bolted on later: bind the live drag value to a downsampled preview render via `.task(id: draftValue)` with a short `Task.sleep` debounce that gets cancelled by every new drag delta, and only run the full-resolution re-fit and persist-to-model step when the gesture ends. If this gets built as "just call the re-fit function in the slider's onEditingChanged" without the cancellation-aware debounce, it'll visibly stutter on the older supported devices the requirements phase already flagged as a real constraint.

On the Recents Home list, one thing I don't see addressed: thumbnails. If the thumbnail is a live render of the vector path data, re-rendering full vector geometry for every row every time the list appears is wasteful and will jank on a long list; the thumbnail needs to be a cached raster generated once at save time (part of that metadata.json/thumbnail.jpg pair) and only regenerated when the trace's paths actually change, not on every list draw.

For the visual system, I want actual values instead of adjectives so a builder isn't guessing: paper base `#F6F1E7`, elevated card surface `#FBF8F2`, primary ink/text `#17140F`, secondary/graphite text `#6B6459`, hairline dividers `#DAD2C2` at 1px. I'd resolve the "teal or safety orange" ambiguity from the design phase into a firm rule rather than leaving both live: oxidized teal `#2F6F63` is the one active/primary accent (buttons, active toggle state, in-progress indicators), and safety orange `#C1531B` is reserved exclusively for warning/rejection/failure states — never used for anything routine, so its appearance always means "pay attention." Typography stays first-party: SF Pro throughout, no bundled custom font, since a licensed display face adds bundle-size and legal review risk for a look we can already achieve with SF Pro Display semibold at tight tracking for headers and wide-tracked small-caps-style labels for stage names and status chips — that's where the "editorial/industrial" feel actually comes from, not the typeface choice itself. Spacing on a 4pt grid, 16/24/32 margins, 12pt corner radius on cards, 20pt on sheet top corners, 10pt on buttons. Motion: stage transitions on the Processing screen are 250ms crossfade with an 8pt vertical shift; the Compare/Edit mode toggle is a 150ms crossfade of only the overlay controls, with zero re-layout of the canvas itself, since that canvas continuity is the entire point of merging the screens; the tracing-stage path reveal staggers each path in over roughly 40–60ms capped at 900ms total regardless of path count, and Reduce Motion collapses this straight to the final rendered state plus the existing text stage label, never leaving reduce-motion users staring at nothing.

Components I'd bank as the reusable inventory: StatusChip, RejectionReasonCard (icon + label + body + two actions), StageLabel, PathCanvas (the shared Compare/Edit render surface), ToolStrip (fixed, always-visible, disabled-state-aware), PathListRow (name, shape glyph, open/closed + size metadata, with delete/merge exposed as `.accessibilityAction`s directly on the row rather than requiring a VoiceOver swipe-to-reveal, which is friendlier under VoiceOver than hidden swipe actions), CompareControl (wipe handle + segmented fallback, same view, mode-gated as described above), SimplifySlider (debounced), ExportSheet. Accessibility notes I'd add on top of what's already locked: 44pt minimum tap targets throughout the tool strip; Dynamic Type tested at accessibility sizes specifically on the seven rejection cards since that copy has to keep both icon and two actions visible without truncation; and Reduce Transparency needs a defined fallback for the Processing screen's "faintly visible image behind the stage label" treatment — if that's implemented as opacity/blur, Reduce Transparency should swap it for a solid dimmed scrim rather than ignoring the setting.

On external design: I don't think a static-image tool adds much value for the Workspace, the gesture-heavy screens, or the accessibility list fallback — those need to be built straight from this written interaction spec, and a picture of them would understate the real complexity. Where I would use Claude Design is narrowly: generate polished static comps for Recents Home (empty and populated), one Quality Gate rejection card, the Correction Review before/after, and the Trace Workspace in Compare mode at rest — enough to validate the paper-tone/ink/accent system reads as premium before we commit tokens to code, not to dictate interaction. A workable prompt: "Generate iOS app mockups for cleanline, a craft tool that traces hand-drawn paper sketches into vector line art. Warm paper-tone background (#F6F1E7), true-black ink linework as the only saturated element, oxidized teal (#2F6F63) as the sole active-state accent, safety orange (#C1531B) reserved only for warnings/errors, SF Pro typography with wide-tracked small caps for status labels, quiet hairline dividers, no gradients or drop shadows beyond subtle elevation. Show: (1) Recents Home empty state with a large New Trace button, (2) Recents Home with three saved traces as thumbnail rows with status chips, (3) a quality-gate rejection card for 'shadow coverage too high' with icon, message, and Retake/Choose Another actions, (4) a before/after correction review screen with original and corrected sketch side by side, (5) the Trace Workspace in Compare mode showing a wipe-reveal between corrected photo and traced vector result." That gives us a visual-tone check without pretending a static mockup tool can specify gesture ownership, undo semantics, or checkpoint behavior — those have to stay owned by this written spec.

The single detail I don't want lost heading into architecture: the "one canvas, one shared state" promise for Compare/Edit is only real if zoom/pan/selection live in one `TraceSession` object and the gesture recognizers for wipe/pan/select are explicitly mode- and zoom-gated as I described — if that gesture composition gets skipped or hand-waved, we'll ship a workspace where zooming in breaks compare, or comparing breaks selection, and the fix at that point is a rewrite of exactly the thing this whole phase argued hardest to unify.

Claude then came in wearing an engineering-skeptic hat and mostly validated Codex's direction, but pushed it from "design adjectives" toward actual buildable specifics, and in doing so surfaced a few things Codex hasn't weighed in on yet.

The biggest one: gesture ownership on the Trace Workspace canvas. Codex's spec never resolved what happens when wipe-to-compare, tap-to-select, and pinch-to-zoom/pan all want to live on the same canvas — Claude proposed a concrete disambiguation scheme (zoom-gated gesture arming, wipe-handle-only drag, accessible toggle doubling as the sighted answer once zoomed in) that Codex hasn't confirmed or reacted to.

Second: the tracing-stage path-reveal animation. Codex framed it as "real strokes appear as they're computed, with a plain-label fallback if that's not feasible." That needs Codex's sign-off, not just Claude's unilateral call.

Everything else — token-level color/spacing/typography choices, the component inventory, the TraceSession state-ownership model, accessibility requirements — reads as Claude filling in detail underneath Codex's structure rather than contradicting it, but it's still unconfirmed by Codex.

Next round needs Codex to react directly to: the proposed gesture-disambiguation scheme, the more conservative (sequenced-reveal-of-complete-data) version of the trace animation, and to settle the external-design-tool scope question one way or the other so the handoff has one clear answer instead of two drafts.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 2**

Claude’s gesture call is the strongest new contribution in the room, and I think we should lock it instead of leaving “shared canvas” as a slogan. The workspace only earns its keep if it stays predictable when someone is zoomed into a lettering corner at 3x, and that means Compare cannot use an anywhere-on-canvas drag. I’d adopt Claude’s handle-based wipe exactly because it keeps the canvas honest: pinch and pan are always available in both modes, but the compare wipe only arms from a visible handle when you’re at base zoom or very close to it. Once you’re meaningfully zoomed in, the wipe steps aside and the Original/Traced segmented toggle becomes the comparison tool for everyone, not just VoiceOver users. That is better interaction design, not just an accessibility fallback, because it resolves the actual gesture conflict instead of pretending SwiftUI gesture composition will magically feel good. I’d carry the same explicitness into Edit: tap-to-select is only live in Edit mode, never in Compare, and double-tap zoom should not exist in MVP at all. It sounds convenient, but it introduces exactly the tap ambiguity we don’t need. Pinch plus pan is enough.

I also think Claude is right to tighten the tracing reveal promise. “Strokes appear as they’re actually computed” is a beautiful line, but if Vision is effectively single-shot here, then saying that in the handoff bakes in a hidden architecture dependency and violates our own honesty rule. The bolder move is not the riskier claim; it’s the clearer one. I’d specify a sequenced reveal of real completed path data after trace completion, capped tightly so it feels crisp rather than theatrical, with the stage label still doing the primary trust work. If architecture later proves it can stream partial vector output cleanly, great, upgrade it. But the design handoff should promise what the builder can implement without inventing a streaming pipeline just to satisfy a sentence in the spec. On external design, I’m with Claude’s narrower use as well. A full-flow Claude Design prompt for every screen sounds comprehensive, but static comps are the wrong tool for the hardest parts of this app: gesture ownership, zoom continuity, checkpoint recovery, accessible list-based editing. I’d use external design surgically to validate visual tone and layout on the calm, legible screens: Recents empty and populated, one rejection state, Correction Review, Processing at rest, and the Workspace in Compare mode at base zoom. Then the written handoff owns the interactive screens and state logic. That’s the cleanest final direction to me: one rigorous written spec for behavior, plus a small set of premium visual comps to prove the paper/ink/teal system feels as good as we think it does.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Codex, the Correction Review call — swap the side-by-side thumbnails for a plain Original/Corrected segmented switch — is actually the thing I want to point back at when I press you again on the Workspace gesture question, because it's a clean existence proof of the right instinct. You solved "let the user inspect a transformation" on that screen with a discrete, unambiguous control instead of a gesture, and it works precisely because that screen isn't also hosting pinch-zoom and tap-to-select on the same surface. The Workspace doesn't get that luxury — Compare's wipe, Edit's tap-to-select, and zoom/pan all want the same canvas — which is exactly why I don't think "one canvas, two modes" is fully specified yet. You raised the risk yourself: "overdesigning the Compare workspace with fancy gesture logic that becomes fragile." I'd argue the fix isn't to avoid gesture logic, it's to write down the disambiguation rule now so it's disciplined instead of improvised, the same way the segmented switch disciplined Correction Review. My proposal from last round stands — wipe only arms via a visible handle you touch first, tap-to-select only arms below ~1.15x zoom, and above that the accessible Original/Traced toggle becomes the sighted answer too — and I want to add one more constraint I skipped: no double-tap-to-zoom. Photo apps conventionally bind double-tap to zoom, but if we do that here, iOS has to wait out the double-tap recognition window (roughly 300-400ms) before it can commit to treating a single tap as "select this path," which means every path selection in Edit mode gets a perceptible delay. Pinch-only zoom, no double-tap gesture at all, keeps tap-to-select instant. That's a small thing that would be genuinely annoying to discover after the tool strip already ships.

On the trace reveal, I want to firm up my position rather than leave it as "try streaming, here's a fallback." You wrote "if the architecture can emit partial vector data honestly... if it cannot, the design still stands without that flourish" — I get the instinct to keep both doors open, but that phrasing puts a real architecture decision (does the vectorization stage need a producer/AsyncStream shape, or a single atomic call) behind a UI contingency, and contingencies like that have a way of getting resolved by whoever's building the pipeline three weeks from now discovering, late, that VNDetectContoursRequest just doesn't stream. I'd rather this handoff commit to the honest, buildable version now: the reveal renders real, complete path data, sequenced onto the canvas at a short fixed stagger after the trace finishes — not synced to computation, just an honest presentation of a real result. That's still exactly the "no fake progress" principle in your original pitch, it's just not pretending the timing means something it doesn't. If the architecture phase later finds the pipeline can genuinely emit incrementally, that's a strict upgrade to this same feature, not a redesign — but I don't want "maybe it streams" sitting in the handoff as if it's a coin flip someone else resolves.

On external design scope, I think there's actually a real compromise here rather than a fight. You want a comprehensive prompt across the whole screen list plus uploading the locked feature summary and rejection vocabulary so design doesn't have to infer boundaries — I don't disagree with any of that as prep material. Where I pushed back last round was narrower than it may have sounded: I don't think a static image tool should be asked to invent gesture ownership, undo semantics, or checkpoint behavior, because it can't, and if we ask for that we'll get pretty pictures that quietly misrepresent how the thing behaves. So here's the version I'll actually commit to: expand the mockup ask to your full screen list — Recents Home, Intake, Camera Capture, all seven rejection variants, Correction Review, Processing, Workspace in both Compare and Edit, Export sheet, Settings — for visual-tone and token validation, exactly as you described, plus uploading the feature summary and rejection vocabulary as context. But the prompt needs one explicit sentence stating these are static tone references, and that gesture behavior, state transitions, undo/redo semantics, and checkpoint/resume logic are owned by this written spec and not to be inferred from the images. That gives us your comprehensiveness and my worry about interaction fidelity in the same document, and it's a real synthesis, not just me caving.

One thing you introduced that I want to flag before it becomes an inconsistency nobody notices: giving each of the seven rejection reasons "its own icon and accent treatment" runs straight into the rule your own component-inventory paragraph states a few lines later — "status chips never become rainbow clutter" — plus the design-phase rule we already inherited that oxidized teal is the one active accent and safety orange is the one warning accent. If "accent treatment" means seven different colors, one per rejection reason, we've quietly reintroduced a rainbow system through the back door of error states. I think the fix is easy and doesn't lose the recognizability goal you're after: all seven rejection cards share the same single safety-orange treatment — same accent border, same icon frame color — and the recognizability comes from seven distinct icon glyphs (a tilted-frame icon for skew, a sun/shadow icon for shadow coverage, a faint-pencil icon for sketch-too-faint, and so on), not from seven different colors. That's still "visually distinct enough to pattern-match at a glance," which was the actual goal, without breaking the one-accent-plus-one-warning-color rule we already locked.

Last thing, smaller but worth nailing down before it becomes a fuzzy escape hatch during build: the conditional threshold-adjustment slider on Correction Review, gated on "only if product testing proves it materially rescues real traces" — I'd rather that be an explicit build-time flag on a config struct (`let showsManualThresholdSlider: Bool`) evaluated against the fixture-set testing the requirements phase already locked, not a vibes-based call made mid-implementation. Otherwise it's the kind of thing that either never gets removed once someone adds it "just in case," or never gets seriously tried because nobody wants to be the one to add scope. And I'll close by underlining the one place we're in full agreement and I think it's worth writing down explicitly as a locked architectural fact: your line that "the comparison wipe should... operate on the same transformed canvas coordinates as Edit mode" is precisely the single-`TraceSession`-source-of-truth model I described, so between the two of us we've now specified both the state model and the coordinate/gesture contract for the one screen in this app that was genuinely at risk of being underspecified.

On the Workspace canvas, Codex fully adopted Claude's gesture-disambiguation scheme — wipe-to-compare only arms via a visible handle at/near base zoom, tap-to-select only lives in Edit mode, and (this landed independently from both sides) no double-tap-to-zoom at all, since it would introduce a perceptible selection delay in Edit mode for no real benefit.
