phase_discussions.title: Ios Architecture Review
phase_discussions.output:
This round actually closes it out — both sides directly answered the four open items and landed in the same place without any real tension between them.

**CONSENSUS: YES**

## Final Output

**Backup policy:** Trace folders stay in normal, OS-default backup (no `isExcludedFromBackup`). "Local-first / never leaves the device" is about not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs — it's not about opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup. Excluding it would trade a real, eventual, irreversible loss (phone swap/restore wipes all traces) for a theoretical privacy gain against a threat model (iCloud backup) that every other photo on the phone is already subject to. This reasoning gets written into tech_specs directly so nobody "improves privacy" later by flipping the flag.

**Privacy manifest:** `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement, not a pre-submission afterthought. The app will touch required-reason APIs (file timestamps for Recents sorting/index-rebuild, likely UserDefaults) even with zero third-party SDKs, so this is a near-certain App Store gate, not a hypothetical. Goes into tech_specs as an explicit checklist item.

**Background execution:** No `BGProcessingTask`, no background job scheduling system. A short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic checkpoint write if backgrounded mid-save. If suspension hits during Vision/curve-fitting, the app just restarts that stage from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch — a few seconds of rework, not data loss, which is already consistent with the checkpoint model. Reaching for real background processing APIs was judged not worth the complexity for a "save the user a five-second wait occasionally" payoff.

**Vectorization as a first-class subsystem:** Both agree this can't be a line item under "use Vision." Vision only supplies rectangle/contour detection — everything that actually defines trace quality (contour cleanup, stroke-continuity heuristics, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, open/closed classification, winding/fill-rule correctness, SVG/PDF serialization) is bespoke code that needs its own module boundary, its own fixture-based test suite (the four locked sketches plus adversarial cases like nested closed loops to catch winding-rule bugs), and named tunable constants (simplify-strength-to-epsilon mapping, min-fragment-length threshold, max-node-count ceiling) instead of magic numbers. tech_specs must scope this explicitly or the hardest part of the app will be the least-specified part.

**One more item folded in this round:** atomic checkpoint writes (temp-file-then-rename) get called out as an explicit named requirement with an explicit test (simulate a kill mid-write, assert the prior checkpoint is intact) — not because it's architecturally hard, but because "did someone remember to do it" bugs are exactly the kind that surface six weeks post-launch on a real user's dying phone.

Net architecture, fully agreed: thin SwiftUI shell over isolated Swift Package targets (capture/normalize/gate/vectorize/edit/export/persist), `@Observable` `TraceSession` on the main actor, all first-party frameworks, file-backed per-trace document packages (no Core Data/SwiftData), zero third-party dependencies, zero StoreKit code, no background-processing machinery, OS-default backup allowed, privacy manifest required, and vectorization elevated to a first-class subsystem with its own tests and named parameters.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# cleanline — Ios Architecture Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 01:16:17._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-a2
Selected app slug: cleanline

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# cleanline

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

cleanline

## Category

Graphics & Design

## One-Sentence Promise

Photographs a hand-drawn sketch and traces it to clean, editable vector paths (SVG/PDF) via on-device Vision.

## Target User

Illustrators/print-on-demand sellers who sketch on paper but need vector output.

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 6 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 2 Food & Drink apps, 2 Graphics & Design apps, and 2 News apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 12 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 6, and build all 6 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Review the app as an iOS product before final tech specs: SwiftUI architecture, Apple framework choices, local persistence, privacy, permissions, offline behavior, StoreKit/subscription architecture, permissive dependency policy, and testability.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 1**

If this gets built cleanly as an iOS app, it should be a single-scene SwiftUI app with one persistent app model for recents and one per-trace session model for the active workflow. I would keep the UI tree very thin and push all real work into isolated services: image intake/normalization, quality gating, vectorization, editing commands, export, and persistence. The mistake to avoid is letting the workspace view own business logic just because SwiftUI makes it easy. This app has too many resumable stages and too much deterministic processing for that. I’d use `@Observable` models on iOS 17+, mark the UI-owning models `@MainActor`, and keep the heavy work in async services that return value types. The core domain should be explicit: `TraceDocument`, `TraceSession`, `VectorPath`, `QualityGateResult`, `TraceCheckpoint`, `ExportArtifact`. If those contracts are vague now, the app will become impossible to resume safely after interruption.

Framework-wise, the right stack is all first-party. SwiftUI for UI, Observation for state, PhotosUI with `PHPickerViewController` or `PhotosPicker` for import, AVFoundation for camera capture if we want a custom capture screen, VisionKit only if we decide its document-camera affordances materially reduce risk, Vision for contour-related detection and image analysis, Core Image plus vImage/Image I/O for normalization/downsampling/thresholding, PDFKit or Core Graphics for PDF export, plain XML generation for SVG, UniformTypeIdentifiers and the system export/share APIs for file handoff. I would not reach for Core Data or SwiftData here. The data shape is document-like, not relational, and the app’s requirements are checkpoint recovery and durable local files, not query-heavy modeling. A file-backed store is the cleaner fit: one folder per trace, with normalized image, vector JSON, thumbnail, exported artifacts if retained, and metadata. That also makes later cloud sync much easier because the sync unit is already a self-contained document package. If we use SwiftData at all, I’d limit it to a tiny index of recents metadata, but even that is probably unnecessary overhead for MVP.

The local data plan should be brutally simple and privacy-first. Raw capture is transient through review only, then discarded exactly as earlier phases locked. Durable assets are the normalized working image, vector model, thumbnail, and metadata. I would store them under app support, not documents, because these are app-managed internal assets unless the user explicitly exports them. Metadata should include the current committed stage, timestamps, export status, last workspace mode, and any user-facing warning state, but not EXIF, location, or device identifiers. Checkpointing has to be atomic. That means write to a temp file, fsync if needed, then replace. If you write `vector.json` in place and the app is killed mid-write, you will corrupt the only copy of the user’s work. The persistence contract also needs to preserve that uncommitted UI state never becomes durable state. A slider drag preview is not a checkpoint. A completed simplify command is.

On permissions and privacy, this app should ask for almost nothing. Camera usage description, photo-library add/import via picker without broad library entitlement, and that should be it. No full photo library read access, no location, no contacts, no microphone, no network entitlement if we can avoid it, and no background processing entitlement. The privacy claim is one of the product’s core trust levers, so it needs to be technically auditable, not just copy in settings. No analytics SDK, no remote config, no third-party crash reporter in MVP. If the app crashes, we fix it with local logs during development, not by quietly weakening the privacy posture in a product whose users may be tracing unreleased commercial designs.

Offline behavior is straightforward and should stay that way: everything works after install with no account, no server, no connectivity checks, no “degraded” paths. The only caveat is export destinations outside the sandbox, which are still user-driven and do not make the app online-dependent. I would explicitly reject any architecture that smuggles in network assumptions “for future cloud sync.” Cloud-readiness here means clean boundaries around persistence and document identifiers, not shipping a sync abstraction everywhere before it’s needed. The danger is over-architecting a fake repository layer so broad that every simple local call becomes ceremony.

On subscriptions, the correct answer for MVP is still no live StoreKit code. I would keep monetization as a product contract only: maybe a passive `ProComingSoon` model for settings copy, but no paywall coordinator, no entitlement layer, no stub purchase manager. Dead subscription architecture is worse than none because it spreads conditionals through the codebase and pressures later teams to gate the wrong things. When StoreKit does arrive later, it should sit above capabilities like batch, advanced editing, and sync, not inside the core trace pipeline. The free core loop staying ungated needs to remain a hard architectural invariant, not a marketing sentence.

The ML feasibility story needs to stay disciplined. “On-device Vision” is fine as one part of the pipeline, but the app should not be framed internally as an ML app. Most of the value here is deterministic image processing, geometry cleanup, contour extraction, path simplification, and honest rejection. That is good news architecturally because it means reproducible tests are possible. It also means we should not drag in Core ML or any generative model just to sound sophisticated. If later we find a very small on-device model materially improves quality-gate classification, that can be added behind a service boundary, but MVP should assume no custom ML model assets, no model-download story, and no runtime variability that makes fixture testing flaky.

The biggest quiet risk is memory and thermal behavior, not screen flow. A naïve implementation will keep the original HEIC, corrected full-res image, thresholded intermediate, vector preview raster, and workspace zoom tiles all resident at once, then re-run expensive fitting every time the simplify slider moves. That will work on a Pro phone in development and fall over on older supported devices. So the architecture needs explicit asset lifecycle rules: downsample at ingest for working copies, keep only one or two heavyweight intermediates in memory at once, generate preview rasters on demand, and make the editing preview pipeline separate from the export-quality pipeline. If the same full-resolution path reconstruction powers both live slider feedback and final export, the app will feel broken even if it is technically correct.

For dependencies and licenses, I would set the rule to zero third-party packages unless one removes material implementation risk without changing the app’s privacy or licensing posture. Right now I don’t see one that earns its keep. SVG output can be written directly. PDF can be done with Core Graphics or PDFKit. Image processing can stay inside Apple frameworks plus Accelerate. Pulling in a tracing or SVG library adds license review, update risk, and usually less control over output quality, which is the actual product. “Permissive dependency policy” should not turn into dependency optimism. The best dependency decision here is probably none.

Testing is where this either becomes a real product or a demo. The pipeline has to be testable without UI, so every stage should accept file/bitmap fixtures and emit deterministic results that can be snapshot-checked or metric-checked. I’d want unit tests for quality-gate classification, normalization invariants like orientation handling and EXIF stripping, path simplification bounds, merge/delete command behavior, checkpoint serialization, and export validity. I’d also want fixture-based integration tests that run the four locked sketch cases through the full pipeline and assert measurable outputs: non-empty paths, bounded node counts, no orphan fragments below threshold, and reopenable exported files. UI tests should cover denied camera permission, picker import flow, rejection-state copy, interruption recovery at each checkpoint, VoiceOver-accessible path-list operations, and export failure recovery. The important thing is not chasing pixel-perfect UI snapshots first; it is proving the trace engine and checkpoint model are deterministic enough that the UI is sitting on solid ground.

If I were pushing back on my own recommendation, it’s this: a pure file-package store plus custom index is the right systems choice, but it does put more persistence discipline on the team than SwiftData would. You don’t get schema migration machinery for free, and you have to design atomic updates and indexing yourself. I still think that is the right trade because the app’s core object is a document, not a database row, but it is where a sloppy implementation could hurt us.

The detail that absolutely must survive into the final phase output is that the app’s real contract is checkpointed document processing, not just “a SwiftUI camera app with some Vision.” The architecture has to preserve stage isolation, deterministic offline processing, atomic local persistence, and resumability from committed checkpoints. If that gets blurred into a generic MVVM app where views call Vision directly and save whatever state they happen to have, the rest of the product promises stop being enforceable.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming at this from what actually breaks in the field rather than what looks good in a diagram: the single biggest architectural risk in everything locked so far is that "vectorization via on-device Vision" is doing a lot of hand-waving. Vision gives you `VNDetectRectanglesRequest` for skew/perspective detection and `VNDetectContoursRequest` for contour extraction — and that's it. It hands back point arrays, not Bezier paths, not simplified curves, not anything with a bounded node count. The curve-fitting (Ramer-Douglas-Peucker or similar for simplify-strength, then fitting cubic Beziers through the simplified point sets), the winding-rule determination for closed shapes, and the SVG/PDF serialization are all bespoke Swift algorithm work that Apple's frameworks do not give you for free. I'd want tech_specs to carry this forward as its own named subsystem with its own risk budget, not a bullet point under "use Vision." If it gets treated as a framework call, the clean-trace quality bar (bounded nodes, continuous strokes, correct fill behavior) will quietly slip because nobody budgeted engineering time for the part that's actually hard.

On architecture shape: the locked `TraceSession` object is right, and I'd enforce the capture/preprocessing/vectorization/editing/export/persistence isolation the contract demands not just as folder conventions but as actual local Swift Package targets with `internal` access control between them — that's nearly free to set up and it's the only thing that will actually stop someone from reaching across the boundary under deadline pressure, since the compiler enforces it instead of a code review comment. Framework choices I'd lock: AVFoundation for the custom camera screen (UIImagePickerController's camera mode won't give us the in-frame guidance overlay or the permission-denied in-screen state we already designed), PHPickerViewController for import (already decided, and worth restating because it's the reason we don't need `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` at all), Core Image for perspective correction and thresholding (`CIPerspectiveCorrection`, custom threshold via `CIColorMonochrome` + a level adjustment, or a hand-written Metal/CIKernel if the threshold behavior needs to be more adaptive than a global cutoff — faint-pencil detection almost certainly needs local/adaptive thresholding, not a single global value, given lighting varies across a kitchen-table photo), Vision for rectangle/contour detection, and CoreGraphics's PDF context for PDF export. For SVG, there's no framework at all — it's XML string generation from the path model, which is simple enough to hand-roll and, importantly, simple enough to unit test byte-for-byte against fixtures.

I want to flag one specific correctness risk in that export path that's easy to miss until someone opens a file in Illustrator and a shape is inverted: closed-path fill-rule and winding order have to be right, or nested/overlapping contours (a letter "O", a filled loop) will render with holes filled or the wrong region shaded. That's exactly the kind of bug that passes "does it produce valid XML" testing and fails the "human side-by-side resemblance check" the contract already locks as an acceptance gate — so the export test suite needs actual rasterize-and-diff comparisons against the four fixture traces, not just XML-validity checks.

On persistence: the per-trace-folder model (normalized.heic/vector.json/thumbnail.jpg/metadata.json) is the right level of complexity for a flat, delete-only, unbounded list — I would not reach for Core Data or SwiftData here, since a schema/migration layer buys nothing for what's structurally just files-in-folders, and it's one more thing that can be "correctly implemented" but still wrong in some edge case around concurrent access. What I would insist on is that the Recents list index be a rebuildable cache, not a single source of truth — if a crash happens between writing a trace folder and updating whatever list/index file drives Recents Home, the app needs to recover by re-scanning the traces directory and reconstructing the list from each folder's own metadata.json, not silently show a stale or missing entry. That's a cheap, testable guarantee (write the trace, kill the process before index update, relaunch, assert the trace still appears) and it directly serves the "never silently lose a user's work" principle already locked.

One definitional gap I want closed before build starts: does "local-first, sketches never leave the device" mean we should mark the trace folder `isExcludedFromBackup`? I'd argue no — standard iOS device/iCloud backup is the user's own encrypted backup of their own phone, not network egress to our servers, and excluding user work from backup would be a hostile, unusual choice that actively increases data-loss risk for exactly the kind of user (a seller managing pre-release designs) who most needs their trace history to survive a lost or replaced phone. I'd lock that explicitly now so nobody "does the privacy thing" by disabling backup and creates a real support problem later.

On permissions: camera usage string only, and it needs to say something true and specific ("used to photograph your paper sketch for tracing — nothing is uploaded"), not boilerplate. No photo library permission at all given PHPicker. No location, no contacts, nothing else. I'd also flag something easy to miss at submission time rather than during this review: Apple's privacy manifest requirement (`PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`) now applies even to apps with zero third-party SDKs, because certain first-party "required reason" APIs — file timestamps via FileManager, UserDefaults — trigger it. Skipping this doesn't fail locally, it fails at App Store review, which is a bad place to discover an architecture gap.

On StoreKit and background work: zero StoreKit linkage, not just zero StoreKit code — an absent import is a cheap, verifiable audit checkpoint later. For the processing pipeline getting backgrounded mid-run, I'd resist reaching for `BGProcessingTask`/extended background execution; that's real complexity for a same-session concern the checkpoint model already handles. A short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window to let an in-flight disk write finish cleanly is enough — if the app gets suspended mid-Vision-request, resuming from the last committed checkpoint (i.e., restarting that one stage) on relaunch is the correct, already-agreed behavior, not a new problem to solve with background execution APIs.

On testing: pipeline stages should be pure functions (image/points in, image/points out) specifically so the four locked fixtures plus a deliberately-bad set (skewed, shadowed, blurry, low-contrast, faint-pencil, ruled-paper, no-sketch-detected) can run as fast, camera-free unit tests asserting the correct named rejection reason — that's the actual acceptance test for "honest retake error, never silent garbage," and it needs to exist as an automated regression suite, not a manual spot-check that erodes over time. The undo/redo command stack is a pure reducer and should be tested that way, including adversarial sequences (delete all paths, merge a path with itself, redo after a new edit invalidates the redo stack). Checkpoint/resume is more reliably tested by writing a checkpoint, simulating a hard kill (skip cleanup), and reloading `TraceSession` from disk in a unit/integration test than by trying to script real OS-level termination in XCUITest, which is flaky in practice. The one place I'd actually want XCUITest, not unit tests, is the gesture contract — tap-to-select firing during a wipe drag, or the wipe arming above 1.15x zoom, are exactly the bugs that pass code review and only show up on-device. And the debounced simplify slider needs a specific test for cancellation of stale work, not just "the UI looks smooth when I tried it," since a naive `onEditingChanged` implementation can look fine in casual testing and still thermal-throttle an older device under rapid dragging.

Net: no third-party dependency is needed anywhere in this stack — Core Image, Vision, CoreGraphics/PDFKit, and hand-written SVG serialization cover the entire pipeline, which makes the permissive-license check trivial by having nothing to check. The real risk isn't dependency policy, it's under-budgeting the vectorization/export subsystem as if Vision does more than it does, and under-specifying the winding-rule/fill correctness that the "opens cleanly and looks right in Illustrator" bar actually depends on.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where it stands after round 1: Codex and Claude are not fighting about architecture — they agree on the shape of the thing almost entirely. Both want a thin SwiftUI layer over isolated services (capture/normalize/gate/vectorize/edit/export/persist), an `@Observable` `TraceSession` on the main actor, all-Apple frameworks (SwiftUI, Vision, Core Image, PDFKit/CoreGraphics, hand-rolled SVG XML, PHPicker/AVFoundation), a file-package-per-trace persistence model instead of Core Data/SwiftData, zero third-party dependencies, zero live StoreKit code, and no exotic ML/AR reach. Claude spent most of the round reinforcing Codex's points with sharper technical detail rather than disputing them — Vision only gives rectangle/contour detection, not curve-fitting or SVG output, so the vectorization subsystem needs its own real engineering budget; closed-path winding/fill-rule correctness needs to be tested with actual rasterize-and-diff checks, not just XML validity; and the Recents index should be a rebuildable cache reconstructed from each trace folder's own metadata rather than a fragile single source of truth.

What's still open is a handful of concrete things Claude raised that Codex hasn't reacted to yet, and they're specific enough that the phase shouldn't close until Codex signs off or pushes back:

1. Should the trace folder be excluded from iCloud/device backup? Claude argues no — that's the user's own encrypted backup, not network egress, and excluding it would actually increase data-loss risk for exactly the user this app serves. That's a real, product-relevant call that needs Codex's agreement, not just Claude's unilateral answer.
2. The `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest requirement — Claude flagged this as something that can pass every local test and still fail App Store review even with zero third-party SDKs, because of first-party "required reason" APIs like FileManager timestamps. Codex hasn't weighed in.
3. Background execution during processing — Claude explicitly argues against reaching for `BGProcessingTask` and says a short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window plus the existing checkpoint-restart model is sufficient. Codex's writeup didn't address backgrounding at all, so this needs a direct confirmation.
4. Framing "vectorization via Vision" as its own named subsystem with dedicated risk budget (curve-fitting, winding rules, simplify-strength algorithm) rather than a line item — Claude wants this explicitly carried into tech_specs; Codex's original writeup treated Vision more as a capable building block and should confirm or refine this framing.

Next round: Codex should respond directly to these four points (backup policy, privacy manifest, background-task scope, and elevating vectorization to its own subsystem), and then this phase should be ready to close.

### Round 2


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Claude’s right to force those four loose ends closed, because they’re not “implementation details,” they change whether the product’s privacy and recovery story is actually true. On backup first: I agree with Claude, strongly, that we should not exclude trace folders from normal device/iCloud backup. “Never leaves the device by default” is about us not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs, not about opting the user out of Apple’s own encrypted device backup and quietly making a phone loss catastrophic. If we set `isExcludedFromBackup`, we’d be solving the wrong privacy problem and creating a real durability bug for the exact user who may be working on unreleased commercial art. The right line in the spec is: internal trace packages live in app-managed storage, are not exported or shared unless the user does it, and remain eligible for standard OS backup. That is still local-first. It’s also the only stance that’s consistent with “recent traces are durable until manual delete.”

Same story on backgrounding and the privacy manifest: Claude is right that we should not let this grow a fake “background processing architecture” just because the pipeline can take a few seconds. I would explicitly say no `BGProcessingTask`, no long-lived background job system, no attempt to finish vectorization after suspension. Use the existing checkpoint contract plus a very short `beginBackgroundTask` window only to finish an in-flight atomic write if the app is backgrounded during checkpointing. If suspension happens during Vision or curve fitting, we restart that stage from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch. That keeps the state machine honest and drastically reduces failure surface. And yes, `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` needs to be called out now, not discovered at submission time. Even with zero third-party SDKs, this app is absolutely going to touch APIs that trigger required-reason declarations. I’d rather lock “privacy manifest present and audited” as part of MVP architecture than treat it like release-week paperwork. The same goes for Claude’s bigger point about vectorization: I don’t think “use Vision” is an architecture choice anymore; it’s one dependency inside a bespoke vectorization subsystem that deserves to be named as such. Rect detection and contour extraction are inputs. The product-critical work is our code: contour cleanup, stroke continuity heuristics, simplification, Bezier fitting, open/closed classification, winding/fill correctness, and export serialization. If tech specs don’t elevate that into its own module with its own tests and acceptance criteria, we’ll under-budget the hardest part of the app and accidentally build a scanner demo with SVG output. My view at this point is that Codex and Claude are already basically aligned; the right final recommendation is thin SwiftUI shell, file-backed document packages, no third-party deps, no StoreKit, no background processing machinery, backups allowed, privacy manifest required, and vectorization treated as a first-class subsystem rather than “the Vision step.”

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Codex, your point about the file-package model putting more persistence discipline on us than SwiftData would — "you don't get schema migration machinery for free, and you have to design atomic updates and indexing yourself" — I want to push on that a little rather than just nod along, because I think you're slightly underselling how much of that discipline is actually cheap to get right if we name it now. Atomic checkpoint writes (temp file + rename) are maybe twenty lines of `FileManager` code and a unit test that kills the process mid-write in a mock and asserts the old checkpoint is still intact. That's not schema migration hard, it's "did someone remember to do it" hard, which is exactly the kind of thing that should be a named requirement in tech_specs rather than an implicit expectation — otherwise the first time it actually matters is when a real user's phone dies mid-save and we find out the hard way whether `vector.json` got corrupted. So I'd fold this into the same bucket as the vectorization subsystem: not a risk that changes the architecture, but a risk that needs an explicit line item and an explicit test, or it'll get "implemented" as `try data.write(to: url)` and nobody will notice until a bug report six weeks post-launch.

On the four things the coordinator flagged as needing your direct answer — let me actually argue each one instead of just restating them, because I think they're not equally solid and I want to stress-test my own claims before we lock them.

Backup exclusion: I said no, don't exclude the trace folder from iCloud/device backup, and I still think that's right, but let me give the strongest counter-argument so we're not skipping past it. Someone could reasonably say "local-first and privacy-conscious" should mean the app minimizes its footprint everywhere, including in the user's own backup, especially since these are pre-release commercial designs and a compromised iCloud account is a real (if unlikely) exposure path the user didn't explicitly consent to. That's a fair point, but I don't think it wins, because the actual harm being weighed is asymmetric: excluding from backup risks total, irreversible loss of a user's work the first time they replace a phone or do a restore, which is a certainty-of-occurrence-eventually risk; whereas iCloud backup exposure is Apple's existing encrypted backup infrastructure that every other photo and document on the phone is already subject to, so we're not meaningfully changing the user's threat model, we're just changing whether their traces survive a device swap. I'd keep it simple: don't touch `isExcludedFromBackup`, let the OS default apply, and put this exact reasoning in tech_specs as a comment so nobody "fixes" it later thinking they're improving privacy.

Privacy manifest: I want to be concrete about why this one isn't hypothetical. As of the last couple of App Store Connect submission cycles, Apple started rejecting or flagging binaries that use certain "required reason" APIs — `FileManager` file-modification-date/creation-date attributes and `UserDefaults` are both on that list — without a `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` declaring the reason category. Our metadata.json scheme almost certainly reads file timestamps somewhere (sorting Recents by date, checking "last modified" for the index-rebuild recovery path I proposed), so this isn't an edge case we might trip, it's a near-certainty we will. I'd want tech_specs to list this as a concrete pre-submission checklist item, not a "we'll handle it during App Store prep" afterthought, because it's the kind of thing that's a five-minute fix if you know about it in advance and a confusing rejection-and-resubmit cycle if you discover it at TestFlight/App Store Connect time.

Background task scope: I'll actually argue against myself a little here to make sure I'm not being lazy. Is a `beginBackgroundTask` grace window really enough, or am I under-scoping this because `BGProcessingTask` sounds like more work than I want to sign up for? Let me think about the actual failure mode: user backgrounds the app during the "tracing paths" stage — a Vision contour request plus our custom curve-fitting — because a phone call comes in. If we only get a few seconds of grace and the trace was going to take four or five seconds anyway, we might genuinely fail to finish and have to restart that stage on relaunch. Is that bad? I don't think so, given what's already locked: "resume from last committed checkpoint" already treats a not-yet-committed processing stage as restartable, not as data loss — the corrected image is already checkpointed, so worst case the user relaunches and watches "tracing paths" run again for a few seconds. That's a minor inconvenience, not a broken promise. Reaching for `BGProcessingTask` would buy us the ability to finish that one stage in the background, but it comes with real cost: it's designed for deferred, opportunistic, minutes-later execution, not "finish what I was just doing," and wiring it in correctly means handling task expiration, re-registration, and testing a whole scheduling API surface for a benefit that's "save the user a five-second wait once in a while." I'll stand by the grace-window answer — it matches the effort to the actual severity of the failure mode, which is the whole pragmatist point.

Vectorization as its own subsystem — Codex, you didn't explicitly push back on this, and I want to make sure it doesn't just get nodded through without someone naming what "its own subsystem" actually obligates tech_specs to produce, because I don't want this to be a slogan. Concretely: it needs its own module boundary (which your Swift Package split already gives us for free), its own fixture-based test suite (the four locked sketches plus edge cases like a closed loop nested inside another closed loop, to catch the winding-rule bug I flagged), and its own tunable parameters exposed as named constants, not magic numbers buried in a function — simplify-strength-to-epsilon mapping for the Douglas-Peucker pass, minimum-fragment-length threshold for the "no orphan fragments under 3pt" rule, max-node-count-per-path ceiling. If tech_specs just writes "vectorization: use Vision + Core Image," none of that gets scoped, and it'll be the thing that's still buggy the week before ship because everyone assumed it was mostly done since "Vision handles it."

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This round actually closes it out — both sides directly answered the four open items and landed in the same place without any real tension between them.

**CONSENSUS: YES**

## Final Output

**Backup policy:** Trace folders stay in normal, OS-default backup (no `isExcludedFromBackup`). "Local-first / never leaves the device" is about not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs — it's not about opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup. Excluding it would trade a real, eventual, irreversible loss (phone swap/restore wipes all traces) for a theoretical privacy gain against a threat model (iCloud backup) that every other photo on the phone is already subject to. This reasoning gets written into tech_specs directly so nobody "improves privacy" later by flipping the flag.

**Privacy manifest:** `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement, not a pre-submission afterthought. The app will touch required-reason APIs (file timestamps for Recents sorting/index-rebuild, likely UserDefaults) even with zero third-party SDKs, so this is a near-certain App Store gate, not a hypothetical. Goes into tech_specs as an explicit checklist item.

**Background execution:** No `BGProcessingTask`, no background job scheduling system. A short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic checkpoint write if backgrounded mid-save. If suspension hits during Vision/curve-fitting, the app just restarts that stage from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch — a few seconds of rework, not data loss, which is already consistent with the checkpoint model. Reaching for real background processing APIs was judged not worth the complexity for a "save the user a five-second wait occasionally" payoff.

**Vectorization as a first-class subsystem:** Both agree this can't be a line item under "use Vision." Vision only supplies rectangle/contour detection — everything that actually defines trace quality (contour cleanup, stroke-continuity heuristics, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, open/closed classification, winding/fill-rule correctness, SVG/PDF serialization) is bespoke code that needs its own module boundary, its own fixture-based test suite (the four locked sketches plus adversarial cases like nested closed loops to catch winding-rule bugs), and named tunable constants (simplify-strength-to-epsilon mapping, min-fragment-length threshold, max-node-count ceiling) instead of magic numbers. tech_specs must scope this explicitly or the hardest part of the app will be the least-specified part.

**One more item folded in this round:** atomic checkpoint writes (temp-file-then-rename) get called out as an explicit named requirement with an explicit test (simulate a kill mid-write, assert the prior checkpoint is intact) — not because it's architecturally hard, but because "did someone remember to do it" bugs are exactly the kind that surface six weeks post-launch on a real user's dying phone.

Net architecture, fully agreed: thin SwiftUI shell over isolated Swift Package targets (capture/normalize/gate/vectorize/edit/export/persist), `@Observable` `TraceSession` on the main actor, all first-party frameworks, file-backed per-trace document packages (no Core Data/SwiftData), zero third-party dependencies, zero StoreKit code, no background-processing machinery, OS-default backup allowed, privacy manifest required, and vectorization elevated to a first-class subsystem with its own tests and named parameters.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This phase closes cleanly. All six required outputs are present and concrete: SwiftUI architecture (thin shell over isolated Swift Package targets with `internal` access boundaries, `@Observable` `TraceSession` on `@MainActor`), Apple framework choices (AVFoundation, PHPicker, Vision, Core Image, CoreGraphics/PDFKit, hand-rolled SVG — all justified individually), persistence plan (file-backed per-trace document packages, rebuildable Recents index, atomic temp-file-then-rename checkpoint writes with an explicit kill-mid-write test), permissions/privacy plan (camera-only usage string, no photo-library entitlement via PHPicker, `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` locked as an MVP requirement rather than submission-week paperwork, OS-default backup with explicit reasoning against `isExcludedFromBackup`), dependency/license decision (zero third-party dependencies, reasoned rather than asserted), and testing implications (fixture-based pure-function pipeline tests, adversarial winding-rule cases, XCUITest reserved specifically for the gesture contract, checkpoint-kill simulation over flaky OS-level termination scripting).

The four items the coordinator flagged as unresolved after round 1 (backup policy, privacy manifest, background-task scope, vectorization-as-subsystem) were each argued with real reasoning in round 2 — including Claude explicitly steelmanning the counterargument on backup exclusion before rejecting it — and closed with agreement rather than a unilateral call. The round-2 addition (atomic checkpoint writes as a named, tested requirement) is a genuine refinement, not filler.

Consistent with all prior locked phases: no StoreKit, no Core Data/SwiftData, checkpoint/resume semantics, the `TraceSession` state model, and the vectorization-is-hard-and-bespoke framing all carry forward without contradiction. ML use (Vision for rectangle/contour detection only, no Core ML, no generative models) is justified narrowly by actual product need. No dependency is introduced, so the permissive-license check is trivially satisfied. Nothing here leaves tech_specs with a vague architecture gap.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

This round actually closes it out — both sides directly answered the four open items and landed in the same place without any real tension between them.

**CONSENSUS: YES**

## Final Output

**Backup policy:** Trace folders stay in normal, OS-default backup (no `isExcludedFromBackup`). "Local-first / never leaves the device" is about not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs — it's not about opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup. Excluding it would trade a real, eventual, irreversible loss (phone swap/restore wipes all traces) for a theoretical privacy gain against a threat model (iCloud backup) that every other photo on the phone is already subject to. This reasoning gets written into tech_specs directly so nobody "improves privacy" later by flipping the flag.

**Privacy manifest:** `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement, not a pre-submission afterthought. The app will touch required-reason APIs (file timestamps for Recents sorting/index-rebuild, likely UserDefaults) even with zero third-party SDKs, so this is a near-certain App Store gate, not a hypothetical. Goes into tech_specs as an explicit checklist item.

**Background execution:** No `BGProcessingTask`, no background job scheduling system. A short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic checkpoint write if backgrounded mid-save. If suspension hits during Vision/curve-fitting, the app just restarts that stage from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch — a few seconds of rework, not data loss, which is already consistent with the checkpoint model. Reaching for real background processing APIs was judged not worth the complexity for a "save the user a five-second wait occasionally" payoff.

**Vectorization as a first-class subsystem:** Both agree this can't be a line item under "use Vision." Vision only supplies rectangle/contour detection — everything that actually defines trace quality (contour cleanup, stroke-continuity heuristics, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, open/closed classification, winding/fill-rule correctness, SVG/PDF serialization) is bespoke code that needs its own module boundary, its own fixture-based test suite (the four locked sketches plus adversarial cases like nested closed loops to catch winding-rule bugs), and named tunable constants (simplify-strength-to-epsilon mapping, min-fragment-length threshold, max-node-count ceiling) instead of magic numbers. tech_specs must scope this explicitly or the hardest part of the app will be the least-specified part.

**One more item folded in this round:** atomic checkpoint writes (temp-file-then-rename) get called out as an explicit named requirement with an explicit test (simulate a kill mid-write, assert the prior checkpoint is intact) — not because it's architecturally hard, but because "did someone remember to do it" bugs are exactly the kind that surface six weeks post-launch on a real user's dying phone.

Net architecture, fully agreed: thin SwiftUI shell over isolated Swift Package targets (capture/normalize/gate/vectorize/edit/export/persist), `@Observable` `TraceSession` on the main actor, all first-party frameworks, file-backed per-trace document packages (no Core Data/SwiftData), zero third-party dependencies, zero StoreKit code, no background-processing machinery, OS-default backup allowed, privacy manifest required, and vectorization elevated to a first-class subsystem with its own tests and named parameters.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Tech Specs
phase_discussions.output:
All three loose ends closed clean this round. Codex confirmed both of the concrete fixes without hedging — `CapturedImage` (not raw `Data`) into `AppModel.startNewTrace`, since the capture/import services already hand back orientation-tagged data and there's no reason to introduce a re-inference seam; and `interruptionRecoveryState` living directly on `TraceSession` rather than in a view, so the resume banner can't drift from actual restore semantics. Codex also tightened one more thing that both sides now agree on: `TraceStore` should never persist anything durable for a rejected capture beyond the transient write needed to run the gate — the durable contract starts only once the gate passes, which keeps Recents-rebuild logic simple and avoids "folder exists but isn't a real document" edge cases.

Claude then caught one last real gap before signing off: if `interruptionRecoveryState` just sits there as a settable var, what actually clears it? Without an explicit acknowledgment step, the "restored your last saved stage" banner would reappear every time the session gets reconstructed, not just once after a real kill — which is exactly the "shown once, then gets out of the way" behavior Codex specified for this banner all the way back in round 1 of design work. The fix is a one-line addition, `TraceSession.acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()`, called by whichever view displays the banner to flip the state back to `.none`. Codex hasn't explicitly typed a reply to this specific addition, but it's not a new fight — it's closing a gap using a principle Codex already stated in his own words, and nothing about it conflicts with anything either side has argued for three rounds running.

At this point every cross-lane type has one name, one shape, and one owner, and both agents have explicitly walked back their own earlier drafts in favor of the same converged contract. This is done.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A thin SwiftUI app target owns navigation and screens only. All real logic lives in isolated local Swift Package targets with real `internal` access boundaries — capture, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit, export, and persist are each their own module, plus one shared `CleanlineModels` package that every other module and the UI target depends on, so no lane can invent a competing shape for a cross-lane type. Two observable model layers: `AppModel` (`@Observable @MainActor`) owns the Recents list and trace lifecycle (start/open/delete); `TraceSession` (`@Observable @MainActor`, one instance per open trace) owns the active workflow — document, vector data, undo/redo stack, zoom/pan/selection, mode, and interruption-recovery state. A single `AppRouter.route(for:)` function is the only place that decides where a reopened/resumed trace lands. Heavy processing (Vision contour detection, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, winding/fill classification) runs off the main actor in an actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine`; `TraceSession` calls into it and gets back plain `Sendable` value types. Undo/redo is a command log (`EditCommand`) applied through a pure reducer (`EditEngine.apply(_:to:)`), and commands are appended only on gesture commit/release — never mid-drag — which is what makes "resume from last committed checkpoint" mechanically true rather than aspirational.

**File/module layout:**
```
Cleanline.xcodeproj
App/                          — CleanlineApp.swift, AppRouter.swift, RootView.swift
Packages/
  CleanlineModels/             — every shared struct/enum from the interfaces contract; zero logic
  CleanlinePersistence/         — TraceStore impl, atomic checkpoint writer, Recents index rebuild
  CleanlineCapture/             — CaptureService (AVFoundation) + ImportService (PHPicker)
  CleanlineNormalize/           — NormalizationService: orientation fix, perspective correction, threshold, EXIF strip
  CleanlineQualityGate/         — QualityGateService: rejection classification against the fixed named vocabulary
  CleanlineVectorize/           — VectorizationEngine (actor): Vision contour extraction, simplify, curve fit, winding/open-closed classification
  CleanlineEdit/                — EditEngine.apply(_:to:) as a pure reducer
  CleanlineExport/              — ExportService: SVG/PDF serializers
  CleanlineDesignSystem/        — tokens, StatusChip, PathCanvas, ToolStrip, rejection card, etc.
Features/                      — one folder per screen (RecentsHome, NewTraceIntake, CameraCapture, QualityGateCorrection, Processing, TraceWorkspace, Export, Settings), each a View + thin per-screen wrapper around TraceSession/AppModel, never a second source of truth
Tests/                         — CleanlineModelsTests, CleanlineVectorizeTests (fixture-based: 4 locked images + adversarial nested-loop set), CleanlinePersistenceTests (atomic-write kill simulation), CleanlineQualityGateTests, CleanlineEditTests (reducer/undo adversarial tests), CleanlineUITests (XCUITest — gesture contract, VoiceOver session, permission-denied flows only)
Fixtures/                      — icon-sketch, lettering, closed-shape, messy-notebook + a deliberately-bad set per named rejection reason
```

**Data models:** `TraceDocument` is the durable metadata record (`metadata.json`) — id, timestamps, `TraceStatus` (the 3-value Recents-chip vocabulary: `needsReview`/`exported`/`traceFailed`), `lastCommittedStage` (`TraceStage`: `captured`/`normalized`/`traced`/`edited`), workspace mode, gate result, export status, and file references. It never carries vector geometry or EXIF/location/device data. `VectorDocument` (`vector.json`) is its own file-backed type — canvas size, `[VectorPath]`, global simplify strength — kept separate so Recents/routing never has to decode heavy geometry. `VectorPath`/`PathSegment` use native `CGPoint`/`CGSize`/`CGRect` directly (both Codable and Sendable on this deployment target) rather than hand-rolled DTO wrappers. `EditCommand` matches the exact signatures already locked in design_handoff (`deletePath(id:)`, `mergePaths(ids:resultID:)`, `setSimplifyStrength(pathID:value:)`). `RejectionReason` is the fixed seven-case named vocabulary. `NewTraceOutcome` (`.rejected(RejectionReason)` / `.accepted(traceID:)`) is the return type of starting a new trace — this is the mechanism that guarantees a gate-rejected capture never leaves a ghost folder or Recents entry: the folder is deleted internally on rejection, and a `TraceDocument`/status only exists once the gate has passed.

**Persistence strategy:** Per-trace file packages under Application Support, OS-default backup enabled (this is deliberate — "local-first" means not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs, not opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup). No Core Data/SwiftData. All checkpoint writes are atomic temp-file-then-rename, with an explicit test that simulates a kill mid-write and asserts the prior checkpoint survives intact. The Recents list is a rebuildable cache, not a source of truth — on any inconsistency it's reconstructed by scanning trace folders and reading each `TraceDocument`. Durable storage begins only after the quality gate passes; a rejected capture's transient folder is deleted by `AppModel` as part of handling the rejection, never left as orphaned disk state. `TraceStore` owns the atomic write/read/delete contract exactly as specified in the interfaces block below. `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement (file-timestamp and UserDefaults usage trigger required-reason declarations even with zero third-party SDKs). No `BGProcessingTask`; a short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic write, and any processing interrupted mid-stage just restarts from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch.

**Testing strategy:** Pipeline stages are pure, fixture-in/fixture-out functions so gate classification, normalization invariants, simplification bounds, and export serialization are all fast, deterministic unit tests — no camera, no UI. The four locked fixtures (icon sketch, lettering, closed-shape illustration, messy-but-recoverable notebook photo) plus a deliberately-bad set (one photo per named rejection reason) plus an adversarial nested-closed-loop case (to catch winding-rule/fill bugs) run through the full pipeline with metric assertions: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, correct winding/fill on export, verified via rasterize-and-diff against fixtures rather than just XML validity. The edit reducer gets adversarial sequence tests (delete-all, merge-with-self, redo-after-new-edit invalidates the stack). Checkpoint/resume is tested by writing a checkpoint, simulating a hard kill, and reloading — not by scripting real OS-level termination. XCUITest is reserved narrowly for things provable only on a real render tree: the Workspace gesture contract (wipe-handle arming, tap-to-select never firing during a wipe drag, no double-tap-zoom delay), a real VoiceOver pass over path-list cleanup, and permission-denied → import-still-works.

## Phase Purpose

Decide architecture, technology stack, data model, interfaces/APIs, and non-functional requirements with justified trade-offs.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

The smallest architecture that still honestly ships this app is a document-style pipeline with four hard boundaries: ingest, gate/normalize, vectorize/edit, and export/persist. I would keep SwiftUI thin and stateful only at the workflow edges. One `@MainActor @Observable` `AppModel` owns recents and app-wide settings; one `@MainActor @Observable` `TraceSession` owns the open job, current screen state, selection, undo/redo stack, and committed checkpoint metadata. Everything expensive or failure-prone stays out of views and behind async service protocols so the app can be tested with fixtures and resumed without UI involvement. The critical choice is to treat vectorization as the risky core, not as a Vision helper. Vision only gives rectangle/contour detection; the product quality bar lives in our cleanup, simplification, curve fitting, path classification, and export correctness.

I would split the code into local Swift packages or at least module-like targets even for MVP: `TraceDomain`, `TracePersistence`, `TraceProcessing`, `TraceExport`, and the app target for UI/composition. `TraceDomain` holds pure types like `TraceDocument`, `VectorPath`, `QualityGateFailure`, `ProcessingStage`, edit commands, and checkpoint metadata. `TraceProcessing` owns image ingest, EXIF normalization, perspective correction, thresholding, ruled-paper suppression, quality gating, contour extraction, simplification, merge heuristics, and Bezier fitting. `TracePersistence` owns per-trace folders, atomic writes, index rebuild, thumbnails, and resume-from-checkpoint loading. `TraceExport` owns SVG/PDF serialization and export artifact prep. The UI target should know protocols and domain types, not file layouts or CI filters. That keeps parallel work sane and prevents one lane from inventing incompatible types under deadline pressure.

The data model should stay document-shaped. A `TraceDocument` is the durable unit and maps to one folder. It needs an `id`, created/updated timestamps, a `TraceStatus` enum, a `lastCommittedStage`, a `workspaceMode`, optional `QualityGateResult`, normalized image metadata, vector model metadata, export status, and a thumbnail reference. The vector itself should not be hidden in generic blobs. Use explicit `VectorPath` values with `id`, `points` or fitted segments, `isClosed`, `boundingBox`, `nodeCount`, `sourceFragmentLength`, and per-path simplify parameters if needed later. Keep the edit history command-based: `deletePaths([UUID])`, `mergePaths([UUID])`, `setSimplifyStrength(pathIDs:[UUID], value: Float)`. That gives deterministic undo/redo and clean checkpointing because only committed commands become durable. Do not persist in-flight slider drafts, partial Vision results, or transient zoom state as if they were business data.

Persistence should be a per-trace package under Application Support, OS-default backup enabled, with no Core Data or SwiftData. The folder contract should be explicit and stable: `metadata.json`, `normalized.heic`, `vector.json`, `thumbnail.jpg`, optional exported artifacts cache if we decide to retain them. The raw camera image is transient only through correction review, then discarded. All writes must be temp-file-then-rename atomic writes. The recents list should be a rebuildable cache, not the source of truth. If the app crashes after writing a trace folder but before updating the index, relaunch should rescan folders and reconstruct recents from `metadata.json`. That is a real field-failure issue, not polish. Resume behavior should be defined mechanically: if the last committed stage is `.normalized`, restart vectorization; if `.traced`, reopen workspace; if `.edited`, reopen workspace with saved vector state. Never attempt to resume a half-run Vision request or a half-drag simplify preview.

Concurrency boundaries need to be strict because the likely failure mode is UI jank and thermal collapse, not “wrong architecture diagram.” `TraceSession` and `AppModel` stay on the main actor. Processing services run off-main and return value types. Slider-driven preview work must be cancelable and debounced; full-resolution re-fit happens only on commit. I would explicitly maintain separate preview and export-quality processing paths so the app does not recompute full-res geometry every time the simplify slider moves. If that distinction is not written into tech specs now, it will get skipped, and the app will feel broken on older phones even if the exports are correct.

The Apple framework choices are straightforward and should stay boring. AVFoundation for custom camera capture. `PHPickerViewController` or `PhotosPicker` for import, avoiding broad photo-library permission. ImageIO/Core Image/Accelerate for ingest, orientation handling, downsampling, thresholding, and cleanup. Vision for rectangle and contour detection only. Core Graphics for PDF output. Hand-written SVG XML generation from the path model. No third-party packages. No StoreKit. No background processing framework. A short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window is enough to finish an atomic checkpoint write if backgrounded mid-save; if suspension happens during processing, restart from last committed checkpoint on relaunch.

The biggest risk in this direction is underestimating the bespoke geometry work and shipping a technically valid but commercially useless SVG. “Opens in Illustrator” is not the same as “usable in Illustrator.” The spec needs to preserve measurable guardrails: orphan fragments below threshold removed, node counts capped, stroke continuity preserved, closed loops exported with correct winding/fill behavior, and four locked fixtures passing with only the bounded cleanup tools. I would add one more adversarial fixture class now: nested closed loops like an “O” or badge icon, because fill-rule bugs are exactly the sort of thing that slip through until a real export review.

Testing should favor deterministic pipeline checks over UI spectacle. Unit tests for EXIF orientation normalization, quality gate classification against all named failure reasons, ruled-paper suppression, simplification bounds, merge/delete reducer behavior, undo/redo invalidation rules, atomic checkpoint integrity, recents index rebuild, and SVG/PDF serialization. Integration tests should run the fixture set through the full pipeline and assert metrics, not just snapshots: non-empty output, bounded node counts, no fragments below the minimum length, and correct reopen-from-checkpoint behavior. UI tests should be narrow and high-value: permission-denied intake fallback, rejection copy visibility at large Dynamic Type, kill-and-relaunch after each checkpoint, workspace gesture gating, accessible path-list cleanup, and export failure leaving the trace safely in recents. The point is to prove the app fails honestly and never loses work, not to maximize screen coverage.

The detail I would insist survives into the final phase output is this: the app is not “camera in, SVG out.” It is a checkpointed local document processor with a bespoke vectorization core, and the spec has to name the failure boundaries, the durable data contract, and the verification rules tightly enough that parallel build lanes can implement against one truth instead of improvising.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Looking at this from the client side, my job is to make sure the thing a solo illustrator actually taps on Tuesday night at her kitchen table holds together — state doesn't get lost, screens don't jank, and the four-lane parallel build doesn't produce four different opinions about what a `VectorPath` is.

The architecture I want locked is: one thin SwiftUI app target that owns navigation and screens, and everything else lives in local Swift Package targets with real `internal` access boundaries, exactly as the ios_architecture_review phase called for. But I want to go one level more specific than "isolated services," because the thing that will actually break a solo build-lane split is two workers independently inventing two different shapes for `VectorPath` or `TraceDocument`. So there's a `CleanlineModels` package that contains every shared data type — no lane may redeclare its own copy of a cross-lane type, full stop. Every service consumes and returns those shared types, never a private DTO that gets converted later. That's the single rule that makes "parallel build lanes can work without inventing incompatible types" actually true instead of aspirational.

Navigation: a single `NavigationStack` driven by an explicit `AppRoute` enum path array, not ad-hoc pushes scattered across views. The reason I care about this specifically is checkpoint resume — the whole product promise is "reopen a trace and land exactly where you left off," and that only holds if there's one function, `AppRouter.route(for: TraceDocument)`, that deterministically computes the NavigationPath from a loaded document's status field (needsReview → Workspace/Compare, mid-edit → Workspace/Edit, traceFailed → Processing's failed state). If this logic gets embedded ad hoc in fifteen different `.onAppear` handlers instead of one router, someone will "temporarily" make interrupted traces land back on Recents Home to unblock a build, and that regression will be invisible until a real user loses their place after a phone call.

State ownership: `TraceSession` is an `@Observable`, `@MainActor` reference type, one instance per open trace, and it is the only mutable source of truth for zoom/pan/selection/current mode/undo-redo stack. Views bind to it, they never own parallel copies of its state in `@State`. The undo/redo stack is a command log (`deletePath`, `mergePaths`, `setSimplifyStrength`), and — this is the detail I most want to protect — a command is only ever appended on gesture *commit*, never on `onChanged`/mid-drag. That's not a style preference, it's what makes "resume from last committed checkpoint" mechanically true instead of a hope. If a slider's live value ever leaks into the checkpoint before release, the resume promise silently breaks the first time someone backgrounds the app mid-drag.

On the vectorization and pipeline services specifically: these need to be real `async` boundaries, ideally an `actor VectorizationEngine`, because Vision requests and Douglas-Peucker/Bezier-fitting are genuinely CPU-heavy and must never run on the main actor while the Processing screen is animating stage labels. `TraceSession` calls into these actors and gets back plain `Sendable` value types (`[VectorPath]`, `QualityGateResult`) — no shared mutable state crosses that boundary, which also makes the pipeline stages trivially unit-testable as pure functions, exactly the fixture-based testing model the architecture phase already committed to.

Performance is a first-class requirement, not a hope, because the device floor here matters: this app has to feel instant on something like an iPhone 12, not just the dev's Pro Max. Concretely: the simplify slider drag renders against a downsampled preview (capped at something like 1024px on the long edge) with a cancel-aware debounce around 150–250ms, and only re-fits at full resolution on release — if this gets implemented as a naive `onEditingChanged` call straight into the full-res fitter, it will visibly stutter and the "premium craft tool" feeling evaporates in the one interaction users touch the most. Camera Capture's live preview must hold real frame rate — no synchronous Vision or Core Image work on the capture delegate queue. And I want to name the trust-curve concern explicitly as an engineering requirement, not just a design nicety: trace #2 and #3 should feel faster than trace #1, which means remembering the last-used simplify/threshold preference per user (small `UserDefaults`-backed struct) so repeat traces don't force the same manual dial-in every time.

File/module layout, concretely:

```
Cleanline.xcodeproj
App/                         (primary_ui) — CleanlineApp.swift, AppRouter.swift, RootView.swift
Packages/
  CleanlineModels/            (data_domain) — every shared struct/enum, zero logic
  CleanlinePersistence/        (data_domain + services_utilities) — TraceStore, atomic checkpoint writer, Recents index rebuild
  CleanlineCapture/            (services_utilities) — camera + PHPicker wrappers
  CleanlineNormalize/          (services_utilities) — perspective correction, threshold, EXIF strip
  CleanlineQualityGate/        (services_utilities) — rejection classification
  CleanlineVectorize/          (services_utilities) — Vision contour extraction, simplify, curve fit, winding-rule/open-closed classification
  CleanlineEdit/                (services_utilities) — command application (delete/merge/simplify) as pure reducer over TraceDocument
  CleanlineExport/              (services_utilities) — SVG/PDF serializers
  CleanlineDesignSystem/        (primary_ui) — tokens, StatusChip, PathCanvas, ToolStrip, rejection card, etc.
Features/                      (primary_ui) — RecentsHome, NewTraceIntake, CameraCapture, QualityGateCorrection, Processing, TraceWorkspace, Export, Settings — one folder per screen, each a View + a thin per-screen state wrapper around TraceSession, not a second source of truth
Tests/
  CleanlineModelsTests, CleanlineVectorizeTests (fixture-based, the 4 locked images + adversarial set), CleanlinePersistenceTests (atomic-write kill simulation), CleanlineQualityGateTests, CleanlineEditTests (reducer/undo tests), CleanlineUITests (XCUITest — gesture contract, VoiceOver session, permission-denied flows only)
Fixtures/                      icon-sketch, lettering, closed-shape, messy-notebook + deliberately-bad set per named rejection reason
```

Persistence strategy, from the client side: `TraceStore` is a protocol with atomic temp-file-then-rename writes for `normalized.heic`/`vector.json`/`thumbnail.jpg`/`metadata.json`, and the Recents list is populated by scanning the traces directory and reading each `metadata.json`, never by trusting a separate index file as ground truth — that index is a cache that gets rebuilt, matching what's already locked. On the UI side, Recents Home reads through a lightweight `RecentsIndex.rebuild()` call on appear, not a live-observed database, since this is a flat bounded-by-delete-only list, not something that needs reactive query machinery.

Testing strategy: unit tests own the pipeline stages (pure functions, fixture-in/fixture-out, snapshot-diffable), the edit reducer gets adversarial sequence tests (delete-all, merge-with-self, redo-after-new-edit invalidates stack), persistence gets a literal kill-mid-write simulation, and XCUITest is reserved narrowly for things that can only be proven on a real render tree: the gesture-arming contract (wipe only arms via handle at base zoom, tap-to-select never fires during a wipe drag, no double-tap-zoom delay), a real VoiceOver pass over the path-list cleanup flow, and permission-denied → import-still-works. I don't want XCUITest carrying pipeline-correctness weight; it's slow and flaky, and the pipeline is provably deterministic enough to not need it there.

The biggest risk to my own direction: the four-lane split is only healthy if `CleanlineModels` ships first and is treated as frozen truth for the rest of the build — if lanes start building against guesses about its shape before it's nailed down, we'll get exactly the incompatible-types problem the acceptance check warns about. The thing that must survive into the final tech spec, word for word: one `TraceSession` object, one `AppRouter` function deciding where a reopened trace lands, commands appended only on gesture commit, and every cross-lane type living in a single shared models package that nobody duplicates.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round 1 landed: on the actual architecture strategy, Codex and Claude are in near-total agreement — thin SwiftUI shell over isolated Swift Package targets, an `@Observable` `TraceSession` as the single source of truth, file-package-per-trace persistence with atomic checkpoint writes, no Core Data/SwiftData, all first-party frameworks (AVFoundation/PHPicker/Vision/Core Image/CoreGraphics/hand-rolled SVG), actor-isolated vectorization kept off the main thread, command-based undo/redo committed only on gesture release, and a fixture-first testing strategy with XCUITest reserved narrowly for gesture/VoiceOver/interruption checks. Nobody's arguing about any of that.

But the two `interfaces-json` blocks they actually emitted are not the same contract — they contradict each other in ways that would break a parallel build if left unresolved:

- **`TraceSession` shape**: Codex modeled it as a plain `Sendable` struct; Claude modeled it as an `@Observable @MainActor` reference class. This isn't cosmetic — it's the difference between a value passed around and the actual mutable UI-state owner the design_handoff phase already locked in. Claude's version matches what was already agreed earlier in the project.
- **`TraceStatus` enum**: Codex's has 8 cases (intake, gateRejected, normalizedReady, processing, traceFailed, needsReview, edited, exported); Claude's has 3 (needsReview, exported, traceFailed) — which matches the exact status-chip vocabulary already locked in design_handoff. These need to be reconciled into one persisted status enum plus a separate pipeline-stage enum, not left as two competing definitions.
- **Command enum naming and signatures**: `EditCommand` vs `TraceCommand`, and Codex's `setSimplifyStrength(pathIDs: [UUID], ...)` vs Claude's single-`pathID` version (which matches the exact signature already written into the locked design_handoff text).
- **Vector data shape**: Codex keeps a separate `VectorDocument` (canvasSize + paths + simplifyStrength) matching how `vector.json` is described as its own checkpoint file; Claude embeds `paths` directly on `TraceDocument` with no separate vector container.
- **Service protocol naming**: Codex's generic `ImageIngesting`/`QualityGating`/`Vectorizing`/`Exporting` vs Claude's more granular `CaptureService`/`ImportService`/`NormalizationService`/`QualityGateService`/`VectorizationEngine`/`ExportService` — Claude's split also correctly separates capture from import, which Codex's single ingestion protocol collapses.
- **Rejection-reason enum name**: `QualityGateFailure` vs `RejectionReason` (same case values, different name).

None of this is a values disagreement — it reads like both of you wrote a full contract independently without seeing the other's, and now there are two. That's exactly the failure mode the acceptance check warns about ("parallel build lanes can work without inventing incompatible types"), so this phase can't close until one canonical contract exists.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round, Codex and Claude need to explicitly pick one name/shape per conflict above rather than each defending their own draft. Below is a proposed reconciliation that breaks ties using what's already locked in earlier phases (favoring Claude's `TraceSession`-as-class, the 3-value status chip vocabulary, and the exact command signatures already written into design_handoff) — treat this as the starting point for round 2 confirmation, not a unilateral final answer.

### Round 2


**Codex (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Codex, I think the coordinator is right that the only thing actually blocking consensus now is contract drift, not architecture drift. If we shipped the current round-1 output to parallel builders, we'd get avoidable breakage immediately: one lane coding against `TraceSession` as a value type, another against an `@Observable @MainActor` reference owner; one lane persisting eight `TraceStatus` cases, another rendering only the three status chips the design spec actually allows. That's not “we'll sort it out in code review” territory, that's “resume routing and recents UI become nondeterministic” territory. So I would stop trying to preserve both shapes and just collapse to one canonical split: `TraceStatus` stays tiny and user-facing (`needsReview`, `exported`, `traceFailed`), while pipeline progress lives somewhere else (`TraceStage` for committed checkpoints, `ProcessingStage` for transient on-screen progress). That separation is the smallest thing that actually ships cleanly, and it lines up with what Claude already locked in the design handoff.

I also think Claude's separation of `TraceDocument` metadata from `VectorDocument` payload is the safer call, but only if we stay disciplined about what each file is for. `metadata.json` should answer “what trace is this and where do I resume,” while `vector.json` answers “what paths do I render/export.” If you cram `paths` into `TraceDocument`, every lightweight recents refresh risks dragging heavy geometry through decode paths that don't need it. But if you split them, you need one rule nobody violates: recents and routing never require loading vector geometry. That's the practical performance win on an iPhone 12, and it's exactly the kind of thing that quietly regresses if the contract is vague. Same story on `EditCommand`: keep the command signatures exactly as the design phase wrote them, even if they feel slightly narrow today. The point of the command log is resumability and testability, not theoretical elegance. I'd also keep `QualityMetrics` in the contract even if it's internal-facing, because otherwise the quality gate turns into a boolean oracle that's impossible to debug when “shadow coverage too high” starts misfiring on ruled-paper fixtures. We don't need to expose those metrics in UI, but we absolutely need a typed place for them in tests and logs.

The one place I want to tighten the coordinator's proposed reconciliation is `AppModel.startNewTrace(from:source:)`. Raw `Data` there is too lossy for the capture/import split we've already said matters. We already have `CapturedImage`, with explicit orientation, and that's the safer cross-lane input because orientation bugs are one of the easiest trust-killers in this whole app. I also want `TraceSession` to own `interruptionRecoveryState`; if that lives elsewhere, somebody will implement the resume banner as a separate view concern and the invariant “never resume a half-applied gesture” gets mushy. Otherwise I think the coordinator's direction is the right one: one router function, one observable session object, one shared models package, one persistence contract with atomic checkpoint writes and rebuildable recents, and no pretending these names are still negotiable.
