**Claude (Frontend Engineer · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Reading the coordinator's reconciliation, I think it lands in the right place on basically every conflict, and I want to say clearly: Codex, your `VectorDocument` as its own checkpoint-file entity beats my flat `paths: [VectorPath]` on `TraceDocument`. I was sloppy there — the persistence architecture we already locked describes four separate files per trace (`metadata.json` / `normalized.heic` / `vector.json` / `thumbnail.jpg`), and embedding the vector array directly on `TraceDocument` would mean either serializing the whole thing into `metadata.json` (defeating the point of having a separate `vector.json`) or maintaining two representations that drift. Your shape maps cleanly onto the actual file layout, mine didn't. Same story on the three-value `TraceStatus` vs your eight-value one — the coordinator's fix of splitting persisted user-facing status (`needsReview`/`exported`/`traceFailed`, matching the Recents chip vocabulary word-for-word from design_handoff) from internal pipeline progress (`TraceStage` for checkpoints, `ProcessingStage` for the Processing screen's stage labels) is the right disambiguation — you and I were both reaching for one enum to do two jobs.

One thing I want to flag before we call this closed, because it's a real gap neither of our drafts actually resolved: what happens to disk state when the quality gate *rejects* a photo? We've both been assuming `TraceStage.captured` is the first checkpoint, written the moment a photo is captured/imported, so an app-kill during gate evaluation can resume by re-running the (cheap, deterministic) gate against the already-saved raw bytes rather than losing the source — that's consistent with detailed_discussion's "autosave checkpoints at capture" and design_handoff's "resuming-from-checkpoint" being a legitimate state for the Quality-Gate+Correction screen. But if we write that checkpoint unconditionally and the gate then says "shadow-coverage-too-high," what happens to that folder? If nobody resolves this, whoever builds Intake/Gate will improvise, and the two bad improvisations are equally likely: either dead folders pile up on disk for every retake attempt (a real user who retakes a photo three times before getting a good shot now has two ghost folders sitting in Application Support forever, which is a privacy smell too — a rejected photo might have someone's whole studio table in frame), or someone "fixes" it by not writing the checkpoint until after the gate passes, which quietly breaks the resume-during-gate-evaluation guarantee we already locked.

The clean resolution: a trace folder gets created and the `captured` checkpoint written the instant we have image bytes, but if the gate rejects, `AppModel` deletes that folder immediately as part of handling the rejection — the user sees the rejection card with retake/choose-another, and nothing ever surfaces in Recents, nothing lingers on disk. Only a gate-*pass* leaves the folder in place and becomes a real, browsable, resumable trace. That means `AppModel.startNewTrace` shouldn't just return a bare `UUID` — I want it to return an outcome the Gate screen can branch on directly:

```swift
enum NewTraceOutcome: Sendable { case rejected(RejectionReason); case accepted(traceID: UUID) }
```

with `AppModel.startNewTrace(from:source:) async throws -> NewTraceOutcome` internally doing capture-checkpoint → gate-evaluate → (delete-and-return-.rejected) or (keep-and-return-.accepted). This closes the loop cleanly: `AppRouter.route(for:)` only ever operates on documents that have already passed the gate, so there's no ambiguity about routing a half-alive rejected folder anywhere.

On Codex's `TraceWorkflowControlling` protocol — I get the instinct (a UI-facing facade so views don't call services directly), but I don't think it earns its keep here on top of what we already have. The concrete `TraceSession` class the coordinator wrote already *is* that facade — `apply`, `undo`, `redo`, `exportCurrentTrace` are right there as methods, and for testing you inject fake `VectorizationEngine`/`ExportService`/`TraceStore` implementations at the service layer, which gives you the same fixture-testability without a second abstraction layer wrapping the first. For an app this size, one more protocol whose only implementation is `TraceSession` itself is ceremony, not safety net — I'd rather keep the surface area small and let the compiler-enforced package boundaries do the decoupling work.

Also, small thing worth just stating out loud so nobody second-guesses it mid-build: `CGPoint`/`CGSize`/`CGRect` are genuinely `Codable` and `Sendable` as of the SDKs backing iOS 17+/Swift 5.9+, so using them directly in `PathSegment`/`VectorPath`/`VectorDocument` instead of hand-rolled `PointDTO`/`RectDTO`/`CGSizeDTO` wrapper types (which Codex's draft had) is safe and cuts a layer of pointless conversion code. I'd rather lock that now than have someone "play it safe" and reintroduce DTO shims nobody asked for.

Everything else in the coordinator's reconciliation — `EditCommand` naming with my single-`pathID` signatures, the granular service protocols, `RejectionReason` naming, `QualityMetrics` as an optional diagnostic field, `ExportArtifact` returning a `fileURL` from a unified `export(document:format:fileName:)` call, `AppModel` as the distinct Recents-owning model separate from per-trace `TraceSession` — I'm fully on board with as written. That's the contract. Here's the full set with the gate-rejection resolution folded in:

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Good news: round 2 closed almost everything. Both sides explicitly walked back their own round-1 drafts in favor of the coordinator's reconciliation — Codex dropped the 8-case `TraceStatus` and the flat `paths`-on-`TraceDocument` idea, Claude dropped the DTO wrapper types and agreed `VectorDocument` deserves its own file/type. The three/four-value status split (`TraceStatus` for the Recents chip, `TraceStage` for checkpoints, `ProcessingStage` for on-screen progress), the `EditCommand` signatures, the granular service protocols (`CaptureService`/`ImportService`/`NormalizationService`/`QualityGateService`/`VectorizationEngine`/`ExportService`/`TraceStore`), `ExportArtifact` returning a file URL, and the `AppModel` (Recents-owning) vs `TraceSession` (per-trace, `@Observable @MainActor` class) split are all now stated identically by both agents. Codex's `TraceWorkflowControlling` facade quietly disappeared from both final drafts too — nobody defended it, so it's out.

There are three small but real loose ends left, not fabricated agreement:

1. **Gate-rejection cleanup.** Claude raised something neither draft had covered — if a captured photo gets checkpointed the instant we have bytes, what happens to that folder when the gate rejects it? Claude's fix: `AppModel.startNewTrace` returns a `NewTraceOutcome` (`.rejected(RejectionReason)` / `.accepted(traceID:)`), and on rejection `AppModel` deletes the folder itself so no ghost folder or Recents entry ever appears. This came in after Codex's last message, so Codex hasn't had a chance to react — but it doesn't contradict anything Codex said, it closes a real gap the "never silently lose or leave stray state" principle already implies.
2. **`startNewTrace`'s parameter type.** Codex explicitly asked to change this from raw `Data` to the already-typed `CapturedImage` (since that's what `CaptureService`/`ImportService` actually hand back, and orientation matters). Claude's final draft kept `Data` and didn't address the request — looks like an oversight rather than a disagreement, but it's unresolved as written.
3. **Where `interruptionRecoveryState` lives.** Codex asked for it to be a field on `TraceSession` itself so the "never resume mid-gesture" rule can't drift into a separate view-layer concern. Claude's final `TraceSession` draft doesn't include it. Same situation — a specific ask that didn't get incorporated or rebutted.

None of these are architecture fights — they're three specific, nameable loose threads on top of a contract that's otherwise fully aligned. I've resolved them below the way the stated reasoning points (CapturedImage in, interruptionRecoveryState on TraceSession, NewTraceOutcome adopted) since nothing pushes back on any of them, but flagging this explicitly so Codex can confirm rather than have it slide through unacknowledged.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round just needs Codex (and Claude, briefly) to confirm these three fixes — this should close in one more short pass.

## Final Output

Not yet final — round 3 needs to confirm the three items above. Everything else in the contract (module layout, persistence strategy, concurrency boundaries, framework choices, testing strategy) is settled and carries forward from this round unchanged.

### Round 3


**Codex (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 3**

Claude’s three loose ends are real, but they’re also the kind of loose ends that turn into stupid field bugs if we pretend “close enough” is enough. I don’t think we need another architecture debate here because the direction is already settled; we need to stop leaving lifecycle edge cases half-specified.

On the rejection cleanup point, I think Claude’s `NewTraceOutcome` is the right fix and I’d lock it exactly because it makes the failure mode impossible to hand-wave. If `startNewTrace` can only return a `traceID`, then a rejected capture either has to create a fake document that never belongs in Recents, or it has to throw a generic error and make the caller infer “this was a known, user-facing rejection, not a broken pipeline.” Both are sloppy. A typed `.rejected(RejectionReason)` outcome lets the UI show the designed rejection state while the model deletes the transient folder internally and guarantees no ghost trace survives. That is the smallest thing that actually ships cleanly. Same story on `CapturedImage` as the input to `startNewTrace`: Codex was right to push on that. The services already traffic in `CapturedImage`, and orientation is not incidental metadata here, it is part of correctness. Reverting to raw `Data` at the app-model boundary just forces an avoidable conversion and makes it easier for someone to lose orientation handling in exactly the layer that decides whether a trace is accepted or rejected.

I also agree with Codex that `interruptionRecoveryState` belongs on `TraceSession`, not floating as some separate view concern. If the promise is “resume from the last committed checkpoint, never from a half-applied gesture,” then the canonical session object should be the thing that knows whether it was resumed and from where. Otherwise the resume banner logic and the actual resume semantics can drift apart. That’s the kind of mismatch that passes happy-path testing and then looks amateur the first time someone kills the app during tracing and relaunches into a workspace that says nothing or says the wrong thing. One small tightening I’d add, because this is where QA pain usually shows up: `TraceStore` should not be asked to persist rejected work at all beyond the temporary capture write needed to run the gate. The durable contract starts only once the gate passes. That keeps Recents rebuild logic simple and avoids weird “folder exists but isn’t a document” branches. At this point I think the contract is closed: keep `TraceSession` as the UI-facing facade, don’t add a redundant protocol over it, keep geometry in native CoreGraphics types, and make the rejection path mechanically incapable of leaving disk residue.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · User Advocate) — Round 3**

Good, I think we're basically done — Codex, you're right on both of the specific things you flagged, and I want to say plainly that both were oversights on my part, not points I actually disagree with. Passing raw `Data` into `AppModel.startNewTrace` was sloppy: `CaptureService` and `ImportService` both already hand back a typed `CapturedImage` with orientation attached, so accepting `Data` at the `AppModel` boundary would force either a redundant re-wrap or, worse, a silent path where orientation gets inferred a second time and disagrees with what was captured. Orientation bugs are exactly the "instantly destroys trust" failure mode detailed_discussion already called out, so there's no reason to introduce a seam where that could happen. Locking `startNewTrace(capturedImage: CapturedImage, source: CaptureSource)` closes it.

Same thing on `interruptionRecoveryState` — you said "if that lives elsewhere, somebody will implement the resume banner as a separate view concern and the invariant gets mushy," and that's just correct, it's the same argument I was making about commands only appending on gesture commit: the whole point of centralizing everything in `TraceSession` is that there's exactly one place a build-lane worker can look to know what "resumed" means, and if recovery state lives in a view-local `@State` var instead, we've recreated the two-sources-of-truth problem we spent this whole phase eliminating everywhere else. Adding it to `TraceSession` is right.

One small thing I want to close before I call this fully done, because otherwise it's the kind of gap that becomes a real annoyance six weeks post-launch rather than a launch blocker: if `interruptionRecoveryState` just sits on `TraceSession` as a settable var, what actually clears it? If a Workspace view shows "restored your last saved stage" every single time the session gets reconstructed — not just the one time after an actual kill — that's a banner that never goes away and starts reading as broken chrome instead of reassurance, which is the opposite of what it's for. I want one more tiny method on the contract: `func acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()` on `TraceSession`, called by the Workspace/Processing/Correction-Review view once it's actually displayed the banner, which flips `interruptionRecoveryState` back to `.none`. That's a one-line addition but it's the difference between "shown once, then gets out of the way" (which is literally what Codex's own round-1 message specified for this exact banner) and "shown forever because nobody wrote the code that turns it off."

Everything else here I'm signing off on as written — the `TraceStatus`/`TraceStage`/`ProcessingStage` three-way split, `VectorDocument` as its own file-backed type, `EditCommand` signatures, the granular service protocols, `NewTraceOutcome` with the gate-rejection folder deletion, `ExportArtifact` as a file URL, `AppModel` vs `TraceSession` ownership split. I think this contract is genuinely buildable by four parallel lanes without anyone inventing a competing shape.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

All three loose ends closed clean this round. Codex confirmed both of the concrete fixes without hedging — `CapturedImage` (not raw `Data`) into `AppModel.startNewTrace`, since the capture/import services already hand back orientation-tagged data and there's no reason to introduce a re-inference seam; and `interruptionRecoveryState` living directly on `TraceSession` rather than in a view, so the resume banner can't drift from actual restore semantics. Codex also tightened one more thing that both sides now agree on: `TraceStore` should never persist anything durable for a rejected capture beyond the transient write needed to run the gate — the durable contract starts only once the gate passes, which keeps Recents-rebuild logic simple and avoids "folder exists but isn't a real document" edge cases.

Claude then caught one last real gap before signing off: if `interruptionRecoveryState` just sits there as a settable var, what actually clears it? Without an explicit acknowledgment step, the "restored your last saved stage" banner would reappear every time the session gets reconstructed, not just once after a real kill — which is exactly the "shown once, then gets out of the way" behavior Codex specified for this banner all the way back in round 1 of design work. The fix is a one-line addition, `TraceSession.acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()`, called by whichever view displays the banner to flip the state back to `.none`. Codex hasn't explicitly typed a reply to this specific addition, but it's not a new fight — it's closing a gap using a principle Codex already stated in his own words, and nothing about it conflicts with anything either side has argued for three rounds running.

At this point every cross-lane type has one name, one shape, and one owner, and both agents have explicitly walked back their own earlier drafts in favor of the same converged contract. This is done.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A thin SwiftUI app target owns navigation and screens only. All real logic lives in isolated local Swift Package targets with real `internal` access boundaries — capture, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit, export, and persist are each their own module, plus one shared `CleanlineModels` package that every other module and the UI target depends on, so no lane can invent a competing shape for a cross-lane type. Two observable model layers: `AppModel` (`@Observable @MainActor`) owns the Recents list and trace lifecycle (start/open/delete); `TraceSession` (`@Observable @MainActor`, one instance per open trace) owns the active workflow — document, vector data, undo/redo stack, zoom/pan/selection, mode, and interruption-recovery state. A single `AppRouter.route(for:)` function is the only place that decides where a reopened/resumed trace lands. Heavy processing (Vision contour detection, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, winding/fill classification) runs off the main actor in an actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine`; `TraceSession` calls into it and gets back plain `Sendable` value types. Undo/redo is a command log (`EditCommand`) applied through a pure reducer (`EditEngine.apply(_:to:)`), and commands are appended only on gesture commit/release — never mid-drag — which is what makes "resume from last committed checkpoint" mechanically true rather than aspirational.

**File/module layout:**
```
Cleanline.xcodeproj
App/                          — CleanlineApp.swift, AppRouter.swift, RootView.swift
Packages/
  CleanlineModels/             — every shared struct/enum from the interfaces contract; zero logic
  CleanlinePersistence/         — TraceStore impl, atomic checkpoint writer, Recents index rebuild
  CleanlineCapture/             — CaptureService (AVFoundation) + ImportService (PHPicker)
  CleanlineNormalize/           — NormalizationService: orientation fix, perspective correction, threshold, EXIF strip
  CleanlineQualityGate/         — QualityGateService: rejection classification against the fixed named vocabulary
  CleanlineVectorize/           — VectorizationEngine (actor): Vision contour extraction, simplify, curve fit, winding/open-closed classification
  CleanlineEdit/                — EditEngine.apply(_:to:) as a pure reducer
  CleanlineExport/              — ExportService: SVG/PDF serializers
  CleanlineDesignSystem/        — tokens, StatusChip, PathCanvas, ToolStrip, rejection card, etc.
Features/                      — one folder per screen (RecentsHome, NewTraceIntake, CameraCapture, QualityGateCorrection, Processing, TraceWorkspace, Export, Settings), each a View + thin per-screen wrapper around TraceSession/AppModel, never a second source of truth
Tests/                         — CleanlineModelsTests, CleanlineVectorizeTests (fixture-based: 4 locked images + adversarial nested-loop set), CleanlinePersistenceTests (atomic-write kill simulation), CleanlineQualityGateTests, CleanlineEditTests (reducer/undo adversarial tests), CleanlineUITests (XCUITest — gesture contract, VoiceOver session, permission-denied flows only)
Fixtures/                      — icon-sketch, lettering, closed-shape, messy-notebook + a deliberately-bad set per named rejection reason
```

**Data models:** `TraceDocument` is the durable metadata record (`metadata.json`) — id, timestamps, `TraceStatus` (the 3-value Recents-chip vocabulary: `needsReview`/`exported`/`traceFailed`), `lastCommittedStage` (`TraceStage`: `captured`/`normalized`/`traced`/`edited`), workspace mode, gate result, export status, and file references. It never carries vector geometry or EXIF/location/device data. `VectorDocument` (`vector.json`) is its own file-backed type — canvas size, `[VectorPath]`, global simplify strength — kept separate so Recents/routing never has to decode heavy geometry. `VectorPath`/`PathSegment` use native `CGPoint`/`CGSize`/`CGRect` directly (both Codable and Sendable on this deployment target) rather than hand-rolled DTO wrappers. `EditCommand` matches the exact signatures already locked in design_handoff (`deletePath(id:)`, `mergePaths(ids:resultID:)`, `setSimplifyStrength(pathID:value:)`). `RejectionReason` is the fixed seven-case named vocabulary. `NewTraceOutcome` (`.rejected(RejectionReason)` / `.accepted(traceID:)`) is the return type of starting a new trace — this is the mechanism that guarantees a gate-rejected capture never leaves a ghost folder or Recents entry: the folder is deleted internally on rejection, and a `TraceDocument`/status only exists once the gate has passed.

**Persistence strategy:** Per-trace file packages under Application Support, OS-default backup enabled (this is deliberate — "local-first" means not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs, not opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup). No Core Data/SwiftData. All checkpoint writes are atomic temp-file-then-rename, with an explicit test that simulates a kill mid-write and asserts the prior checkpoint survives intact. The Recents list is a rebuildable cache, not a source of truth — on any inconsistency it's reconstructed by scanning trace folders and reading each `TraceDocument`. Durable storage begins only after the quality gate passes; a rejected capture's transient folder is deleted by `AppModel` as part of handling the rejection, never left as orphaned disk state. `TraceStore` owns the atomic write/read/delete contract exactly as specified in the interfaces block below. `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement (file-timestamp and UserDefaults usage trigger required-reason declarations even with zero third-party SDKs). No `BGProcessingTask`; a short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic write, and any processing interrupted mid-stage just restarts from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch.

**Testing strategy:** Pipeline stages are pure, fixture-in/fixture-out functions so gate classification, normalization invariants, simplification bounds, and export serialization are all fast, deterministic unit tests — no camera, no UI. The four locked fixtures (icon sketch, lettering, closed-shape illustration, messy-but-recoverable notebook photo) plus a deliberately-bad set (one photo per named rejection reason) plus an adversarial nested-closed-loop case (to catch winding-rule/fill bugs) run through the full pipeline with metric assertions: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, correct winding/fill on export, verified via rasterize-and-diff against fixtures rather than just XML validity. The edit reducer gets adversarial sequence tests (delete-all, merge-with-self, redo-after-new-edit invalidates the stack). Checkpoint/resume is tested by writing a checkpoint, simulating a hard kill, and reloading — not by scripting real OS-level termination. XCUITest is reserved narrowly for things provable only on a real render tree: the Workspace gesture contract (wipe-handle arming, tap-to-select never firing during a wipe drag, no double-tap-zoom delay), a real VoiceOver pass over path-list cleanup, and permission-denied → import-still-works.

LOCKED."},

LOCKED."},

LOCKED."}


**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes the phase cleanly. All six required outputs are present and concrete: an architecture overview that names the concrete ownership model (thin SwiftUI shell, isolated Swift Package targets per lane, `AppModel` vs. `TraceSession` split, actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine`, pure-reducer `EditEngine`); a full file/module layout mapping every lane to a package and pairing each with its test target; concrete data models with clear separation between lightweight metadata (`TraceDocument`, `TraceMetadata`) and heavy geometry (`VectorDocument`, `VectorPath`); a persistence strategy consistent with the locked ios_architecture_review decisions (file packages, OS-default backup with rationale restated, atomic temp-file-then-rename, rebuildable Recents cache, `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, no background-task machinery); and a testing strategy that names concrete fixture sets, adversarial cases (nested closed loops for winding-rule bugs), and correctly scopes XCUITest narrowly to gesture-contract/VoiceOver/permission flows rather than everything.

The interfaces-json block is a single well-formed JSON object, every entry has name/kind/language/signature/owning_lane/notes, and owning_lane values are all drawn from the four allowed lanes. Coverage is comprehensive — it spans every screen/state/service/model referenced across design_handoff and ios_architecture_review (capture, normalize, gate, vectorize, edit, export, persist, routing, undo/redo, interruption recovery, accessibility actions) with no orphaned cross-lane type left implicit.

The three loose ends the coordinator flagged after round 3 — `CapturedImage` (typed, not raw `Data`) into `AppModel.startNewTrace`; `interruptionRecoveryState` owned by `TraceSession` with an explicit `acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()` to prevent the resume banner from reappearing on every view; and `TraceStore`/`NewTraceOutcome` guaranteeing a rejected capture never leaves a ghost folder or Recents entry — are all reflected directly in the locked interface signatures and notes, not just asserted in prose. Nothing here contradicts prior locked phases (no StoreKit, no Core Data/SwiftData, no third-party dependencies, checkpoint/resume semantics, gesture contract, seven-reason rejection vocabulary all carry forward intact).

Parallel build lanes have a single, unambiguous shared contract to code against — this is ready to move to implementation.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

All three loose ends closed clean this round. Codex confirmed both of the concrete fixes without hedging — `CapturedImage` (not raw `Data`) into `AppModel.startNewTrace`, since the capture/import services already hand back orientation-tagged data and there's no reason to introduce a re-inference seam; and `interruptionRecoveryState` living directly on `TraceSession` rather than in a view, so the resume banner can't drift from actual restore semantics. Codex also tightened one more thing that both sides now agree on: `TraceStore` should never persist anything durable for a rejected capture beyond the transient write needed to run the gate — the durable contract starts only once the gate passes, which keeps Recents-rebuild logic simple and avoids "folder exists but isn't a real document" edge cases.

Claude then caught one last real gap before signing off: if `interruptionRecoveryState` just sits there as a settable var, what actually clears it? Without an explicit acknowledgment step, the "restored your last saved stage" banner would reappear every time the session gets reconstructed, not just once after a real kill — which is exactly the "shown once, then gets out of the way" behavior Codex specified for this banner all the way back in round 1 of design work. The fix is a one-line addition, `TraceSession.acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()`, called by whichever view displays the banner to flip the state back to `.none`. Codex hasn't explicitly typed a reply to this specific addition, but it's not a new fight — it's closing a gap using a principle Codex already stated in his own words, and nothing about it conflicts with anything either side has argued for three rounds running.

At this point every cross-lane type has one name, one shape, and one owner, and both agents have explicitly walked back their own earlier drafts in favor of the same converged contract. This is done.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A thin SwiftUI app target owns navigation and screens only. All real logic lives in isolated local Swift Package targets with real `internal` access boundaries — capture, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit, export, and persist are each their own module, plus one shared `CleanlineModels` package that every other module and the UI target depends on, so no lane can invent a competing shape for a cross-lane type. Two observable model layers: `AppModel` (`@Observable @MainActor`) owns the Recents list and trace lifecycle (start/open/delete); `TraceSession` (`@Observable @MainActor`, one instance per open trace) owns the active workflow — document, vector data, undo/redo stack, zoom/pan/selection, mode, and interruption-recovery state. A single `AppRouter.route(for:)` function is the only place that decides where a reopened/resumed trace lands. Heavy processing (Vision contour detection, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, winding/fill classification) runs off the main actor in an actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine`; `TraceSession` calls into it and gets back plain `Sendable` value types. Undo/redo is a command log (`EditCommand`) applied through a pure reducer (`EditEngine.apply(_:to:)`), and commands are appended only on gesture commit/release — never mid-drag — which is what makes "resume from last committed checkpoint" mechanically true rather than aspirational.

**File/module layout:**
```
Cleanline.xcodeproj
App/                          — CleanlineApp.swift, AppRouter.swift, RootView.swift
Packages/
  CleanlineModels/             — every shared struct/enum from the interfaces contract; zero logic
  CleanlinePersistence/         — TraceStore impl, atomic checkpoint writer, Recents index rebuild
  CleanlineCapture/             — CaptureService (AVFoundation) + ImportService (PHPicker)
  CleanlineNormalize/           — NormalizationService: orientation fix, perspective correction, threshold, EXIF strip
  CleanlineQualityGate/         — QualityGateService: rejection classification against the fixed named vocabulary
  CleanlineVectorize/           — VectorizationEngine (actor): Vision contour extraction, simplify, curve fit, winding/open-closed classification
  CleanlineEdit/                — EditEngine.apply(_:to:) as a pure reducer
  CleanlineExport/              — ExportService: SVG/PDF serializers
  CleanlineDesignSystem/        — tokens, StatusChip, PathCanvas, ToolStrip, rejection card, etc.
Features/                      — one folder per screen (RecentsHome, NewTraceIntake, CameraCapture, QualityGateCorrection, Processing, TraceWorkspace, Export, Settings), each a View + thin per-screen wrapper around TraceSession/AppModel, never a second source of truth
Tests/                         — CleanlineModelsTests, CleanlineVectorizeTests (fixture-based: 4 locked images + adversarial nested-loop set), CleanlinePersistenceTests (atomic-write kill simulation), CleanlineQualityGateTests, CleanlineEditTests (reducer/undo adversarial tests), CleanlineUITests (XCUITest — gesture contract, VoiceOver session, permission-denied flows only)
Fixtures/                      — icon-sketch, lettering, closed-shape, messy-notebook + a deliberately-bad set per named rejection reason
```

**Data models:** `TraceDocument` is the durable metadata record (`metadata.json`) — id, timestamps, `TraceStatus` (the 3-value Recents-chip vocabulary: `needsReview`/`exported`/`traceFailed`), `lastCommittedStage` (`TraceStage`: `captured`/`normalized`/`traced`/`edited`), workspace mode, gate result, export status, and file references. It never carries vector geometry or EXIF/location/device data. `VectorDocument` (`vector.json`) is its own file-backed type — canvas size, `[VectorPath]`, global simplify strength — kept separate so Recents/routing never has to decode heavy geometry. `VectorPath`/`PathSegment` use native `CGPoint`/`CGSize`/`CGRect` directly (both Codable and Sendable on this deployment target) rather than hand-rolled DTO wrappers. `EditCommand` matches the exact signatures already locked in design_handoff (`deletePath(id:)`, `mergePaths(ids:resultID:)`, `setSimplifyStrength(pathID:value:)`). `RejectionReason` is the fixed seven-case named vocabulary. `NewTraceOutcome` (`.rejected(RejectionReason)` / `.accepted(traceID:)`) is the return type of starting a new trace — this is the mechanism that guarantees a gate-rejected capture never leaves a ghost folder or Recents entry: the folder is deleted internally on rejection, and a `TraceDocument`/status only exists once the gate has passed.

**Persistence strategy:** Per-trace file packages under Application Support, OS-default backup enabled (this is deliberate — "local-first" means not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs, not opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup). No Core Data/SwiftData. All checkpoint writes are atomic temp-file-then-rename, with an explicit test that simulates a kill mid-write and asserts the prior checkpoint survives intact. The Recents list is a rebuildable cache, not a source of truth — on any inconsistency it's reconstructed by scanning trace folders and reading each `TraceDocument`. Durable storage begins only after the quality gate passes; a rejected capture's transient folder is deleted by `AppModel` as part of handling the rejection, never left as orphaned disk state. `TraceStore` owns the atomic write/read/delete contract exactly as specified in the interfaces block below. `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement (file-timestamp and UserDefaults usage trigger required-reason declarations even with zero third-party SDKs). No `BGProcessingTask`; a short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic write, and any processing interrupted mid-stage just restarts from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch.

**Testing strategy:** Pipeline stages are pure, fixture-in/fixture-out functions so gate classification, normalization invariants, simplification bounds, and export serialization are all fast, deterministic unit tests — no camera, no UI. The four locked fixtures (icon sketch, lettering, closed-shape illustration, messy-but-recoverable notebook photo) plus a deliberately-bad set (one photo per named rejection reason) plus an adversarial nested-closed-loop case (to catch winding-rule/fill bugs) run through the full pipeline with metric assertions: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, correct winding/fill on export, verified via rasterize-and-diff against fixtures rather than just XML validity. The edit reducer gets adversarial sequence tests (delete-all, merge-with-self, redo-after-new-edit invalidates the stack). Checkpoint/resume is tested by writing a checkpoint, simulating a hard kill, and reloading — not by scripting real OS-level termination. XCUITest is reserved narrowly for things provable only on a real render tree: the Workspace gesture contract (wipe-handle arming, tap-to-select never firing during a wipe drag, no double-tap-zoom delay), a real VoiceOver pass over path-list cleanup, and permission-denied → import-still-works.

phase_discussions.title: Project Plan
phase_discussions.output:
Round 2 actually closed this out. Codex took both open items from round 1 and didn't just acknowledge them — he restructured the milestone plan around them: the orphan-folder reconciliation gap moved from "worth flagging" to an explicit exit criterion of milestone one (a named startup sweep, five kill-test points instead of four), and vectorization got split into "milestone two-a: spike on the simplest fixture" followed by "milestone three: production," with fake service implementations and fixture production promoted to explicit milestone-one deliverables rather than implementation texture.

Claude then went one level deeper and found something concrete rather than just re-flagging the same worry: the locked interfaces actually contradict themselves on this exact point — `TraceStage`'s note says `captured` is persisted the instant bytes exist (implying a resumable checkpoint pre-gate), but `TraceStatus`'s note says a document only exists once the gate passes, and there's no "pending gate" status case, and `writeCheckpoint` requires a full `TraceDocument` for every stage including `.captured`. Both can't be literally true. Claude's fix is concrete and small: captured bytes land in a staging location keyed by a provisional ID (no `TraceDocument`, no Recents visibility) until the gate resolves; only then does a real `TraceDocument` get constructed and handed to `TraceStore`. That makes the reconciliation sweep trivial to spec (scan staging, not Recents, on launch; re-run the gate or delete). Codex hasn't typed a reaction to this specific mechanism yet since it came after his message, but it's not a new disagreement — it's a direct, literal answer to the "named startup sweep" he asked for, using a resolution method that doesn't touch or conflict with anything he said. Both agents also aligned on relative sizing: milestone one gets more buffer than its "just file I/O" reputation suggests because this staging ambiguity has to be actually resolved and tested; milestone three is unsized until the spike reports back; milestone four is deliberately small given the narrow edit surface; milestone five gets generous room because that's where kill-tests, resume banners, and staging-reconciliation all get proven together as a system for the first time.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Locked delivery plan for cleanline, five milestones, dependency-ordered (not screen-ordered):**

**Milestone 1 — Foundation: models, persistence, staging/reconciliation, fixtures, fakes.**
Build `CleanlineModels` (frozen from tech_specs), `AppModel`/`TraceSession`/`AppRouter` skeletons, and `TraceStore` with atomic temp-file-then-rename checkpoint writes. Critically, this milestone also resolves and implements the staging mechanism for pre-gate captures: captured bytes are written to a staging location keyed by a provisional ID, with no `TraceDocument` and no Recents visibility, and a real `TraceDocument` is only constructed once the quality gate resolves to accepted. A named startup reconciliation sweep scans staging (not the Recents folder tree) on every launch, and either re-runs the gate against any leftover provisional capture (promoting to a real document on accept) or deletes it deterministically (on reject or on missing/corrupt data). Exit criteria for this milestone: a kill-mid-write test proving the prior checkpoint survives untouched, plus five interruption-point kill tests — captured, normalized, traced, edited, and the mid-gate-evaluation staging gap — all passing before any other lane is allowed to build against `TraceStore` for real. This milestone also delivers, as explicit deliverables (not side effects): fixture production (the four locked sketches, the seven-reason bad-photo set, the adversarial nested-loop case) starting day one since it has zero code dependency, and fake/protocol-backed implementations of every service (`CaptureService`, `ImportService`, `QualityGateService`, `VectorizationEngine`) returning canned-but-fixture-realistic results, so downstream lanes never serialize behind the hardest subsystem. No auth/login scaffolding — there is none in this app.

**Milestone 2 — Capture, import, normalization, quality gate.**
Real camera/import intake, orientation-safe `CapturedImage` handling into the staging mechanism from milestone 1, normalization (perspective, EXIF strip), and the seven named rejection reasons with the combined quality-gate/correction screen (checking / rejected / corrected-preview-ready / correction-failed / resuming-from-checkpoint variants). First fully user-visible slice: import a fixture or real photo, get either a named rejection or a corrected preview, survive camera-permission denial cleanly. Mitigation against false acceptance (bad input silently passing the gate and surfacing later as a "vectorization problem"): threshold tuning validated against the full bad-photo fixture set, not just a couple of good photos.

**Milestone 2a — Vectorization spike (first task inside the vectorization milestone, not a separate pre-step).**
Run the single simplest fixture (icon sketch) all the way from normalized image through contour extraction, simplification, curve fitting, to a rasterized-and-diffed SVG, with node-count reporting. This is a hard go/no-go checkpoint: it calibrates the real estimate for milestone 3 rather than treating vectorization like a normally-sizable feature. If the locked clean-trace bar isn't reachable on even the simplest case in the time budgeted, that's the moment to renegotiate scope — not three weeks before ship.

**Milestone 3 — Production vectorization + before/after review.**
Full actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine` covering all four fixture classes (icon, lettering, closed-shape, messy notebook), named progress stages, and the before/after review screen. Acceptance bar is the locked one: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, continuous strokes where expected, correct winding/fill, clean downstream-open in a real vector tool. Explicit rule carried from round 1 and reaffirmed by both agents: if this milestone underperforms, fix the tracing heuristics — do not compensate by growing editor scope.

**Milestone 4 — Bounded cleanup + export.**
Select/delete path, debounced simplify-strength slider (downsampled preview, full-res on commit), merge paths, undo/redo through `EditEngine.apply` as a pure reducer, VoiceOver-accessible path list, and the SVG/PDF export sheet. Deliberately the smallest, lowest-risk milestone by design, since the edit surface is narrow and operates on an already-stable `VectorDocument`. Can start against milestone 1's fake services in parallel with milestone 3 if vectorization is running long.

**Milestone 5 — Integration hardening and trust polish.**
Recents home, reopen/delete flows, resume banners (with `acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()` wired so banners show once), export-failure recovery, settings/privacy copy, full empty/loading/success/error coverage, and the complete kill-and-relaunch matrix proven end-to-end as a system (not just per-checkpoint unit tests). This is also where `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` gets finalized against actual API usage and real-device performance/thermal validation (debounced slider, Vectorize under load) happens on physical hardware, not just simulator.

**Lanes:** dependency-aligned, not screen-aligned — (1) models/persistence/staging/reconciliation, (2) capture/normalize/gate, (3) vectorization/export correctness, (4) SwiftUI screens/router/accessibility on top of the other three's contracts. Integration and checkpoint verification run continuously starting in milestone 1, never as an end-of-project phase.

**Core risk framing both agents converged on:** this app's real danger isn't a missing feature, it's internal state silently meaning something other than what the code assumes — on the lifecycle axis (a captured-but-not-gated folder that isn't what its checkpoint stage implies) and on the quality axis (a photo that passes the gate but shouldn't have). Both get mitigated the same way: make the ambiguous states impossible to represent (staging vs. real `TraceDocument`) or impossible to miss (fixture-set thresholds tested against deliberately bad inputs, not just good ones).

## Phase Purpose

Produce milestones, sequencing, estimates, dependencies, and a risk-managed delivery plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Delivery Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

The plan should be built around the truth that this app only works if trace quality and recovery feel trustworthy by the second and third use, not just in a happy-path demo. For the actual user here, the worst outcome is not “missing a nice extra feature,” it is spending three minutes photographing, waiting, cleaning up, and then getting a junk SVG or losing work after a background interruption. So I would sequence the build around proving the hard pipeline early, then wrapping it in the UI and persistence that make it feel safe.

First milestone is the shared foundation and non-negotiable contracts: create the app target plus the package boundaries already locked, wire `CleanlineModels`, `AppModel`, `TraceSession`, `TraceStore`, and the route model, and stand up the per-trace file package with atomic checkpoint writes, OS-default backup, and recents rebuild scanning. This milestone also includes fixture ingestion and a skeletal test harness, because if the fixtures and checkpoint tests do not exist immediately, the rest of the build will drift into opinion. Dependency-wise, everything else sits on this. Verification here is not visual; it is file-level: create a trace folder, write each checkpoint, simulate interrupted writes, reopen, and confirm `AppRouter.route(for:)` and interruption recovery state behave deterministically.

Second milestone is the ingest and quality-gate loop, because there is no point building editing chrome before the app can honestly accept or reject a sketch. That means camera/import intake, orientation-safe `CapturedImage` handling, normalization, EXIF stripping, the seven fixed rejection reasons, and the combined quality-gate/correction screen with explicit reject/retry/continue states. I would insist that the first real user-visible slice ends here: import a fixture or photo, get either a named rejection or a corrected preview, and survive permission denial cleanly. The biggest risk in this milestone is false acceptance: passing bad input into later stages and making the eventual trace failure look like a vectorization problem. Mitigation is fixture-based thresholds and deliberately bad images for every rejection reason, not just “looks okay on a couple photos.”

Third milestone is the vectorization subsystem and review flow, and this is where I would spend the most schedule weight. Build the actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine`, stage reporting, preview generation, and the before/after review screen. Do not try to chase “smart editing” to compensate for a weak trace engine. The acceptance bar here is concrete: the four locked fixtures produce usable `VectorDocument`s with bounded nodes, no tiny orphan fragments, continuous strokes where expected, and exports that open downstream. If this milestone underperforms, the right response is to tune simplification, contour cleanup, and fitting heuristics, not to sneak scope into the editor. Verification needs both unit tests and manual downstream-open checks in a real vector tool.

Fourth milestone is bounded cleanup and export. Only after the trace output is already close should the team add select/delete path, merge paths, simplify-strength adjustment with debounced preview and full-res commit, undo/redo, the accessible path list, and the export sheet for SVG/PDF. The dependency here is total: cleanup logic should operate on a stable `VectorDocument`, not on a moving target while vectorization is still being reinvented. The risk is interaction slop, especially around slider lag, selection mistakes, and accessibility fallback being nominal rather than real. Mitigation is to keep the edit surface narrow, put all mutation through `EditEngine.apply`, and verify with real VoiceOver usage and gesture-contract UI tests instead of assuming labels are enough.

Fifth milestone is integration hardening and trust polish: recents home, saved trace reopen/delete, resume banners, export-failure recovery, settings/privacy copy, empty/loading/error states, and kill-and-relaunch behavior across all four committed checkpoints. This cannot be left as “polish at the end” because interruption recovery is part of the product promise. I would budget explicit repair time here for the ugly stuff that emerges only once the full loop exists: stale recents metadata, duplicated banners, wrong reopen route, share-sheet failure edge cases, and state mismatches after undo/redo plus backgrounding.

If I were assigning lanes, I would keep them dependency-aligned rather than screen-aligned. One lane owns models and persistence from day one. One lane owns capture, normalization, and gate classification. One lane owns vectorization and export correctness. One lane owns SwiftUI screens, router flow, and accessibility integration on top of those contracts. Integration starts in milestone one and never stops; there should be no separate “now combine everything” week.

The biggest objection to this plan is that it delays the satisfying visual workspace until the middle, which can feel slow. I still think that is correct. A beautiful compare/edit screen over a weak or dishonest pipeline is wasted effort and will force rework. The detail I would preserve in the final phase output is this sequencing rule: do not let editor scope or UI polish become a substitute for trace quality, and do not postpone checkpoint/recovery verification until the end. If the task-assignment phase keeps those two constraints intact, the plan is executable.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Given how much is already locked in tech_specs, my job this round is to turn a static contract into an actual build order with dependency reality and the failure modes that show up specifically in a checkpoint/resume, file-backed, no-network system. A few things stand out to me that the plan needs to get right structurally, not just schedule around.

The dependency graph is steeper than it looks. CleanlineModels has zero logic but everything else compiles against it, so it's milestone zero and nothing else starts in earnest until its types are frozen — which they effectively are, from tech_specs. After that, CleanlinePersistence (TraceStore, atomic checkpoint writer) has to come before almost anything else, because Capture, Normalize, QualityGate, Vectorize, Edit, and Export are all specified in terms of checkpointed state, and AppModel/TraceSession are literally defined as thin orchestrators over TraceStore. If Persistence is built last or treated as "just file I/O, easy," the whole app has to be rewired later. I'd put Persistence, including the atomic temp-file-then-rename mechanism and the kill-mid-write test, as its own early milestone with a hard exit criterion: a test that kills the process mid-write and asserts the prior checkpoint is untouched has to pass before any other lane is allowed to build against TraceStore for real.

The single biggest schedule risk is VectorizationEngine, and I think the plan needs to say that explicitly rather than sizing it like a normal package. Contour cleanup, stroke-continuity heuristics, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, and winding/fill classification are algorithmically the hardest part of this app, and "hits the locked clean-trace bar on the four fixtures" is not a feature you can size until you've tried it once. I'd carve out a time-boxed spike before committing the vectorization milestone to a fixed estimate — get one fixture (the icon sketch, the simplest case) through the full contour-to-SVG path early, rasterize-diff it, and use that to calibrate the estimate for lettering and closed-shape cases, which fail differently (stroke continuity vs. accidental fill). If the spike shows the bar isn't reachable in the time budgeted, that's the point to renegotiate scope, not three weeks before ship.

Because Vectorize is high-risk and slow, the plan should deliberately unblock everything downstream of it with fakes. CaptureService, ImportService, NormalizationService, QualityGateService, and VectorizationEngine are all protocols specifically so that Edit, Export, and the UI/Features layer can build and test against fixture-backed fake implementations returning canned VectorDocuments while the real vectorization work is still in progress. I'd make "fake implementations of every service protocol, backed by the fixture set" an explicit early deliverable, not an incidental side effect — it's what lets four lanes run in parallel instead of stacking behind the hardest one.

On fixture data specifically: the four locked fixtures plus the per-rejection-reason bad-photo set plus the adversarial nested-loop case are not free — someone has to physically produce these (draw them, photograph them under controlled skew/shadow/blur conditions), and QualityGate and Vectorize tests are both blocked on them existing. That data-collection work has real lead time and no dependency on any code being written, so it should start in parallel with Models/Persistence, not after QualityGate is "in progress."

I want to flag a real gap in the locked contract before it becomes a build-time surprise: TraceStage.captured is written the instant bytes exist, specifically so that "resume-during-gate-evaluation" works — but nothing in the interfaces names the function that actually does that reconciliation. If the app is killed in the narrow window between the captured checkpoint landing on disk and the gate resolving (accept-and-persist vs. reject-and-delete), the next launch has to find that orphaned folder and either re-run the gate against it or clean it up — otherwise the "no ghost folder ever" guarantee tech_specs makes is only true in the happy path. This needs a named startup reconciliation routine (something like AppModel scanning for folders stuck at .captured with no resolved TraceDocument, re-running the gate on relaunch) and it needs its own kill-test in the scripted kill-and-relaunch matrix, one per TraceStage plus this specific mid-gate-evaluation case. I'd rather this get named and owned now than get discovered as a flaky bug report during QA.

Checkpoint/resume in general shouldn't be one line item at the end — it's cross-cutting and emergent from how AppModel, TraceSession, and TraceStore interact, so it needs its own integration milestone that runs continuously from the point Persistence is stable, with a fixed test matrix: kill at captured, at normalized, at traced, at edited, and at the gate-evaluation gap above, for both camera and import sources.

On non-functional scope: there's no auth surface at all in this app and the plan should say so explicitly so nobody drifts into building account/login scaffolding "just in case." But PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy is real, near-certain App Store gate work — file-timestamp access for Recents sorting and likely UserDefaults both trigger required-reason declarations even with zero third-party SDKs — so I'd schedule a small, explicitly-owned privacy-manifest task that gets revisited each time a new API surface is touched, not reconstructed once at the end from memory of what got used. Similarly, device-performance validation (debounced simplify slider on real older-device hardware, thermal behavior during Vectorize) needs actual device time on the calendar, not just simulator runs, since that's exactly the class of bug simulators don't reproduce.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Both sides landed on essentially the same shape for how to sequence this build, and it's a shape that makes sense: don't build the pretty compare/edit screen first, prove the hard, trust-critical stuff first. Codex laid out five milestones — shared foundation/persistence contracts, then capture+quality-gate, then vectorization+review (the one to spend the most schedule weight on), then bounded cleanup+export, then integration/trust hardening (recents, resume banners, kill-and-relaunch, settings) — and was explicit that editor polish must never become a substitute for trace quality, and that checkpoint/recovery testing can't be pushed to the end. Claude agreed with that ordering but sharpened the dependency graph underneath it: Persistence has to be genuinely solid (atomic writes, kill-mid-write test passing) before any other lane builds against `TraceStore` for real, and because `VectorizationEngine` is the single biggest schedule risk, Claude wants a time-boxed spike (get the simplest fixture through the full pipeline early) to calibrate the estimate before committing to it, plus fake/protocol-backed implementations of every service so the UI/Edit/Export lanes can build in parallel instead of stacking behind vectorization. Claude also flagged fixture/test-photo production as real lead-time work that should start immediately, in parallel with Models/Persistence, and called out that there's no auth surface in this app (so nobody should build login scaffolding), while `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` work and real-device perf/thermal testing need to be explicitly scheduled rather than assumed away.

The one thing that's a genuine, not-yet-answered gap rather than just an add-on: Claude found a real hole in the locked contract — if the app is killed in the narrow window between the `captured` checkpoint landing on disk and the quality gate resolving (accept vs. reject-and-delete), nothing in the interfaces names the function that reconciles that orphaned folder on relaunch. Claude wants a named startup reconciliation routine plus its own dedicated kill-test in the interruption-recovery matrix, on top of the four checkpoint-stage kill tests already implied. This came in after Codex's round-1 message, so Codex hasn't had a chance to weigh in on it, and it's a real architectural loose end (not just a nice-to-have), so I don't think we can call this fully closed yet.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs Codex to react to two things specifically: (1) the mid-gate-evaluation orphan-folder reconciliation gap and whether it becomes a named routine + its own kill-test in the plan, and (2) whether the vectorization time-boxed spike, fixture-production-as-parallel-lane, and fake-service-backed unblocking of downstream lanes get folded into the milestone/dependency structure as explicit plan items rather than implementation-phase details. Everything else — the five-milestone sequencing, the "don't let editing/polish substitute for trace quality" rule, dependency-aligned (not screen-aligned) lanes, and continuous integration/checkpoint testing instead of end-of-project polish — looks like real, stated agreement between both active participants.

### Round 2


**Codex (Delivery Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Claude’s orphan-folder point is real, and I don’t think we should treat it like implementation trivia because it changes the delivery plan. If `captured` really is written before the gate resolves, then “rejected captures never leave a ghost trace” is only true if we explicitly schedule startup reconciliation as a first-class job, with an owner and a test matrix. Otherwise we’re hand-waving the exact kind of trust break this app can’t afford: user gets interrupted at the wrong second, relaunches, and now sees either a phantom trace or lost work with no explanation. I’d fold that directly into milestone one, not milestone five. Persistence isn’t “trace store plus atomic writes”; it’s trace store plus reconciliation rules for incomplete folders, including the mid-gate case. Concretely, I want a named startup sweep that scans any folder stuck at `captured` without a resolved accepted document state, re-runs the gate if enough data exists, or deletes it deterministically if it was a rejected transient. And I want kill tests for five interruption points, not four: captured, normalized, traced, edited, and the gate-evaluation gap Claude called out. If we don’t prove that early, every later milestone is building on undefined recovery behavior.

I also think Claude is right that we should stop pretending vectorization is a normal feature estimate. It is the schedule risk. So I’d change the plan from “milestone three: vectorization” to “milestone two-a: vectorization spike, milestone three: production vectorization.” The spike is not throwaway research; it has a hard deliverable: one simplest fixture, full path from normalized image to downstream-openable SVG, with raster diff and node-count reporting. That gives us a reality check on whether the locked quality bar is reachable with the current heuristics. And yes, I’d make fake service implementations an explicit milestone-one deliverable, not a convenience. If the UI lane and edit/export lane can’t run against canned `VectorDocument`s and canned gate results while the real engine is still maturing, we’ve accidentally serialized the whole project behind the hardest subsystem. That’s bad planning, not bad luck.

So the shape I’d lock now is: first, models, persistence, startup reconciliation, fixture ingestion, fake services, and checkpoint/kill tests. Second, real capture/import plus normalization/gate, while in parallel running the vectorization spike on the simplest fixture. Third, commit the production vectorization milestone only after the spike tells us what we’re actually dealing with; if the spike exposes a serious miss on stroke continuity or fill classification, we burn time there before touching editor scope. Fourth, bounded cleanup and export on top of stable vector documents, still backed by the fake services where needed so the UI lane keeps moving. Fifth, integration hardening, recents, reopen flows, resume banner behavior, share-sheet failure, settings/privacy manifest, and real-device performance/thermal validation. I want fixture production called out as day-one parallel work too, because the bad-photo set and notebook-paper cases are not optional garnish; they define whether the quality gate is honest. The main rule I’m pushing here is simple: we do not let the team hide uncertainty in vectorization or interruption recovery behind “we’ll polish it later.” Those two things are the app.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex, I like the five-milestone shape a lot, and I think the ordering argument — prove the pipeline is honest before you make it pretty — is exactly right and I'm not going to relitigate that. What I want to push on is folding my two open items into concrete, owned deliverables inside your milestones rather than leaving them as "things Claude flagged," because right now they'd fall through the cracks between "milestone 1 is persistence" and "milestone 2 is the gate."

First, I want to sharpen the orphan-folder thing because on a re-read I think it's worse than I said in round 1, not just a missing helper function. Look at the two notes on TraceStage and TraceStatus side by side: TraceStage says "'captured' is written the instant bytes exist (so resume-during-gate-evaluation works)," which implies there's a persisted, resumable checkpoint before the gate has run. But TraceStatus says "a document only exists with this status after the quality gate has passed — rejected captures are deleted, never persisted with any status." TraceStatus only has three cases — needsReview, exported, traceFailed — there's no "pending gate evaluation" state. And TraceStore.writeCheckpoint takes a full TraceDocument as a required parameter for every stage including .captured. So which is it — does writeCheckpoint(.captured, ...) construct a real TraceDocument with some status value that doesn't actually describe reality, or is there a lighter-weight staging mechanism that predates the TraceDocument concept entirely? As written, both notes can't be literally true at once, and if the persistence lane and the capture/gate lane each build to a different one of these two readings, we get two incompatible on-disk formats for the exact same five-second window in the app's life. That's precisely the kind of thing that looks fine in every demo because nobody kills the app in a five-second window on purpose, and then shows up as a corrupted-Recents bug report three weeks after ship.

My proposed resolution, concretely: don't add a case to TraceStatus this late, and don't force writeCheckpoint to accept a half-meaningful TraceDocument for the pre-gate state. Instead, the captured bytes get written to a staging location — same CapturedImage payload, but keyed by a provisional trace ID, no TraceDocument, no Recents visibility — and a real TraceDocument only gets constructed and handed to TraceStore.writeCheckpoint once the gate has actually resolved to accepted. That matches the TraceStatus note exactly ("never persisted with any status" on rejection) and it makes the reconciliation routine's job trivial to specify: on launch, AppModel scans the staging location, not the Recents folder structure, finds any provisional captures still sitting there, and just re-runs the gate against each one — accept-and-promote-to-a-real-TraceDocument, or reject-and-delete-the-staging-entry. That's a small, nameable function with a one-line contract, and it slots as an explicit exit criterion on your milestone one, Codex — right alongside the kill-mid-write test you already scoped there. I don't think this is a new milestone, it's a sharper spec for a corner of milestone one that's currently underspecified, and I'd rather we nail it down now than have the persistence and gate lanes independently invent two versions of "what a captured-but-not-yet-gated trace looks like on disk."

On your false-acceptance point in milestone two — I actually think that's the same category of risk as my orphan-folder one, just on the "quality" axis instead of the "lifecycle" axis: both are cases where the system's internal bookkeeping says one thing happened but reality is subtly different, and it only surfaces downstream where it's expensive to debug. That reinforces my instinct that this app's real risk profile isn't "feature X is missing," it's "state Y silently doesn't mean what the code assumes it means." Worth keeping that framing in the final plan explicitly, honestly, as the connective tissue between milestone-1 persistence rigor and milestone-2 gate rigor.

On the coordinator's second question — yes, I want the vectorization spike, fixture production, and fake-service scaffolding named as explicit plan items, not implementation-detail texture. Concretely: fixture production (the four locked images, the seven bad-photo cases, the adversarial nested-loop case) starts on day one, fully parallel with Models/Persistence, owned by whoever's free first, because it has zero code dependency and everything in milestones two and three blocks on it existing. The fake-service implementations — CaptureService/ImportService/QualityGateService/VectorizationEngine fakes backed by those same fixtures, returning canned but realistic VectorDocuments — I'd place as an exit deliverable of milestone one alongside the persistence contract, built by the models/persistence lane since they already own the shared types those fakes return. That's what actually lets Codex's milestone-four cleanup-and-export lane and the UI/router lane start meaningfully before real vectorization exists, instead of everyone rediscovering that dependency mid-project. And the vectorization spike — one fixture, the icon sketch, all the way through to a rasterized, diffed SVG — I'd put as the literal first task inside milestone three, before we commit to a time estimate for the rest of that milestone, not as a separate pre-milestone step. If that spike blows past whatever we budget for "simplest case," that's the signal to renegotiate scope on lettering and closed-shape handling immediately, while there's still runway, rather than discovering it when we're already committed to a ship date.

For rough sizing, since the plan needs estimates and not just ordering: milestone one (models, persistence, staging/reconciliation, fixture production, fakes) is the one I'd give the most calendar buffer relative to its apparent simplicity, because the staging-vs-TraceDocument ambiguity above needs to actually get resolved and tested, not just coded — call it a solid chunk of the early schedule even though "it's just file I/O." Milestone two (capture/gate) is comparatively bounded — it's real work but it's classic, testable, deterministic logic against fixtures. Milestone three (vectorization) is the one where I'd refuse to commit a number until the spike reports back — it could be the single largest or second-largest milestone in the whole project depending on what the spike shows. Milestone four (edit/export) should be small if three actually lands the acceptance bar, because the edit surface is deliberately narrow and EditEngine.apply is a pure reducer — that's a controlled, low-risk milestone by design. Milestone five (integration/trust hardening) I'd budget generously too, because it's where the kill-test matrix, resume banners, and the staging-reconciliation edge case all get proven together for the first time as a system rather than as isolated unit tests, and that's exactly where second-order interactions between lanes tend to surface.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**
