**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Round 2 actually closed this out. Codex took both open items from round 1 and didn't just acknowledge them — he restructured the milestone plan around them: the orphan-folder reconciliation gap moved from "worth flagging" to an explicit exit criterion of milestone one (a named startup sweep, five kill-test points instead of four), and vectorization got split into "milestone two-a: spike on the simplest fixture" followed by "milestone three: production," with fake service implementations and fixture production promoted to explicit milestone-one deliverables rather than implementation texture.

Claude then went one level deeper and found something concrete rather than just re-flagging the same worry: the locked interfaces actually contradict themselves on this exact point — `TraceStage`'s note says `captured` is persisted the instant bytes exist (implying a resumable checkpoint pre-gate), but `TraceStatus`'s note says a document only exists once the gate passes, and there's no "pending gate" status case, and `writeCheckpoint` requires a full `TraceDocument` for every stage including `.captured`. Both can't be literally true. Claude's fix is concrete and small: captured bytes land in a staging location keyed by a provisional ID (no `TraceDocument`, no Recents visibility) until the gate resolves; only then does a real `TraceDocument` get constructed and handed to `TraceStore`. That makes the reconciliation sweep trivial to spec (scan staging, not Recents, on launch; re-run the gate or delete). Codex hasn't typed a reaction to this specific mechanism yet since it came after his message, but it's not a new disagreement — it's a direct, literal answer to the "named startup sweep" he asked for, using a resolution method that doesn't touch or conflict with anything he said. Both agents also aligned on relative sizing: milestone one gets more buffer than its "just file I/O" reputation suggests because this staging ambiguity has to be actually resolved and tested; milestone three is unsized until the spike reports back; milestone four is deliberately small given the narrow edit surface; milestone five gets generous room because that's where kill-tests, resume banners, and staging-reconciliation all get proven together as a system for the first time.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Locked delivery plan for cleanline, five milestones, dependency-ordered (not screen-ordered):**

**Milestone 1 — Foundation: models, persistence, staging/reconciliation, fixtures, fakes.**
Build `CleanlineModels` (frozen from tech_specs), `AppModel`/`TraceSession`/`AppRouter` skeletons, and `TraceStore` with atomic temp-file-then-rename checkpoint writes. Critically, this milestone also resolves and implements the staging mechanism for pre-gate captures: captured bytes are written to a staging location keyed by a provisional ID, with no `TraceDocument` and no Recents visibility, and a real `TraceDocument` is only constructed once the quality gate resolves to accepted. A named startup reconciliation sweep scans staging (not the Recents folder tree) on every launch, and either re-runs the gate against any leftover provisional capture (promoting to a real document on accept) or deletes it deterministically (on reject or on missing/corrupt data). Exit criteria for this milestone: a kill-mid-write test proving the prior checkpoint survives untouched, plus five interruption-point kill tests — captured, normalized, traced, edited, and the mid-gate-evaluation staging gap — all passing before any other lane is allowed to build against `TraceStore` for real. This milestone also delivers, as explicit deliverables (not side effects): fixture production (the four locked sketches, the seven-reason bad-photo set, the adversarial nested-loop case) starting day one since it has zero code dependency, and fake/protocol-backed implementations of every service (`CaptureService`, `ImportService`, `QualityGateService`, `VectorizationEngine`) returning canned-but-fixture-realistic results, so downstream lanes never serialize behind the hardest subsystem. No auth/login scaffolding — there is none in this app.

**Milestone 2 — Capture, import, normalization, quality gate.**
Real camera/import intake, orientation-safe `CapturedImage` handling into the staging mechanism from milestone 1, normalization (perspective, EXIF strip), and the seven named rejection reasons with the combined quality-gate/correction screen (checking / rejected / corrected-preview-ready / correction-failed / resuming-from-checkpoint variants). First fully user-visible slice: import a fixture or real photo, get either a named rejection or a corrected preview, survive camera-permission denial cleanly. Mitigation against false acceptance (bad input silently passing the gate and surfacing later as a "vectorization problem"): threshold tuning validated against the full bad-photo fixture set, not just a couple of good photos.

**Milestone 2a — Vectorization spike (first task inside the vectorization milestone, not a separate pre-step).**
Run the single simplest fixture (icon sketch) all the way from normalized image through contour extraction, simplification, curve fitting, to a rasterized-and-diffed SVG, with node-count reporting. This is a hard go/no-go checkpoint: it calibrates the real estimate for milestone 3 rather than treating vectorization like a normally-sizable feature. If the locked clean-trace bar isn't reachable on even the simplest case in the time budgeted, that's the moment to renegotiate scope — not three weeks before ship.

**Milestone 3 — Production vectorization + before/after review.**
Full actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine` covering all four fixture classes (icon, lettering, closed-shape, messy notebook), named progress stages, and the before/after review screen. Acceptance bar is the locked one: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, continuous strokes where expected, correct winding/fill, clean downstream-open in a real vector tool. Explicit rule carried from round 1 and reaffirmed by both agents: if this milestone underperforms, fix the tracing heuristics — do not compensate by growing editor scope.

**Milestone 4 — Bounded cleanup + export.**
Select/delete path, debounced simplify-strength slider (downsampled preview, full-res on commit), merge paths, undo/redo through `EditEngine.apply` as a pure reducer, VoiceOver-accessible path list, and the SVG/PDF export sheet. Deliberately the smallest, lowest-risk milestone by design, since the edit surface is narrow and operates on an already-stable `VectorDocument`. Can start against milestone 1's fake services in parallel with milestone 3 if vectorization is running long.

**Milestone 5 — Integration hardening and trust polish.**
Recents home, reopen/delete flows, resume banners (with `acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()` wired so banners show once), export-failure recovery, settings/privacy copy, full empty/loading/success/error coverage, and the complete kill-and-relaunch matrix proven end-to-end as a system (not just per-checkpoint unit tests). This is also where `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` gets finalized against actual API usage and real-device performance/thermal validation (debounced slider, Vectorize under load) happens on physical hardware, not just simulator.

**Lanes:** dependency-aligned, not screen-aligned — (1) models/persistence/staging/reconciliation, (2) capture/normalize/gate, (3) vectorization/export correctness, (4) SwiftUI screens/router/accessibility on top of the other three's contracts. Integration and checkpoint verification run continuously starting in milestone 1, never as an end-of-project phase.

**Core risk framing both agents converged on:** this app's real danger isn't a missing feature, it's internal state silently meaning something other than what the code assumes — on the lifecycle axis (a captured-but-not-gated folder that isn't what its checkpoint stage implies) and on the quality axis (a photo that passes the gate but shouldn't have). Both get mitigated the same way: make the ambiguous states impossible to represent (staging vs. real `TraceDocument`) or impossible to miss (fixture-set thresholds tested against deliberately bad inputs, not just good ones).

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

The plan is concrete, dependency-ordered, and directly resolves the round-1 open items rather than papering over them. It covers all four required outputs: five explicit milestones with clear entry/exit criteria, a dependency-aligned lane structure (not screen-aligned) with a stated rule that integration/checkpoint verification runs continuously from milestone 1 (not deferred to the end), named risk mitigations (staging vs. `TraceDocument` ambiguity resolved architecturally, false-acceptance risk mitigated via full bad-photo fixture testing, vectorization risk bounded by a time-boxed spike with an explicit go/no-go), and a verification plan with concrete kill-test points (five, not four), fixture-based acceptance criteria, and real-device perf/thermal validation called out separately from simulator testing.

It correctly incorporates the substantive disagreement/discovery from this phase — the real contradiction between `TraceStage`'s and `TraceStatus`'s notes in the locked interfaces — and gives it a concrete, small, buildable resolution (provisional staging location keyed by ID, no `TraceDocument` until gate acceptance) rather than hand-waving a "reconciliation function" without specifying what it reconciles. It also correctly declines to relitigate settled architecture and stays scoped to sequencing, consistent with this phase's purpose.

Minor note for the next phase (task assignment), not a blocker: the plan explicitly flags that Codex has not yet typed literal confirmation of the staging-mechanism resolution, though it argues persuasively that it's a non-conflicting direct answer to Codex's own ask. Task assignment can proceed on this as locked, since CONSENSUS: YES was declared and the reasoning for why it's not a live dispute is well-argued and specific.

This is ready to hand off to task assignment.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Round 2 actually closed this out. Codex took both open items from round 1 and didn't just acknowledge them — he restructured the milestone plan around them: the orphan-folder reconciliation gap moved from "worth flagging" to an explicit exit criterion of milestone one (a named startup sweep, five kill-test points instead of four), and vectorization got split into "milestone two-a: spike on the simplest fixture" followed by "milestone three: production," with fake service implementations and fixture production promoted to explicit milestone-one deliverables rather than implementation texture.

Claude then went one level deeper and found something concrete rather than just re-flagging the same worry: the locked interfaces actually contradict themselves on this exact point — `TraceStage`'s note says `captured` is persisted the instant bytes exist (implying a resumable checkpoint pre-gate), but `TraceStatus`'s note says a document only exists once the gate passes, and there's no "pending gate" status case, and `writeCheckpoint` requires a full `TraceDocument` for every stage including `.captured`. Both can't be literally true. Claude's fix is concrete and small: captured bytes land in a staging location keyed by a provisional ID (no `TraceDocument`, no Recents visibility) until the gate resolves; only then does a real `TraceDocument` get constructed and handed to `TraceStore`. That makes the reconciliation sweep trivial to spec (scan staging, not Recents, on launch; re-run the gate or delete). Codex hasn't typed a reaction to this specific mechanism yet since it came after his message, but it's not a new disagreement — it's a direct, literal answer to the "named startup sweep" he asked for, using a resolution method that doesn't touch or conflict with anything he said. Both agents also aligned on relative sizing: milestone one gets more buffer than its "just file I/O" reputation suggests because this staging ambiguity has to be actually resolved and tested; milestone three is unsized until the spike reports back; milestone four is deliberately small given the narrow edit surface; milestone five gets generous room because that's where kill-tests, resume banners, and staging-reconciliation all get proven together as a system for the first time.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Locked delivery plan for cleanline, five milestones, dependency-ordered (not screen-ordered):**

**Milestone 1 — Foundation: models, persistence, staging/reconciliation, fixtures, fakes.**
Build `CleanlineModels` (frozen from tech_specs), `AppModel`/`TraceSession`/`AppRouter` skeletons, and `TraceStore` with atomic temp-file-then-rename checkpoint writes. Exit criteria for this milestone: a kill-mid-write test proving the prior checkpoint survives untouched, plus five interruption-point kill tests — captured, normalized, traced, edited, and the mid-gate-evaluation staging gap — all passing before any other lane is allowed to build against `TraceStore` for real. No auth/login scaffolding — there is none in this app.

**Milestone 2 — Capture, import, normalization, quality gate.**

First fully user-visible slice: import a fixture or real photo, get either a named rejection or a corrected preview, survive camera-permission denial cleanly. Mitigation against false acceptance (bad input silently passing the gate and surfacing later as a "vectorization problem"): threshold tuning validated against the full bad-photo fixture set, not just a couple of good photos.

**Milestone 2a — Vectorization spike (first task inside the vectorization milestone, not a separate pre-step).**
Run the single simplest fixture (icon sketch) all the way from normalized image through contour extraction, simplification, curve fitting, to a rasterized-and-diffed SVG, with node-count reporting. This is a hard go/no-go checkpoint: it calibrates the real estimate for milestone 3 rather than treating vectorization like a normally-sizable feature. If the locked clean-trace bar isn't reachable on even the simplest case in the time budgeted, that's the moment to renegotiate scope — not three weeks before ship.

**Milestone 3 — Production vectorization + before/after review.**
Full actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine` covering all four fixture classes (icon, lettering, closed-shape, messy notebook), named progress stages, and the before/after review screen. Acceptance bar is the locked one: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, continuous strokes where expected, correct winding/fill, clean downstream-open in a real vector tool. phase_discussions.title: Task Assignments
phase_discussions.output:
This round is where it actually clicks shut. Codex came in and, instead of doubling down on his own coarser 12-task cut, endorsed Claude's fine-grained 29-task backlog outright — he even posted the same task list back, task-for-task, as his round-2 answer. His pushback landed on two specific tightenings rather than the granularity fight: making `App/AppModel.swift`/`TraceSession.swift`/`AppRouter.swift` an explicit `primary_ui`-only forbidden zone (services can define protocols, they don't reach into session orchestration), and reinforcing that nobody "just tweaks a field" in another lane's `CleanlineModels` file — contract changes get raised in-thread and made by the owning lane. He also introduced a better branching idea: a continuous `cleanline/integration` branch that all four lane branches merge into constantly, instead of only meeting each other at milestone boundaries.

Claude took that branch idea and improved it rather than just accepting it — keep `cleanline/integration` as the continuous collision-catcher, but gate `main` itself on milestone-boundary promotion out of `integration` once that milestone's exit criteria actually pass, so `main` still means "this milestone is proven," not just "it compiled." Claude also called out a real self-contradiction worth fixing: don't make "project wiring" (`.pbxproj`, root `Package.swift`) an owned task assigned to one lane, because that quietly makes whichever lane holds it the permanent integrator by construction — instead it's a standing, continuous integrator responsibility checked at every merge into `integration`, never a discrete deliverable. Both agents ended up in the same place on this without Codex needing to re-type agreement, since Codex's own round-2 tasks-json already reflects the 29-task structure with no separate "project wiring" task in it.

Testing ownership also landed clean: each `services_utilities` package tests its own behavior as part of the task that introduces it, `primary_ui` owns view-state/navigation correctness including fake-backed demoability, `polish_resilience` owns only cross-cutting system verification (kill/relaunch matrix, accessibility, privacy manifest, device perf, state-coverage audit) and never patches another lane's file directly — it files bugs back to the owner. `data_domain` owns schema stability and Codable round-trip tests.

Gemini's still unreachable, but between the two active participants there's no live disagreement left — Codex explicitly signed off on the fine-grained list, tightened two forbidden zones Claude adopted, proposed the integration-branch model Claude then refined, and Claude's harder critiques were aimed at resolving contradictions in earlier drafts, not at anything still standing in the final proposal.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Location:** `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cleanline/` — self-contained, one Xcode project, no sharing with sibling portfolio apps.

**Lane responsibilities:**
- `data_domain` — pure value types in `CleanlineModels` only. No logic. If a task needs logic, it isn't this lane's task.
- `services_utilities` — every protocol-backed engine: capture, import, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit-reducer, export, persistence/`TraceStore`, plus the fixture-backed fakes. Owns its own unit/fixture tests as part of each task, not as separate QA.
- `primary_ui` — `AppModel`, `TraceSession`, `AppRouter`, everything under `Features/`, and `CleanlineDesignSystem`. Owns view-state and navigation-correctness testing for what it builds.
- `polish_resilience` — cross-cutting resilience/accessibility types, fixture *production* (not fake services), resume-banner wiring, and all system-level verification: kill-and-relaunch matrix, accessibility passes, privacy manifest, device performance, and state-coverage audit. Never patches another lane's implementation file directly — files it back to the owner instead.

**Forbidden edit zones:**
- `App/AppModel.swift`, `App/TraceSession.swift`, `App/AppRouter.swift` — `primary_ui` only, no exceptions once T-006 lands.
- `Packages/CleanlineVectorize`, `CleanlineEdit`, `CleanlineExport`, `CleanlinePersistence`, `CleanlineCapture`, `CleanlineNormalize`, `CleanlineQualityGate` — `services_utilities` only.
- `Tests/CleanlineIntegrationTests`, `CleanlineUITests`, `CleanlinePerformanceTests`, `CleanlineStateCoverageTests`, `App/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, `Fixtures/` — `polish_resilience` only.
- `Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/*` — shared directory, but ownership resolved by exact filename (T-001/T-002/T-003 split), never by directory. No lane edits another lane's file in there; contract changes get raised in-thread, the owning lane makes the edit.
- `Cleanline.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` and root `Package.swift` — not owned by any single lane as a deliverable task. Treated as a standing integrator responsibility (rotates, doesn't accrue permanently to one lane), touched only during continuous merges into `cleanline/integration`, never edited opportunistically mid-task.

**Branching & merge strategy:** `cleanline/main` stays always-buildable and only advances via milestone-boundary promotion from `cleanline/integration` once that milestone's locked exit criteria pass. Each lane has a long-lived branch (`lane/data-domain`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/polish-resilience`); tasks branch off as `task/T-0NN-slug`, merge back into their lane branch when acceptance criteria pass, and lane branches merge continuously into `cleanline/integration` — that's where cross-lane breakage surfaces early, not at a big-bang milestone merge.

**Communication protocol:** post "claiming T-0xx, editing [files]" on task start; raise contract concerns before writing code, not after; every handoff states what's stable, what's fake-backed, and what's still blocked. Daily check isn't "what did you do," it's "did any file boundary or dependency assumption become false" — if yes, stop and resolve before continuing.

**Conflict-prevention plan:** file-level ownership eliminates almost all contention by construction; the one shared directory (`CleanlineModels`) is split by exact filename, not folder; the one real hotspot (`project.pbxproj`) is handled as a continuous integrator sweep rather than a lane-owned task; edit/export are deliberately unblocked against fakes (T-008) rather than real vectorization so the riskiest subsystem (T-014→T-015) never becomes a serialization bottleneck for the rest of the build.

**Acceptance checks:** all 29 tasks have exactly one owning lane, `depends_on` is acyclic, and T-001 and T-007 have zero dependencies so build workers can start immediately.

{"id":"T-001","title":"CleanlineModels: core data-domain types","owner_lane":"data_domain","files":["Packages/CleanlineModels/Package.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/TraceDocument.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/TraceStatus.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/TraceStage.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/WorkspaceMode.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/QualityGateResult.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/RejectionReason.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/VectorDocument.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/VectorPath.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/PathSegment.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/EditCommand.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/ExportFormat.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/ExportStatus.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/NewTraceOutcome.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/TraceMetadata.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/CaptureSource.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/CapturedImage.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/NormalizedImage.swift"],"depends_on":[],"acceptance_criteria":["All types compile with the exact signatures locked in tech_specs","Codable round-trip unit tests pass for TraceDocument and VectorDocument","Package builds standalone with zero dependency on any other Cleanline package"],"status":"pending"},
{"id":"T-002","title":"Cross-cutting resilience & accessibility shared types","owner_lane":"polish_resilience","files":["Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/ProcessingStage.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/InterruptionRecoveryState.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/PipelineError.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/AccessibilityPathAction.swift"],"depends_on":["T-001"],"acceptance_criteria":["Compiles as sibling files in the same target without modifying any T-001 file","PipelineError enumerates every stage failure named in tech_specs","ProcessingStage.allCases order matches the locked sequence exactly"],"status":"pending"},
{"id":"T-003","title":"Services-lane shared value types: QualityMetrics, ExportArtifact","owner_lane":"services_utilities","files":["Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/QualityMetrics.swift","Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/ExportArtifact.swift"],"depends_on":["T-001"],"acceptance_criteria":["Compiles alongside T-001/T-002 files with zero edits to either","QualityMetrics carries all five locked metric fields","ExportArtifact exposes a file URL, never raw Data"],"status":"pending"},
{"id":"T-004","title":"CleanlineDesignSystem: tokens and shared components","owner_lane":"primary_ui","files":["Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Package.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/Tokens.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/StatusChip.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/PathCanvas.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/ToolStrip.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/SegmentedToggle.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/RejectionCard.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/PrimaryButton.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/SecondaryButton.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/SourceCard.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/RecentTraceRow.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/ProcessingStageStack.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/ExportOptionRow.swift","Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/Sources/CleanlineDesignSystem/EmptyStateHero.swift"],"depends_on":["T-001"],"acceptance_criteria":["Color/typography/spacing tokens match the locked values exactly (paper #F6F1E7, ink #17140F, teal #2F6F63, orange #C1531B)","PathCanvas is the single shared render surface reused by both Compare and Edit modes","Every component holds up at accessibility Dynamic Type sizes without truncation","No gradients or heavy drop shadows anywhere in the component set"],"status":"pending"},
{"id":"T-005","title":"CleanlinePersistence: TraceStore, atomic checkpoints, staging/reconciliation","owner_lane":"services_utilities","files":["Packages/CleanlinePersistence/Package.swift","Packages/CleanlinePersistence/Sources/CleanlinePersistence/TraceStore.swift","Packages/CleanlinePersistence/Sources/CleanlinePersistence/AtomicCheckpointWriter.swift","Packages/CleanlinePersistence/Sources/CleanlinePersistence/StagingReconciliation.swift","Packages/CleanlinePersistence/Tests/CleanlinePersistenceTests/AtomicWriteTests.swift","Packages/CleanlinePersistence/Tests/CleanlinePersistenceTests/StagingReconciliationTests.swift"],"depends_on":["T-001","T-002"],"acceptance_criteria":["Kill-mid-write test proves the prior checkpoint survives untouched","Staging mechanism matches the locked design exactly: provisional-ID keyed staging, no TraceDocument or Recents visibility before the gate resolves","Startup reconciliation sweep scans staging only and deterministically promotes-on-accept or deletes-on-reject","listAllMetadata rebuilds purely by scanning per-trace folders, never trusting a separate index"],"status":"pending"},

{"id":"T-007","title":"Fixture production: locked sketches, bad-photo set, adversarial case","owner_lane":"polish_resilience","files":["Fixtures/Good/icon-sketch.*","Fixtures/Good/lettering.*","Fixtures/Good/closed-shape.*","Fixtures/Good/messy-notebook.*","Fixtures/Rejections/skewTooHigh.*","Fixtures/Rejections/shadowCoverageTooHigh.*","Fixtures/Rejections/blurTooHigh.*","Fixtures/Rejections/contrastTooLow.*","Fixtures/Rejections/sketchTooFaint.*","Fixtures/Rejections/noSketchRegionDetected.*","Fixtures/Rejections/ruledPaperInterference.*","Fixtures/Adversarial/nested-closed-loops.*","Fixtures/README.md"],"depends_on":[],"acceptance_criteria":["Four good fixtures span the four locked acceptance-bar classes","One dedicated bad-photo fixture per each of the seven named rejection reasons, with the induced condition documented in the README","Nested-closed-loop adversarial case present for winding-rule testing"],"status":"pending"},

## Phase Purpose

Produce concrete workstreams, file ownership, forbidden edit zones, branch names, merge strategy, testing responsibilities, communication protocol, and a conflict-prevention plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

The app should live as its own project root at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cleanline`. Inside that root, keep the split already implied by the architecture contract: `App/`, `Features/`, `Packages/`, `Tests/`, `Fixtures/`, and project-level files like `Cleanline.xcodeproj` and `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`. I would keep branch names lane-scoped and short: `cleanline/data-foundation`, `cleanline/ui-flow`, `cleanline/services-pipeline`, and `cleanline/polish-hardening`. Nobody should work directly on `main`. Merge strategy should be short-lived feature branches into an integration branch `cleanline/integration`, then fast-forward or squash into `main` only after the checkpoint matrix and fixture tests are green. The important detail to preserve is that this app is not organized by screens first; it is organized around state ownership and checkpoint safety first, because that is where the real failure risk lives.

File ownership needs to be strict or the lanes will collide immediately. `data_domain` owns only shared models and fixture metadata surfaces: `Packages/CleanlineModels/**`, fixture manifests, and any schema docs for on-disk state. `services_utilities` owns all implementation behind protocols and all file-system behavior: `Packages/CleanlinePersistence/**`, `Packages/CleanlineCapture/**`, `Packages/CleanlineNormalize/**`, `Packages/CleanlineQualityGate/**`, `Packages/CleanlineVectorize/**`, `Packages/CleanlineEdit/**`, `Packages/CleanlineExport/**`, plus raw fixture assets under `Fixtures/**`. `primary_ui` owns `App/**`, `Features/**`, `Packages/CleanlineDesignSystem/**`, and routing/navigation composition. `polish_resilience` owns cross-cutting verification and hardening files: `Tests/**`, `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, accessibility helpers if they are not already defined in `CleanlineDesignSystem`, kill-test harnesses, and any recovery-state audit tooling. Forbidden edit zones matter: `primary_ui` must not edit persistence or vectorization implementations, `services_utilities` must not change route/view state shapes in `App/` or `Features/`, `data_domain` must not smuggle behavior into model types beyond obviously local helpers, and only `polish_resilience` should touch the end-to-end test harness once it exists. The only shared-edit files should be the Xcode project, package manifests, and CI/test wiring, and those should be changed by the integrator in a merge PR, not ad hoc by all lanes.

The cleanest handoff model is this: `data_domain` freezes model contracts first and becomes mostly read-only after that except for additive fixes. `services_utilities` builds against those contracts and exposes stable protocol implementations plus fakes. `primary_ui` consumes only protocols and model values, never concrete service internals. `polish_resilience` starts early, not last: they define the fixture matrix, interruption matrix, and acceptance assertions while the others are still building. That gives an immediate conflict-prevention rule: if a lane thinks it needs to change a shared type, it proposes the contract change before editing downstream files. If a lane can solve its problem locally without changing a shared type, it must do that instead. Communication should be lightweight but explicit: each lane posts when starting a task, when changing any shared interface, and when handing off a file area. Every handoff should state three things only: what changed, what is now stable, and what still cannot be relied on. The biggest risk in my own plan is that the Xcode project and package wiring become a silent bottleneck; to avoid that, one integrator should own target membership and package graph updates, and everyone else should avoid casual `.pbxproj` churn.

Testing responsibility should track failure modes, not code ownership vanity. `services_utilities` is responsible for unit tests on normalization, gate classification, edit reducer behavior, export validity, and vectorization heuristics. `data_domain` is responsible for serialization round-trip tests and invariants on model evolution. `primary_ui` is responsible for route resolution, state restoration presentation, and view-state behavior with fake services. `polish_resilience` is responsible for the kill-and-relaunch matrix, VoiceOver path-list pass, permission-denied flows, real-device performance checks, and the final downstream-open/export verification. Merge gates should be simple: no merge to `cleanline/integration` unless the task’s acceptance criteria are locally satisfied and the owner declares which fixture or recovery path they verified. No merge to `main` unless all lane-owned tests pass together and the five interruption points still pass after integration.

The final phase output needs to preserve one hard rule above everything else: nobody is allowed to compensate for weak vectorization or weak lifecycle recovery by broadening editor scope or adding UI chrome. If a trace is bad, the fix belongs in the gate/vectorization lane; if a resume path is flaky, the fix belongs in persistence/session ownership. That rule is what keeps parallel work from drifting into four inconsistent products.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Closer) — Round 1**

Right, my job this round is to turn the locked five-milestone plan into an actual work-assignable backlog with hard file ownership, because the whole plan falls apart if two lanes think they own the same `.swift` file or the same Xcode target registration. Let me just decide this outright rather than leave it soft.

**Where this lives:** `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cleanline/` — one self-contained Xcode project folder, nothing shared with sibling portfolio apps. Root layout matches tech_specs exactly: `Cleanline.xcodeproj`, `App/`, `Packages/*` (one local SPM package per module boundary), `Features/*` (one folder per screen), `Tests/*`, `Fixtures/*`.

**The one real hotspot, solved up front:** `Cleanline.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` and the workspace-level package references are the single file every lane would otherwise fight over, because every new package/target needs registering into the app target. I'm making that file integrator-owned, full stop — no task edits it directly. Whoever's playing integrator this build (rotates by milestone, but one person at a time) does a short daily sweep: pull in any new `Packages/*` folder a task created, register its target, done. Because SPM auto-includes new `.swift` files dropped into an already-declared package's `Sources/` directory without touching `Package.swift` again, this means after a package's *first* task creates `Package.swift`, every later task adding files to that same package (like T-002 and T-003 both adding files into `CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/`) never touches the manifest — they just add sibling files. That's what makes the three-way split of `CleanlineModels` across data_domain/polish_resilience/services_utilities lanes (all owning different *types*, same package) safe: file-level ownership stays non-overlapping even though package-level ownership is shared, because each lane's task list names distinct filenames within that directory, never the same file twice.

**Lane responsibilities, mapped straight off tech_specs' own `owning_lane` tags rather than inventing a new split:**
- `data_domain` owns the pure value types in `CleanlineModels` — no logic, ever. If a `data_domain` task needs logic, that's a sign it belongs in `services_utilities` instead.
- `services_utilities` owns every protocol-backed engine: capture, import, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit-reducer, export, and persistence/`TraceStore`. This is where the actual hard work and actual risk live — vectorization especially.
- `primary_ui` owns `AppModel`/`TraceSession`/`AppRouter` and every SwiftUI screen under `Features/`, plus the shared `CleanlineDesignSystem` component package.
- `polish_resilience` owns the cross-cutting resilience/accessibility types (`ProcessingStage`, `InterruptionRecoveryState`, `PipelineError`, `AccessibilityPathAction`), fixture production, the resume-banner wiring, and — critically — all integration/accessibility/performance/state-coverage *test* targets. That lane never patches another lane's implementation file directly; if it finds a bug during verification, it files it back to the owning lane. That's the rule that keeps "testing is everyone's job" from turning into "everyone edits everyone's files."

**Branching:** `main` stays always-buildable. Each lane gets a long-lived integration branch (`lane/data-domain`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/polish-resilience`); each task branches off its lane branch as `task/T-0NN-slug`, PRs back into the lane branch when its own acceptance criteria pass, and the integrator merges lane branches into `main` at each milestone boundary (end of M1, end of M2/M2a, end of M3, end of M4, end of M5) after confirming that milestone's exit criteria — not continuously, so nobody's mid-milestone half-state leaks into `main` and blocks another lane.

**Forbidden zones, stated plainly:** nobody outside `services_utilities` touches anything under `Packages/CleanlineVectorize`, `CleanlineEdit`, `CleanlineExport`, `CleanlinePersistence`, `CleanlineCapture`, `CleanlineNormalize`, `CleanlineQualityGate`. Nobody outside `primary_ui` touches `App/AppModel.swift`, `App/TraceSession.swift`, `App/AppRouter.swift`, or anything under `Features/`. Nobody outside `polish_resilience` touches `Tests/CleanlineIntegrationTests`, `Tests/CleanlineUITests`, `Tests/CleanlinePerformanceTests`, `Tests/CleanlineStateCoverageTests`, `App/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, or `Fixtures/`. The one deliberate exception is the shared `CleanlineModels` directory, resolved above by filename, not by lane.

**Sequencing that actually respects the locked plan's risk framing:** milestone 1's real exit gate isn't "package compiles," it's the kill-mid-write test plus the staging/reconciliation mechanism actually working — I've made that its own task (T-005) with that as literal acceptance criteria, not an incidental detail. The vectorization spike is its own gating task (T-014) that production vectorization (T-015) depends on, so nobody commits to the full engine before the spike reports back. Edit and export (T-018, T-019) are deliberately wired to depend only on the fakes (T-008), not on real vectorization, so that lane isn't stuck waiting behind the riskiest subsystem — which is the entire point of building fakes as a first-class milestone-1 deliverable instead of a convenience.

**Biggest risk in my own plan:** it's 29 tasks, which is a lot of surface area for four lanes, and if the integrator sweep on `project.pbxproj` lags even a day, tasks that just landed can't actually build in `main` even though their own package compiles standalone. The mitigation is that milestone-boundary merges are hard checkpoints, not "whenever's convenient" — if a lane's work can't register into the app target within a milestone window, that's escalated immediately, not discovered at the next milestone's kickoff.

**Testing ownership, concretely:** every `services_utilities` package carries its own `Tests/<Package>Tests` colocated in the same package, owned by the same lane that wrote the implementation — that's not `polish_resilience`'s job. `polish_resilience` only owns tests that cross package/screen boundaries: the full kill-and-relaunch matrix, VoiceOver/Dynamic Type/Reduce Motion verification, real-device perf, and the empty/loading/success/error coverage sweep across all screens. That division means there's never a question of who writes a failing test for a given bug.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at how round 1 played out: Codex kicked off with a first-pass mapping of the locked five milestones onto lane-owned tasks (the tail I can see covers Recents Home, capture/normalize/gate, processing, vectorization, workspace, edit, export, accessibility/privacy — a leaner ~12-task cut). Claude then went substantially further rather than just agreeing — turning that into a full 29-task backlog with hard file-level ownership, a named integrator-owned hotspot (`project.pbxproj`, swept by a rotating single owner at milestone boundaries so no two lanes fight over target registration), a lane-branch + task-branch model with merges gated at milestone boundaries, explicit forbidden-edit zones per lane, and a rule that `polish_resilience` never patches another lane's implementation directly — only files bugs back. Claude also made sure every locked risk item from the prior phases has its own task and acceptance criteria: the staging/reconciliation mechanism (T-005), the vectorization spike as a hard go/no-go gate before production vectorization (T-014 → T-015), edit/export explicitly unblocked against fakes rather than waiting on real vectorization (T-018/T-019 depending on T-008 not T-015), the five-point kill-and-relaunch matrix (T-025), and the once-only resume banner (T-024).

That's a real, substantive expansion of what Codex put on the table — not a restatement — and Codex hasn't had a turn to react to the specific granularity, the forbidden-zone rules, or the branching/merge cadence yet. Gemini is still unavailable. So this isn't closed out yet; it reads as Claude's strong opening bid for how the backlog should look, not confirmed agreement between both active participants.

### Round 2


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Claude’s 29-task cut is the right shape. I don’t want to collapse it back down to a prettier 12-task list, because the dangerous parts of this app are exactly the parts that disappear inside coarse tasks: staging-vs-durable persistence, once-only recovery banners, fake-backed UI progress, and the difference between “vector export exists” and “SVG/PDF correctness is actually testable.” If we make those implicit, we guarantee cross-lane drift. So I’m signing off on the fine-grained backlog, with two constraints tightened from a frontend/state-management angle.

First, the forbidden-zone rule is correct, including the part Claude gave `polish_resilience`: they should not patch another lane’s feature file directly. That sounds annoying until you look at what goes wrong otherwise. If resilience patches `TraceWorkspace` to “fix” a resume-banner race while `primary_ui` is simultaneously changing mode-switch state, we end up with a ghost fix that passes one test run and corrupts the ownership model. Same reason I want one more explicit forbidden-edit zone: `App/AppModel.swift`, `App/TraceSession.swift`, and `App/AppRouter.swift` belong to `primary_ui` alone once T-006 starts. Services can define protocols and contracts; they do not reach into session orchestration. That boundary matters because offline behavior, resume routing, and shared canvas state are product behavior, not just plumbing. Likewise, `Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/*` need to stay lane-owned exactly as already split in T-001/T-002/T-003; nobody “just tweaks a field” in another lane’s model file during implementation. If a lane needs a contract change, they raise it in-thread, and the owning lane makes the edit. That is the only way we avoid three silent schema forks.

Second, I want the merge protocol made explicit enough that workers can actually start without stepping on each other. The app should live at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cleanline`, and everyone branches from `cleanline/main`. Long-lived lane branches are `lane/data-domain`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/services-utilities`, and `lane/polish-resilience`. Actual work happens on short task branches like `task/T-006-app-shell` forked from the lane branch, merged back into the lane branch by the lane owner, then lane branches merge into `cleanline/main` only at milestone boundaries after the dependency gate passes. `project.pbxproj` stays effectively integrator-owned even if one person physically edits it during a milestone sweep; nobody opportunistically edits target membership mid-task unless their task explicitly requires new files to compile, and if it does, they queue those additions into the current sweep owner’s batch instead of hand-merging blind. Communication should be dead simple: every task start posts “claiming T-0xx, editing [files]”; every contract concern posts before code, not after; every handoff includes what is stable, what is fake-backed, and what is still blocked. Daily sync question is not “what did you do,” it’s “did any owned file boundary or task dependency become false.” If yes, stop and resolve that first. That’s the conflict-prevention plan in practice.

Testing responsibility also needs to stay with the lane that creates the risk, not dumped onto resilience at the end. `services_utilities` owns unit and fixture tests for persistence, normalization, gate, vectorization, edit, and export as part of the task that introduces the behavior. `primary_ui` owns view-state and navigation correctness for the screens and shell they build, including fake-backed demos before real services land. `polish_resilience` owns cross-cutting system verification: kill/relaunch matrix, accessibility passes, privacy manifest audit, state coverage audit, and device perf validation. `data_domain` owns codable round-trip and schema stability. If everybody follows that, the build workers can start immediately and nobody is waiting for a mystery “QA pass” to discover structural bugs two milestones late.

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Closer) — Round 2**

Codex, I read your cut and the coordinator's right that you and I converged on the same five-milestone spine but landed on very different granularity, and I want to push back hard on the granularity choice specifically, not just wave it through for the sake of converging fast.

Look at your T-003: it bundles `Fixtures/**`, `CleanlineCapture/**`, `CleanlineQualityGate/**`, and `CleanlineVectorize/**` into one task called "Produce locked fixture set and fake service implementations." Those are four completely different things with four completely different timelines — fixtures have zero code dependency and can start today, capture is a bounded AVFoundation wrapper, quality-gate is deterministic threshold logic, and vectorize is the thing your own prose calls out as needing a spike-then-commit gate. If I assign that as one task to one build-worker, either they block on the slowest piece (vectorize) before reporting anything done, or they silently only do the easy 80% and the acceptance criteria stop meaning anything specific. That's exactly the "hides uncertainty in vectorization behind polish" failure mode you yourself warned about in the project_plan phase — except here it's happening at the task-granularity level, not the milestone level. I'd rather have four small tasks with sharp acceptance criteria than one big task where "done" is ambiguous.

Same problem, more serious, in your T-007: "Spike and then implement production vectorization engine" as a single task with a single acceptance-criteria list that mixes "simplest icon fixture completes end-to-end spike" with "production vectorization covers all locked fixture classes." The whole point of the locked project_plan — the thing both of us signed off on in the prior phase — was that the spike is a hard go/no-go checkpoint specifically so the team can renegotiate scope *before* committing to the production estimate. If it's one task, there's no natural place to stop, report the spike's actual result, and decide whether lettering/closed-shape/messy-notebook are even reachable in the time budgeted. You'd be building the exact ambiguity back into the schedule that the plan phase spent two rounds removing. I've got this split as T-014 (spike, go/no-go, blocking) and T-015 (production, depends_on T-014) specifically so that gate is structural, not just a note in the description.

And here's the one I think actually matters most for how we avoid stepping on each other: your T-012, "Integrate project wiring, target membership, and final branch stabilization," assigns `Cleanline.xcodeproj/**`, `Package.swift`, and `App/**` to `polish_resilience` as an owned task. But your own prose two paragraphs earlier says "one integrator should own target membership and package graph updates, and everyone else should avoid casual `.pbxproj` churn" — which I read the same way, and said explicitly in my own round 1. If you put that file ownership inside a `polish_resilience`-lane task in the backlog, you've made `polish_resilience` the permanent integrator by construction, not a rotating role, and you've also just told `primary_ui` they can't touch `App/**` even though `App/AppModel.swift`/`TraceSession.swift`/`AppRouter.swift` are explicitly `primary_ui`-owned everywhere else in both our plans. That's a direct contradiction inside your own task list. I think the fix is: don't put project-wiring in the task backlog as a lane deliverable at all — it's a standing cross-cutting responsibility that whoever's integrator-for-the-week does continuously, and it doesn't need its own acceptance criteria beyond "the project builds," which is already implicitly checked by every other task's "compiles" criterion.

Where I do want to move toward you: your `cleanline/integration` branch is a genuinely better idea than what I proposed last round, and I'll say so plainly since you asked me to actually change my mind when someone's got the better answer. I had lane branches merging straight to `main` at milestone boundaries only, which means nothing gets integration-tested together until a whole milestone's worth of work lands at once — that's a big-bang integration risk sitting right in the middle of the plan we just spent two rounds trying to de-risk. Your model is better: task branches merge into their lane branch, lane branches merge continuously into `cleanline/integration`, and *that's* where cross-lane breakage gets caught early and often. The only change I'd make is keeping `main` itself gated on milestone-boundary promotion from `cleanline/integration` — so `main` stays the "this milestone's exit criteria are proven" line, while `cleanline/integration` is where the actual day-to-day collision-catching happens. That gives us both things: your continuous cross-lane visibility and my discipline about what's allowed to call itself a milestone-complete `main`.

So here's where I land, concretely, and I think this closes it: keep my 29-task backlog with its file-level ownership (because ambiguous multi-package tasks are a real risk, not pedantry), adopt your `cleanline/integration` branch as the continuous merge target for all four lane branches, gate `main` on milestone-boundary promotion out of `cleanline/integration` once that milestone's exit criteria pass, and drop the idea of a discrete "project wiring" task entirely — it's a standing integrator responsibility that touches `project.pbxproj`/root `Package.swift` only, checked at every merge into `cleanline/integration`, never assigned to a single lane as owned deliverable work. If you're fine with that split, I think this phase is done — the remaining difference between our two lists isn't a disagreement about direction, it's my list being the direct execution of the same principles your prose already states, just carried all the way through the task boundaries instead of stopping at five milestones with twelve tasks.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This round is where it actually clicks shut. Codex came in and, instead of doubling down on his own coarser 12-task cut, endorsed Claude's fine-grained 29-task backlog outright — he even posted the same task list back, task-for-task, as his round-2 answer. His pushback landed on two specific tightenings rather than the granularity fight: making `App/AppModel.swift`/`TraceSession.swift`/`AppRouter.swift` an explicit `primary_ui`-only forbidden zone (services can define protocols, they don't reach into session orchestration), and reinforcing that nobody "just tweaks a field" in another lane's `CleanlineModels` file — contract changes get raised in-thread and made by the owning lane. He also introduced a better branching idea: a continuous `cleanline/integration` branch that all four lane branches merge into constantly, instead of only meeting each other at milestone boundaries.

Claude took that branch idea and improved it rather than just accepting it — keep `cleanline/integration` as the continuous collision-catcher, but gate `main` itself on milestone-boundary promotion out of `integration` once that milestone's exit criteria actually pass, so `main` still means "this milestone is proven," not just "it compiled." Claude also called out a real self-contradiction worth fixing: don't make "project wiring" (`.pbxproj`, root `Package.swift`) an owned task assigned to one lane, because that quietly makes whichever lane holds it the permanent integrator by construction — instead it's a standing, continuous integrator responsibility checked at every merge into `integration`, never a discrete deliverable. Both agents ended up in the same place on this without Codex needing to re-type agreement, since Codex's own round-2 tasks-json already reflects the 29-task structure with no separate "project wiring" task in it.

Testing ownership also landed clean: each `services_utilities` package tests its own behavior as part of the task that introduces it, `primary_ui` owns view-state/navigation correctness including fake-backed demoability, `polish_resilience` owns only cross-cutting system verification (kill/relaunch matrix, accessibility, privacy manifest, device perf, state-coverage audit) and never patches another lane's file directly — it files bugs back to the owner. `data_domain` owns schema stability and Codable round-trip tests.

Gemini's still unreachable, but between the two active participants there's no live disagreement left — Codex explicitly signed off on the fine-grained list, tightened two forbidden zones Claude adopted, proposed the integration-branch model Claude then refined, and Claude's harder critiques were aimed at resolving contradictions in earlier drafts, not at anything still standing in the final proposal.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Location:** `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cleanline/` — self-contained, one Xcode project, no sharing with sibling portfolio apps.

**Lane responsibilities:**
- `data_domain` — pure value types in `CleanlineModels` only. No logic. If a task needs logic, it isn't this lane's task.
- `services_utilities` — every protocol-backed engine: capture, import, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit-reducer, export, persistence/`TraceStore`, plus the fixture-backed fakes. Owns its own unit/fixture tests as part of each task, not as separate QA.
- `primary_ui` — `AppModel`, `TraceSession`, `AppRouter`, everything under `Features/`, and `CleanlineDesignSystem`. Owns view-state and navigation-correctness testing for what it builds.
- `polish_resilience` — cross-cutting resilience/accessibility types, fixture *production* (not fake services), resume-banner wiring, and all system-level verification: kill-and-relaunch matrix, accessibility passes, privacy manifest, device performance, and state-coverage audit. Never patches another lane's implementation file directly — files it back to the owner instead.

**Forbidden edit zones:**
- `App/AppModel.swift`, `App/TraceSession.swift`, `App/AppRouter.swift` — `primary_ui` only, no exceptions once T-006 lands.
- `Packages/CleanlineVectorize`, `CleanlineEdit`, `CleanlineExport`, `CleanlinePersistence`, `CleanlineCapture`, `CleanlineNormalize`, `CleanlineQualityGate` — `services_utilities` only.
- `Tests/CleanlineIntegrationTests`, `CleanlineUITests`, `CleanlinePerformanceTests`, `CleanlineStateCoverageTests`, `App/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, `Fixtures/` — `polish_resilience` only.
- `Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/*` — shared directory, but ownership resolved by exact filename (T-001/T-002/T-003 split), never by directory. No lane edits another lane's file in there; contract changes get raised in-thread, the owning lane makes the edit.
- `Cleanline.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` and root `Package.swift` — not owned by any single lane as a deliverable task. Treated as a standing integrator responsibility (rotates, doesn't accrue permanently to one lane), touched only during continuous merges into `cleanline/integration`, never edited opportunistically mid-task.

**Branching & merge strategy:** `cleanline/main` stays always-buildable and only advances via milestone-boundary promotion from `cleanline/integration` once that milestone's locked exit criteria pass. Each lane has a long-lived branch (`lane/data-domain`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/polish-resilience`); tasks branch off as `task/T-0NN-slug`, merge back into their lane branch when acceptance criteria pass, and lane branches merge continuously into `cleanline/integration` — that's where cross-lane breakage surfaces early, not at a big-bang milestone merge.

**Communication protocol:** post "claiming T-0xx, editing [files]" on task start; raise contract concerns before writing code, not after; every handoff states what's stable, what's fake-backed, and what's still blocked. Daily check isn't "what did you do," it's "did any file boundary or dependency assumption become false" — if yes, stop and resolve before continuing.

**Conflict-prevention plan:** file-level ownership eliminates almost all contention by construction; the one shared directory (`CleanlineModels`) is split by exact filename, not folder; the one real hotspot (`project.pbxproj`) is handled as a continuous integrator sweep rather than a lane-owned task; edit/export are deliberately unblocked against fakes (T-008) rather than real vectorization so the riskiest subsystem (T-014→T-015) never becomes a serialization bottleneck for the rest of the build.

**Acceptance checks:** all 29 tasks have exactly one owning lane, `depends_on` is acyclic, and T-001 and T-007 have zero dependencies so build workers can start immediately.

## Feedback

This phase output meets the bar. All four required outputs are present and concrete: lane-by-lane responsibilities (data_domain/services_utilities/primary_ui/polish_resilience each with clear scope and testing ownership), explicit owned-files mapping via the 29-task backlog, a named forbidden-edit-zones list (App shell files restricted to primary_ui, each services package restricted to services_utilities, integration/test/privacy/fixture directories restricted to polish_resilience, CleanlineModels split by exact filename rather than directory, and .pbxproj/root Package.swift treated as a continuous integrator responsibility rather than an owned task), and a well-formed single tasks-json block.

Acceptance checks verified: every task has exactly one owner_lane drawn from the four valid lanes; dependencies are acyclic (each task's depends_on references only lower-numbered task IDs, which guarantees no cycles by construction); T-001 and T-007 have empty depends_on arrays, so build workers can start immediately in two lanes concurrently (data_domain on models, polish_resilience on fixture production) per the locked project_plan milestone-1 requirement that fixture work start day-one with zero code dependency.

The plan correctly carries forward every load-bearing decision from prior phases without re-litigating them: the staging/reconciliation split (T-005), the vectorization spike-then-production split (T-014/T-015) with the go/no-go framed as a recorded verdict not just a passing test, fake-service-backed unblocking of downstream lanes (T-008 gates T-013/T-016/T-017/T-018/T-019 for demoability ahead of real T-011/T-015), the five-point kill-and-relaunch matrix (T-025), interruption-recovery banner-once semantics (T-024), and the no-StoreKit/no-purchasable-surface constraint (T-023). It also resolves this phase's own live discussion point (branch/merge strategy, integrator-role-not-a-task for pbxproj) rather than leaving it dangling. This is ready to hand to build execution.

## Final Output

This round is where it actually clicks shut. Codex came in and, instead of doubling down on his own coarser 12-task cut, endorsed Claude's fine-grained 29-task backlog outright — he even posted the same task list back, task-for-task, as his round-2 answer. His pushback landed on two specific tightenings rather than the granularity fight: making `App/AppModel.swift`/`TraceSession.swift`/`AppRouter.swift` an explicit `primary_ui`-only forbidden zone (services can define protocols, they don't reach into session orchestration), and reinforcing that nobody "just tweaks a field" in another lane's `CleanlineModels` file — contract changes get raised in-thread and made by the owning lane. He also introduced a better branching idea: a continuous `cleanline/integration` branch that all four lane branches merge into constantly, instead of only meeting each other at milestone boundaries.

Claude took that branch idea and improved it rather than just accepting it — keep `cleanline/integration` as the continuous collision-catcher, but gate `main` itself on milestone-boundary promotion out of `integration` once that milestone's exit criteria actually pass, so `main` still means "this milestone is proven," not just "it compiled." Claude also called out a real self-contradiction worth fixing: don't make "project wiring" (`.pbxproj`, root `Package.swift`) an owned task assigned to one lane, because that quietly makes whichever lane holds it the permanent integrator by construction — instead it's a standing, continuous integrator responsibility checked at every merge into `integration`, never a discrete deliverable. Both agents ended up in the same place on this without Codex needing to re-type agreement, since Codex's own round-2 tasks-json already reflects the 29-task structure with no separate "project wiring" task in it.

Testing ownership also landed clean: each `services_utilities` package tests its own behavior as part of the task that introduces it, `primary_ui` owns view-state/navigation correctness including fake-backed demoability, `polish_resilience` owns only cross-cutting system verification (kill/relaunch matrix, accessibility, privacy manifest, device perf, state-coverage audit) and never patches another lane's file directly — it files bugs back to the owner. `data_domain` owns schema stability and Codable round-trip tests.

Gemini's still unreachable, but between the two active participants there's no live disagreement left — Codex explicitly signed off on the fine-grained list, tightened two forbidden zones Claude adopted, proposed the integration-branch model Claude then refined, and Claude's harder critiques were aimed at resolving contradictions in earlier drafts, not at anything still standing in the final proposal.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Location:** `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cleanline/` — self-contained, one Xcode project, no sharing with sibling portfolio apps.

**Lane responsibilities:**
- `data_domain` — pure value types in `CleanlineModels` only. No logic. If a task needs logic, it isn't this lane's task.
- `services_utilities` — every protocol-backed engine: capture, import, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit-reducer, export, persistence/`TraceStore`, plus the fixture-backed fakes. Owns its own unit/fixture tests as part of each task, not as separate QA.
- `primary_ui` — `AppModel`, `TraceSession`, `AppRouter`, everything under `Features/`, and `CleanlineDesignSystem`. Owns view-state and navigation-correctness testing for what it builds.
- `polish_resilience` — cross-cutting resilience/accessibility types, fixture *production* (not fake services), resume-banner wiring, and all system-level verification: kill-and-relaunch matrix, accessibility passes, privacy manifest, device performance, and state-coverage audit. Never patches another lane's implementation file directly — files it back to the owner instead.

**Forbidden edit zones:**
- `App/AppModel.swift`, `App/TraceSession.swift`, `App/AppRouter.swift` — `primary_ui` only, no exceptions once T-006 lands.
- `Packages/CleanlineVectorize`, `CleanlineEdit`, `CleanlineExport`, `CleanlinePersistence`, `CleanlineCapture`, `CleanlineNormalize`, `CleanlineQualityGate` — `services_utilities` only.
- `Tests/CleanlineIntegrationTests`, `CleanlineUITests`, `CleanlinePerformanceTests`, `CleanlineStateCoverageTests`, `App/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, `Fixtures/` — `polish_resilience` only.
- `Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/*` — shared directory, but ownership resolved by exact filename (T-001/T-002/T-003 split), never by directory. No lane edits another lane's file in there; contract changes get raised in-thread, the owning lane makes the edit.
- `Cleanline.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` and root `Package.swift` — not owned by any single lane as a deliverable task. Treated as a standing integrator responsibility (rotates, doesn't accrue permanently to one lane), touched only during continuous merges into `cleanline/integration`, never edited opportunistically mid-task.

**Branching & merge strategy:** `cleanline/main` stays always-buildable and only advances via milestone-boundary promotion from `cleanline/integration` once that milestone's locked exit criteria pass. Each lane has a long-lived branch (`lane/data-domain`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/polish-resilience`); tasks branch off as `task/T-0NN-slug`, merge back into their lane branch when acceptance criteria pass, and lane branches merge continuously into `cleanline/integration` — that's where cross-lane breakage surfaces early, not at a big-bang milestone merge.

**Communication protocol:** post "claiming T-0xx, editing [files]" on task start; raise contract concerns before writing code, not after; every handoff states what's stable, what's fake-backed, and what's still blocked. Daily check isn't "what did you do," it's "did any file boundary or dependency assumption become false" — if yes, stop and resolve before continuing.

**Conflict-prevention plan:** file-level ownership eliminates almost all contention by construction; the one shared directory (`CleanlineModels`) is split by exact filename, not folder; the one real hotspot (`project.pbxproj`) is handled as a continuous integrator sweep rather than a lane-owned task; edit/export are deliberately unblocked against fakes (T-008) rather than real vectorization so the riskiest subsystem (T-014→T-015) never becomes a serialization bottleneck for the rest of the build.

**Acceptance checks:** all 29 tasks have exactly one owning lane, `depends_on` is acyclic, and T-001 and T-007 have zero dependencies so build workers can start immediately.
