phase_discussions.title: App Store Readiness
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Codex was in cooldown and Gemini's CLI is still unavailable, so this phase closes on Claude's solo pass — but per this project's established pattern (used repeatedly in prior phases, e.g. human_qa_checklist), a thorough single-agent pass that hits every required deliverable and introduces no unresolved disagreement is enough to close rather than stall. There's nothing here to arbitrate: no second voice contested any of it.

**Honest launch-readiness verdict: NOT YET.** Build verification only proves `xcodebuild` produced a binary for the simulator. Nobody in this entire project — across build_coordination, build_verification, and human_qa_checklist — has actually launched the app and watched it run. The checklist from the prior phase was authored, not executed. This project's own history (missing `@main`, a black camera preview, a solid-fill bug on closed paths, a silent capture failure, a missing loading state) proves that code review and compilation both reliably miss exactly the class of bug that only surfaces on-screen. The real launch blocker, ranked above everything else below, is: **run the human QA checklist for real, on a physical device, before touching App Store Connect.** Everything that follows is prep work to have ready for when that pass is clean — not a signal that it's ready today.

**App Store positioning:** Lead with outcome, not mechanism — "photograph a paper sketch, get a usable vector file in minutes." Never market this as "AI-powered" or lean on Vision/ML as the guarantee (architecturally Vision alone was ruled insufficient, and overclaiming invites 1-star reviews the first time someone photographs blue ballpoint or shaded pencil and gets an honest rejection). State the scope fence explicitly in the listing copy — dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time — so a quality-gate rejection reads as honest product behavior to a browsing customer, not as broken software.

**Screenshot plan:** Real captured screenshots only, taken after real runtime verification — no mockups. Order: Recents populated with varied real thumbnails (not three identical fixtures), Camera capture mid-frame, Compare mode before/after at base zoom as the hero shot (this is the single screen the whole growth strategy leans on per the product brief), Edit mode mid-cleanup with the tool strip visible, one quality-gate rejection card (proves "honest, not gimmicky"), and the Export sheet. Cover Apple's required device sizes (minimum 6.9" and 6.5"/5.5" sets) — a missing required size is a mechanical rejection, not a judgment call.

**Privacy label notes:** Two separate deliverables, not one. `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` (already locked in architecture) declares required-reason API usage (file timestamps, UserDefaults) — that's a technical compliance artifact. The App Store Connect privacy questionnaire is the actual data-collection disclosure, and given zero analytics, zero network calls, zero accounts, zero third-party SDKs, that answer should genuinely be "Data Not Collected" across the board. Don't let whoever fills out the questionnaire conflate the two — using required-reason APIs doesn't obligate a "we collect data" answer.

**Permission copy:** `NSCameraUsageDescription` is a real requirement (live AVFoundation capture) and needs specific, true copy — "used to photograph your paper sketch for tracing," not boilerplate. `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` is conditional: if `ImportService` is pure PHPickerViewController with no direct Photos-authorization API calls, Apple doesn't require this string at all, and an unused one sitting in Info.plist is confusing at best. Verify what the actual `ImportService` implementation calls before locking this copy — don't assume.

**Subscription/paywall review risks:** No live purchase exists anywhere — clean under Guideline 3.1.1. But the "Pro — coming soon" settings block naming specific unreleased features (batch, fine-detail tracing, pro editing, cloud sync) is a real, named risk under Guideline 2.3.1 (accurate metadata / no reference to future or in-progress functionality). Mitigation: keep the language aspirational, not a feature roadmap; have a fallback ready (strip it to a single local-first privacy statement with zero forward-looking feature language) if this screen gets flagged in review.

**Launch blockers (concrete, unconfirmed either way — need a yes/no before submission, not an assumption):**
1. Real human QA execution on physical hardware — not yet done.
2. `AppIcon` asset — not a deliverable in any locked task; missing/placeholder icon is an automatic submission blocker.
3. Support URL + contact — required at App Store Connect listing time, unaddressed anywhere in the build; even a static page or support email suffices, but it must exist before submission.
4. `TARGETED_DEVICE_FAMILY` restriction to iPhone — unless `project.yml`/Info.plist explicitly restricts this, Xcode's default can make the app installable on iPad with an unadapted layout; this app did zero iPad work by design, so this needs an explicit check, not an assumption.
5. Age rating — should be a clean 4+ (no objectionable content, no UGC sharing, no web view, no ads, no third-party data sharing) but should be stated as a positive claim in the App Store Connect submission, not left implicit.

VERIFICATION: VERIFIED
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# cleanline — App Store Readiness

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 03:07:40._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-a2
Selected app slug: cleanline

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# cleanline

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

cleanline

## Category

Graphics & Design

## One-Sentence Promise

Photographs a hand-drawn sketch and traces it to clean, editable vector paths (SVG/PDF) via on-device Vision.

## Target User

Illustrators/print-on-demand sellers who sketch on paper but need vector output.

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 6 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 2 Food & Drink apps, 2 Graphics & Design apps, and 2 News apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 12 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 6, and build all 6 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Prepare launch readiness: App Store positioning, screenshots/storyboard needs, privacy nutrition labels, permission copy, subscription/paywall review risks, support/contact requirements, and release blockers.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

The thing I keep coming back to as I look at this whole chain of phases is that "launch readiness" and "runtime verification" have quietly become two different claims that are being treated as one. Build verification confirmed `xcodebuild` produced a binary for the simulator. The human QA checklist phase produced a genuinely good, thorough checklist. But nowhere in this transcript is there evidence that anyone actually launched the app once and tapped a button — the checklist was authored, not executed. That's the single most important thing I want on record before we write App Store copy: the honest launch-readiness verdict right now is "not ready," specifically because the app has never been observed running, and this project's own history (missing `@main`, black camera preview, solid-fill closed paths, silent capture failure, missing loading state) proves that code review and compilation both reliably miss exactly the kind of bug that only shows up on-screen. Writing polished screenshot plans and privacy labels for an app nobody has watched run is a real risk of shipping confidently into a wall. I want the final output to say this plainly rather than let App Store prep read as if it comes after a completed QA pass that hasn't actually happened.

On positioning: the copy has to hold the same line the whole build held — outcome, not mechanism. "Photograph a paper sketch, get a usable vector file in minutes" is fine; "AI-powered instant vectorizer" or anything implying magic, or naming Vision/ML explicitly as the guarantee, is not, both because it's inaccurate (Vision alone was explicitly ruled insufficient architecturally) and because it invites a 1-star pile-on the first time someone photographs blue ballpoint or shaded pencil and gets an honest rejection instead of a trace. The App Store description needs an explicit scope line — dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time — for the same reason the in-app copy has one: so refusal reads as honest product behavior, not broken software, in a one-star review context where nobody's read a design doc.

Screenshot plan: this is a Graphics & Design category listing, so the screenshots are the pitch. I'd want, in order: Recents populated with real varied thumbnails (not three identical fixture icons — that reads as fake), Camera capture mid-frame with a real sketch, the Compare mode before/after at base zoom (this is the one screen the entire growth strategy leans on per the product brief, so it needs to be the hero shot, not an afterthought), Edit mode showing the tool strip actually mid-cleanup, one quality-gate rejection card (proves the "honest, not gimmicky" positioning rather than hiding failure states), and the Export sheet with a real SVG icon. All of these need to come from actual captured screenshots post-runtime-verification, not mockups — and note Apple's current device-size requirements mean at minimum a 6.9" and a 6.5" (or 5.5") screenshot set; skipping a required size is a mechanical rejection, not a judgment call.

Privacy label needs a distinction drawn carefully because it's easy to conflate two different Apple requirements here. The `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` manifest (already locked as required in architecture) is about declaring required-reason API usage — file timestamps for Recents sorting, UserDefaults — and that's a technical compliance artifact, not a data-collection disclosure. The separate App Store Connect privacy "nutrition label" questionnaire is where the actual "Data Not Collected" claim goes, and given no analytics SDK, no network calls, no accounts, no third-party dependencies, that answer should genuinely be "Data Not Collected" across the board. The risk is if whoever fills out that questionnaire in App Store Connect doesn't realize using required-reason APIs doesn't obligate a "we collect data" answer — those are separate questions — and either over-discloses out of caution or under-discloses because the manifest already exists. I want both filled out correctly and separately, not assumed to be the same form.

Permission copy has a real technical nuance that could bite here: if intake is genuinely camera-plus-PHPickerViewController with no direct Photos-library authorization calls, Apple does not require an `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` string at all — PHPicker runs out-of-process specifically so apps can avoid that permission entirely. Earlier phases locked "camera/photo-library usage-description strings must be specific and true," which I'd now narrow: only include a photo-library usage string if the code actually calls an API that triggers the system permission prompt; if it's pure PHPicker, a photo-library string sitting unused in Info.plist is at best confusing and at worst something a reviewer flags as inconsistent with actual app behavior. Someone needs to check what `ImportService`'s real implementation calls before locking that copy. Camera needs a real, specific `NSCameraUsageDescription` — "used to photograph your paper sketch for tracing," not boilerplate — and that one's a genuine requirement since live capture goes through AVFoundation.

The subscription/paywall situation is where I think there's a sharper review risk than has been named so far. There is no live purchase anywhere — good, that's clean under Guideline 3.1.1. But the "Pro — coming soon" settings block, naming specific unreleased features (batch, fine-detail tracing, pro editing, cloud sync), is itself a review-risk pattern. Apple's Guideline 2.3.1 on accurate metadata has a real history of rejecting or flagging apps that reference future functionality, in-progress work, or "coming soon" placeholders, on the theory that the current build should be reviewed as it is, not as it will be. I'm not saying this will definitely get bounced, but it's a real, named risk that needs a mitigation on record rather than discovery at review time: keep the language aspirational and non-committal rather than a feature roadmap, avoid anything that reads as "you're paying for something today that's incomplete," and have a fallback ready (cut the block down to a single local-first privacy statement with zero forward-looking feature language) if this specific screen gets flagged in review.

A few concrete gaps nobody has confirmed exist or don't: an actual `AppIcon` asset — I don't see this as a deliverable in any locked task, and a missing or placeholder icon is an automatic submission blocker, not a soft one. A support URL and contact — App Store Connect requires this at listing time and nothing in the build addresses it; for a no-backend, no-account app this can be as simple as a static page or a support email, but it has to exist before submission, not be improvised at upload time. And device-family restriction: this app is locked iPhone-first with no iPad layout work done anywhere, but unless `project.yml`/Info.plist explicitly restricts `TARGETED_DEVICE_FAMILY` to iPhone, Xcode's default can quietly make the app installable on iPad with an unadapted, letterboxed, or broken layout — that's a real, checkable thing, not a hypothetical, and it needs a yes/no answer before submission rather than an assumption.

Age rating should be straightforward — 4+, no objectionable content, no user-generated content shared with others, no web view, no ads, no third-party data sharing — but I want that stated as a positive claim in the final output, not left implicit.

What the final output has to preserve, whatever else changes in discussion: an explicit, undodged statement that launch-readiness and runtime-verified-working are not yet the same claim for this app; the "outcome not mechanism, name the scope fence" positioning rule; the privacy-manifest-vs-privacy-label distinction stated as two separate deliverables, not one; the PHPicker permission-string caveat as something to verify against actual code before locking copy; the 2.3.1 "coming soon" risk with a concrete fallback wording, not just a flagged worry; and the app-icon, support-URL, and device-family-restriction items named as concrete unresolved launch blockers rather than assumed handled.

It also correctly carries forward this phase's discussion without softening it (single-agent pass acknowledged, no invented consensus). Nothing here contradicts prior locked decisions, and it leaves the next phase a usable, specific checklist rather than prose to reinterpret.

See the coordinator's message above.

phase_discussions.title: Final Review
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Only Claude weighed in this round — Codex was in cooldown and Gemini's CLI is still unavailable — but per this project's established pattern (used at every phase where a second voice didn't show up), a single thorough pass that hits every required output and doesn't hide anything is enough to close. There's no disagreement to resolve here since nobody else was in the room to push back.

**What was built:** A complete SwiftUI iOS MVP matching the locked spec end-to-end — camera/PHPicker intake, a seven-reason quality gate, correction review (perspective + threshold), a real on-device vectorization pipeline (Otsu thresholding, Zhang-Suen skeletonization, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Catmull-Rom curve fitting), a unified Compare/Edit Trace Workspace with the locked gesture contract, bounded cleanup (select/delete/merge/simplify/undo/redo) with a VoiceOver-accessible path list, SVG/PDF export with real vector output, a flat Recents list, checkpointed local persistence with interruption recovery, and a Settings screen with an honest non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" note. Seven Swift packages plus the app shell, all traceable back to the original prompt_contract and app_features decisions with nothing quietly dropped. Three rounds of adversarial code review during build_coordination caught and fixed real bugs (missing `@main`, black camera preview, closed-path solid-fill, silent capture failure, missing loading state).

**Verification status:** `xcodebuild` reports a clean compile for the iOS Simulator — a real state change from the prior phase's honest "UNVERIFIED-BY-INFRASTRUCTURE" call. That's taken at face value as this phase's authoritative result. But it proves the code typechecks and links, nothing more — nobody has yet launched the app and watched it run. The human_qa_checklist that exists for exactly this purpose was authored, never executed. Given this project's own track record — every real bug found so far was caught by reading runtime behavior or code line-by-line, never by a compile succeeding — build-complete and verified-working-on-a-device remain two different claims.

**Prompt coverage:** Full. The MVP scope from the original prompt (photograph a sketch, trace to editable vector paths, export SVG/PDF, local-first) is implemented completely, including the scope fences negotiated along the way (no node-editing, no batch, no cloud, no StoreKit, no library management — all deliberate non-goals, not gaps).

**Known limitations:**
- Human QA has never actually been run — camera capture, VoiceOver-only cleanup, Reduce Motion/Transparency, and the five-point kill-and-relaunch matrix are all unconfirmed in practice.
- T-025–T-029 (automated kill-and-relaunch suite, accessibility suite, device performance tests, state-coverage audit) were never built; the manual checklist is the only intended coverage for that ground, and even that hasn't been executed yet.
- `RecentsHomeState.loadError` is defined but unwired — a failed Recents load currently looks identical to an empty state.
- App Store submission has five unconfirmed items: AppIcon asset, `TARGETED_DEVICE_FAMILY` restricted to iPhone, support URL/contact, and age-rating/privacy-questionnaire answers stated explicitly.

**Top follow-ups, in order:**
1. Run the human QA checklist for real, on a physical device — this is the one gap that determines whether everything upstream is actually true in practice, not just on paper. One owner, one action: run it, fix whatever it finds.
2. In parallel (zero dependency on #1): add the AppIcon, set `TARGETED_DEVICE_FAMILY` explicitly, stand up a support URL/email, and write the age-rating and privacy-questionnaire answers as positive claims.
3. T-025–T-029 are explicitly deprioritized as deferred technical debt rather than an ambiguous "next phase" carry-forward — they're test-infrastructure hardening the manual QA pass substantially covers for a v1 ship.

**Bottom line:** Go for calling the build phase functionally complete against the MVP scope. No-go for App Store submission today. The distance between those two is exactly one QA pass that hasn't happened yet — not a caveat, a scheduled next action with nothing left to design or decide about it.

VERIFICATION: VERIFIED
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# cleanline — Final Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-07 03:09:39._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-cat-a2
Selected app slug: cleanline

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# cleanline

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

cleanline

## Category

Graphics & Design

## One-Sentence Promise

Photographs a hand-drawn sketch and traces it to clean, editable vector paths (SVG/PDF) via on-device Vision.

## Target User

Illustrators/print-on-demand sellers who sketch on paper but need vector output.

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 6 completely separate production-ready iOS apps at the same time: exactly 2 Food & Drink apps, 2 Graphics & Design apps, and 2 News apps.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 12 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 6, and build all 6 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Final review of completeness, consistency, risks, quality gates, and a clear go / no-go decision.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (Delivery Lead · Closer) — Round 1**

What's actually true right now, stripped of the optimistic framing that's crept into a couple of these phase summaries: the code is complete. Every package (Models, Persistence, Services, Vectorization, Edit, Export, DesignSystem) and every screen in the locked design exists with a real implementation, not a stub, and three rounds of adversarial code review caught and fixed the kind of bugs that actually matter (missing @main, black camera preview, closed-path solid-fill, silent capture failure). That's genuine progress and I'm not going to relitigate it. What's new since the last two phases is that the verification result at the top of this phase says VERIFIED via xcodebuild, compiled cleanly for the Simulator — that's a real state change from build_verification's honest "UNVERIFIED-BY-INFRASTRUCTURE" call. I'll take that at face value since it's the authoritative result for this phase. But I want to be precise about what it proves and doesn't: a clean simulator compile means the seven packages and the App target typecheck and link together. It does not mean anyone has watched the app run, tapped through the golden path, or confirmed the quality gate, Compare/Edit gesture contract, or interruption recovery behave the way the design and tech-spec phases locked them to behave. Those are different claims, and this project's own history is the proof — every real bug found so far was found by reading runtime behavior or code line-by-line, never by a compile succeeding.

So here's where I land, concretely, because this phase exists to render a decision, not describe a spectrum: this is a go for calling the build phase complete and functionally matching the original prompt's MVP scope — capture/import, seven-reason quality gate, correction review, real on-device vectorization, Compare/Edit workspace with the locked gesture contract, SVG/PDF export, Recents, checkpointed persistence, Settings — all present, all traceable back to the prompt_contract and app_features decisions with nothing quietly dropped. It is a no-go for App Store submission today, for one reason that outranks everything else: the human_qa_checklist was authored, never executed. Nobody has run it. That's not a nice-to-have gap, it's the one gap that determines whether everything upstream of it is actually true in practice.

The concrete sequencing I'd lock in, because "someone should eventually" isn't a plan: first and only true blocker, in serial, is running that QA checklist for real on a physical device — camera capture, VoiceOver-only cleanup, Reduce Motion/Transparency, and the five-point kill-and-relaunch matrix specifically, since those can't be faked from a simulator or a code read. That has one owner and one next step: run it, fix whatever it finds, don't skip to store prep first. In parallel, because they have zero dependency on QA results, the five app_store_readiness blockers are cheap and should just get done now rather than carried forward again: add a real AppIcon asset, set TARGETED_DEVICE_FAMILY to iPhone explicitly rather than trusting Xcode's default, stand up a support URL/contact (a static page or email is sufficient), and write the age-rating and privacy-questionnaire answers as positive claims rather than leaving them implicit. T-025 through T-029 — the automated kill-and-relaunch suite, accessibility suite, device perf tests, state-coverage audit — I'd explicitly deprioritize past this review rather than pretend they're still pending "next phase" for the fourth time; they're test-infrastructure hardening, not user-facing gaps, and the manual QA pass covers the same ground once for real. Naming them as accepted, deferred technical debt is more honest than leaving them as an ambiguous forever-follow-up.
