phase_outputs.prompt_contract:
All three points actually landed this round. Codex accepted the scope fence and the Vision-isn't-magic framing as settled, and I explicitly confirmed the scope fence too, so that one's closed. Codex made the case that full in-app Bezier/anchor editing would blow the MVP scope and drift toward the "desktop-class editor" non-goal, arguing the honest reading of "editable" is real vector paths that are editable downstream, with in-app cleanup limited to crop/perspective/threshold/simplify/delete-fragment tools. I pushed back some but ended up narrowing my own ask instead of holding the line on full node editing — landing on select/delete-path, simplify-strength slider, merge-paths, and undo/redo as the MVP in-app editing bar, with full anchor-point dragging explicitly deferred to a later pro tier (which doubles as an honest monetization hook). Codex hasn't confirmed that exact list yet, but it's a direct answer to what they raised, not a new fight — it looks like a landing point rather than an open dispute.

I also supplied the concrete "clean trace" quality bar Codex didn't answer directly last round: no orphan fragments under ~3pt, continuous strokes stay continuous, bounded node counts (so paths are actually drag-editable), opens cleanly in Illustrator/Affinity, and passes a human side-by-side resemblance check. And I put a testability condition on Codex's "capture rescue" pitch — it only counts as a real differentiator if it's proven against named conditions (e.g. degrees of skew, shadow coverage), with anything outside that producing an honest retake-photo error rather than a silent garbage trace, rather than just being marketing language.

Nothing here is a live disagreement anymore — it's Codex's direction plus my specifics, and my one revision (narrowing the editing ask) moved toward Codex's position rather than away from it. Gemini still unavailable.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**cleanline execution contract, locked for this phase:**

- **Scope fence:** This phase sequence builds one app — cleanline — end to end. The parent portfolio's 12-ideas/pick-6, cross-app uniqueness enforcement, Jira/Notion backfill, and combined-transcript instructions are inherited upstream constraints (respect uniqueness, keep it a separate project folder), not tasks this app's phases re-run. No later phase should regenerate concepts for cleanline or build portfolio-level artifacts as part of this app's output.

- **Hard requirements:** A real SwiftUI, local-first iOS MVP: capture or import a paper sketch photo, run it through a deterministic on-device processing pipeline (perspective correction, contrast/threshold isolation, noise reduction, contour extraction via Vision, path simplification/curve fitting — Vision alone is explicitly not sufficient and is named as an architectural assumption, not a late discovery), preview the traced result, do meaningful in-app cleanup, and export valid SVG/PDF. Empty/loading/success/error states everywhere the flow can reach them. Accessibility, privacy, local persistence, and testing are in scope, not stretch goals. Architecture isolates capture / preprocessing / vectorization / editing / export / persistence so cloud sync or iPad support can be added later without a rewrite.

- **"Editable" is defined concretely for MVP:** users get real in-app editing — select and delete a stray path, adjust simplify/smooth strength (global or per-path, re-runs curve fitting), merge selected paths, and undo/redo across those actions. Full anchor-point/Bezier-handle dragging is an explicit MVP non-goal, reserved as a credible future paid "pro editing" tier. Exported paths must be genuinely modifiable in downstream tools (Illustrator/Affinity), not a flattened image.

- **Clean-trace quality bar (the acceptance test later phases build and QA against):** no orphan fragments under ~3pt at export scale, continuous source strokes stay continuous, bounded node counts per curve (so paths are actually drag-editable downstream), the exported SVG opens without errors in a mainstream vector tool, and it passes a human side-by-side resemblance check against the source sketch. Tested against a small fixture set: a simple icon sketch, a lettering sketch, a closed-shape illustration, and a messy-but-recoverable notebook photo.

- **Capture-rescue is the differentiator, but only if provably bounded:** perspective correction, shadow/background cleanup, and contrast isolation are the "better than a scan app + desktop cleanup" pitch, but the contract requires this be validated against named conditions (e.g., degrees of skew, percent of frame in shadow) — outside those bounds the app must give an honest "retake photo" error, never a silently garbage trace.

- **Non-goals:** desktop-class Illustrator replacement, full node-by-node vector editing in MVP, generic AI art/style-transfer, server-side tracing, solving color-illustration vectorization in v1, and any portfolio-management/storefront features.

- **Monetization:** the core promise (photograph → usable vector export) must work free. Premium lives in higher-fidelity tracing, batch processing, cloud sync/backup, and advanced pro editing (including future full node editing) — never gating basic SVG/PDF export.

- **Decision rule for conflicts:** when scope tensions come up, protect trace/export quality and the honesty of the promise over feature breadth; when the parent portfolio and child spec seem to disagree, default to the portfolio's local-first/cloud-ready floor requirement and name it as an assumption rather than silently dropping it.
phase_outputs.initial_discussion:
Both sides converged into a single, well-specified product definition rather than staying at the "capture rescue is cool" abstraction level. Codex tightened the promise down to "photograph a paper sketch, walk away with a usable black-line vector file in a few minutes," and made explicit that the target user is a working/semi-pro illustrator or POD seller who already thinks in SVG/cut-file terms — not a hobbyist doodler. I largely echoed that but pushed the promise sentence to describe the outcome rather than the mechanism (deliberately not saying "via Vision" in the marketing copy, since we already agreed Vision alone can't do it), and stressed that the app has to work even for POD sellers who don't know what a Bezier path is.

On the core loop, we're aligned almost word for word: capture/import → auto quality gate → correction (crop/perspective/threshold) → auto-vectorize → before/after review → light cleanup (delete stray path, adjust simplify strength, merge broken segments, undo) → export SVG/PDF. Codex framed the "quality gate before tracing + confidence signal after tracing" as the key anti-false-confidence device; I framed the same idea as "fail honestly on bad input" and tied it to a concrete test (deliberately bad photos, not just good ones, must reliably hit the retake-photo error state).

On scope boundaries we both landed hard on the same fence, from two angles: Codex ruled out anything that turns this into a general vector editor or illustration app (no freehand canvas, no layers, no node editing, no style transfer, no cloud, no marketplace) and restricted MVP source art to high-contrast monochrome linework on plain paper. I added the specific thing most likely to sneak back in during build — project/library management — ruling out any searchable/organizable gallery, batch import queues, or multi-sketch workflows; only a flat, bounded "recent traces" list for persistence is in scope.

One genuinely new thing I added that Codex hasn't reacted to yet: a concrete autosave/interruption-handling requirement (persist the source photo on capture, persist trace results once computed, autosave edits) since the pipeline spans camera → multi-second processing → editing, any of which can get backgrounded or killed. That's not a disagreement, just an unconfirmed addition.

No open disagreements — this reads like real convergence, just missing Gemini's input and Codex's explicit nod to the autosave point.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Product promise:** cleanline lets an illustrator or print-on-demand seller photograph a paper sketch and walk away, in a few minutes, with a genuinely usable black-line vector file (SVG/PDF) — not a raw contour dump, but the output of a correction-and-tracing pipeline they can trust enough to bring straight into Illustrator, Affinity, or a cut-file workflow.

**Primary user & scenario:** A single working or semi-pro illustrator, lettering artist, or POD seller — not necessarily vector-savvy — who just finished one hand-drawn sketch on paper and wants a vector version today, for one listing/cut-file/asset. Not a studio, not a team handoff, not a batch job.

**Core loop:** Start a trace → capture or import sketch photo → app runs an automatic quality check (retake-photo error if the shot is outside supported bounds, rather than a silent garbage trace) → auto-correct (perspective, threshold/contrast) → auto-vectorize → show a before/after comparison → user does light, failure-mode-specific cleanup (delete a stray path, adjust simplify/smoothing strength, merge broken stroke segments, undo/redo) → export SVG/PDF → file persists locally in a flat recent-traces list.

**Hard scope boundaries:** No freehand drawing canvas, layers, brushes, text tool, color-fill workflow, node/Bezier-handle editing, AI style transfer, cloud dependency, or marketplace/storefront angle. No searchable/organizable project library — only a bounded "recent traces" list (open/delete). No batch import queue (batch is an explicit future paid feature). MVP source art is limited to high-contrast monochrome linework on plain paper; pencil shading, watercolor, color illustration, dense crosshatching, and multi-object page extraction are out of scope or explicitly degraded with an honest error rather than pretending to handle them.

**Measurable success criteria:** (1) A first-time user goes from opening the app to a completed SVG export in under 3–5 minutes on a decent source photo. (2) All four fixture-set images (icon sketch, lettering, closed-shape illustration, messy-but-recoverable notebook photo) pass the contract's "clean trace" bar using only the MVP editing tools — needing anchor-dragging to salvage a fixture means the tracing pipeline is under-baked, not that editing scope should grow. (3) Deliberately bad test photos (excess skew, shadow, low contrast) reliably produce the honest retake-photo error rather than a garbage export. (4) State is autosaved at every pipeline stage (capture, trace, edits) so backgrounding or interruption never loses work.
phase_outputs.per_app_product_brief:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Codex and Claude ended up telling almost the same story from two different angles — Codex from "what's the honest product mechanics" and Claude from "what does this feel like for the actual seller at her kitchen table" — and they reinforce rather than contradict each other.

**Target user & use case:** A solo illustrator, hand-letterer, or print-on-demand seller who draws on paper first and needs one clean vector file today — for a listing, a cut file, a product drop — without a scanner, without Illustrator's manual retrace/deskew/cleanup dance, and often without deep vector-tool knowledge.

**Paid value:** Free tier does the whole core promise, unlimited times — capture, correct, trace, light cleanup, export SVG/PDF, no watermark, no format bait-and-switch, because that's the app's right to exist. Subscription lives in things a real production workflow needs once someone has more than one sketch: batch processing (photograph/import a stack, walk away, come back to finished exports), higher-fidelity tracing presets (fine-detail mode for denser linework/serif lettering), the future pro editing tier (real anchor/Bezier dragging, already reserved in the contract), and cloud backup/sync of recent-traces history. All four are functional upgrades tied to real recurring pain, not cosmetic gates.

**Core loop:** Unchanged from earlier phases and both agents want it held there exactly: new trace → capture/import → quality gate with specific retake guidance → auto-correct (perspective/shadow/threshold) → auto-vectorize → before/after review → light, failure-mode-specific cleanup → export → save to recent traces. Claude added that every step of that pipeline needs a progress state that names what it's doing (correcting perspective, isolating linework, tracing) rather than a generic spinner, since the processing genuinely takes seconds and silence during those seconds reads as broken.

**Competitive wedge:** Not "better tracing algorithm" in the abstract — better than a scanner app because you get real paths, better than Illustrator's Image Trace because she never leaves her phone or does a desktop round-trip. The wedge is bounded capture-rescue plus honest rejection: fix what's fixable, clearly refuse what isn't. Both agents flag the same risk as the single biggest thing that could sink the product: if shadow/perspective correction doesn't actually hold up on real kitchen-table photos, the wedge disappears and it's just an over-promising scanner app.

**Growth angle:** Niche, not broad — POD sellers, Etsy seller groups, Cricut communities, and illustrator TikTok/YouTube already share before/after transformation content. The product needs to generate that shareable moment itself (the before/after screen should look good enough to want to post), rather than relying on any built-in social feature.

**Local-first/cloud-ready:** Fully local and offline-capable in v1 — source photos, previews, vector outputs, recent-trace metadata, edit history, no account required, sketches never leave the device unless the user opts into backup. That's framed as a genuine trust point for this user (she's often working pre-release designs), not just an architecture choice. Cloud shows up later, cleanly separated, as opt-in sync of recent-traces history and possibly cross-device batch pickup — never a v1 dependency.

One thing flagged but not yet a disagreement: Claude named the trust curve (trace #2 and #3 needing to feel faster/more trusted than trace #1) as the thing to protect above all in design — this is a design-phase concern, not a brief-level dispute, and nothing in Codex's position pushes against it.

No live disagreement between the two active participants. Gemini remains unavailable.
phase_outputs.detailed_discussion:
Good news — the two things I flagged as genuinely open both got resolved with real give-and-take, not just one side caving.

On monetization, Codex came around independently to exactly the position Claude was arguing: no live StoreKit in this MVP. Building a purchase flow for batch/fine-detail/pro-editing/cloud-sync when none of those exist yet would be the same overclaiming sin the app is designed to avoid in its own tracing pipeline. Landing spot: free loop ships complete and unlocked, settings has an honest non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" note, zero StoreKit code, zero disabled toggles in the main workflow.

On storage, this wasn't "pick a side" — it was a genuine synthesis. Claude proposed keeping the raw capture only transiently (through the correction/review step, so the "inspectable, reversible" requirement is real), then discarding it and keeping the corrected working image + vector data forever until manual delete. Codex arrived at essentially the same shape from the other direction — "persist a normalized working original, not the raw camera file" — with the added detail that orientation-correction, downsampling, and metadata-stripping should all happen at that same normalization step. Both agree: no silent eviction, manual delete only, and EXIF/location/timestamp get stripped at ingest rather than lingering in a retained asset (which matters once cloud sync shows up later).

Codex also explicitly signed off on the rest of Claude's round-1 batch — PHPicker over full library access, EXIF-orientation handling as product correctness not just plumbing, the VoiceOver list-fallback for path cleanup — and drew one useful new boundary: dark pencil is in-scope only when the quality gate judges contrast sufficient, blue ballpoint is explicitly excluded rather than left to silently underperform.

Claude added a few things this round Codex hasn't directly reacted to, but they're extensions of the same honesty principle both sides already hold, not new fights: splitting "contrast too low" into a separate "sketch too faint to trace — darken your lines or go over it in pen" reason (since that's a drawing problem, not a photo problem, and no retake fixes it); making the quality-gate rejection reasons a fixed named vocabulary rather than free-form strings, so repeat failures feel recognizable instead of random; and naming live in-camera framing guidance (à la VisionKit's document scanner) as a requirement direction for architecture to weigh, rather than defaulting by accident to post-capture-only rejection.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- MVP supports one artifact class: high-contrast monochrome linework (black ink, dark marker, dark pencil when contrast is sufficient) on plain paper, one sketch per job. Blue ballpoint, shaded graphite, watercolor, multicolor illustration, and multi-object pages are explicitly out of scope, not silently degraded.
- Capture/import via live camera or PHPickerViewController (not full photo-library access) — reduces permission friction and strengthens the local-first privacy claim.
- Every pipeline stage is inspectable: user can view original, corrected/normalized preview, and traced vector preview, with undo/redo across edits.
- Quality-gate rejections use a fixed, named vocabulary (e.g., skew-too-high, shadow-coverage-too-high, blur-too-high, contrast-too-low, sketch-too-faint, no-sketch-region-detected, ruled-paper-interference), each with specific, consistent copy — not free-formed per attempt — so repeat failures are recognizable rather than random.
- "Sketch too faint to trace" is a distinct rejection reason from generic low-contrast/lighting problems, since faint pencil pressure is a drawing problem no retake fixes.
- Architecture should treat live, in-camera framing guidance (real-time edge/skew feedback before the shot is taken, in the spirit of VisionKit's document scanner) as a requirement direction to evaluate, not default to post-capture-only rejection — the implementation choice (custom vs. VisionKit-based) is deferred to the architecture phase.
- No live StoreKit subscription in this MVP. The complete free loop (capture, correct, trace, cleanup, export) ships fully unlocked. A non-purchasing, honestly-worded "Pro — coming soon" entry in settings describes planned batch/fine-detail/pro-editing/cloud-sync features. No paywall code, no disabled premium toggles in the main workflow.
- Storage/retention: the raw full-resolution capture is transient, kept only through the correction/review step. Once the user proceeds past review, the app discards the raw capture and persists a normalized working image (orientation-corrected, downsampled, EXIF/location/timestamp stripped at this point) plus the vector result — retained indefinitely in the recent-traces list until manual delete. No silent LRU eviction.

**Edge cases:**
- Camera permission denied → import path (PHPicker) must remain fully usable; app must not degrade to a dead end.
- App backgrounded/killed mid-processing → resume from the last saved checkpoint (source or normalized image), not from a partially-run Vision request; never silently lose the source.
- Export destination/share-sheet failure → traced file remains available in recent traces so the user isn't forced to rerun the pipeline.
- HEIC/EXIF orientation must be respected at ingest, before any correction — a silently rotated/mirrored result destroys trust instantly.
- Ruled/grid notebook paper must be distinguished from foreground sketch lines in preprocessing, since this is one of the four committed acceptance fixtures.
- Lettering vs. closed-shape illustrations fail differently (stroke continuity/corner fidelity vs. closed loops not being accidentally filled) — both need to be covered by the acceptance bar, not just one.
- Faint/light pencil source material is a distinct failure mode from bad photo conditions and needs its own honest rejection message.
- VoiceOver users can't precision-tap a small stray vector fragment on a canvas — cleanup needs an accessible list-based fallback (select/delete/merge by name, not just by touch).
- Simplify-strength slider dragging must be debounced and operate on a downsampled preview (full-res only on commit) to avoid visible lag/thermal throttling on older supported devices.

**Data and privacy implications:**
- Sketches never leave the device by default; no account required in MVP.
- No analytics SDK, no phone-home crash reporter, no network entitlement usage in this build — the local-first claim must be auditable, not just asserted in copy.
- EXIF metadata (location, timestamp, device model) is stripped at ingest, not just at export, so nothing sensitive is sitting in retained assets waiting to leak once cloud sync is added later.
- Camera/photo-library usage-description strings must be specific and true, not boilerplate.
- Recent-traces list is unbounded in count with manual delete only — no background/automatic deletion of a user's work.

**Risk register:**
- Capture-rescue overclaim: app markets itself as forgiving but only performs on near-ideal photos. Mitigation: named, testable support envelope plus fixture-based QA using deliberately imperfect photos.
- Vector quality overclaim: technically valid SVG that's still unusable (too many fragments/nodes). Mitigation: the existing clean-trace bar plus real downstream-open tests in a mainstream vector tool.
- Editing-scope mismatch: cleanup tools too weak for real failure modes while the app doesn't fail early enough to stop bad traces. Mitigation: keep cleanup tools mapped to actual failure classes; reject inputs that would need desktop-class editing.
- Trust decay across repeat use: same sketch succeeding once and failing later without explanation, or vague/inconsistent error messages. Mitigation: fixed rejection-reason vocabulary with consistent copy, faster/more predictable repeat use, remembered settings.
- Silent quality miss within "supported" bounds (e.g., a pencil sketch that's technically accepted but underperforms): mitigation is explicit contrast-sufficiency judgment at the gate rather than a blanket "pencil is fine" assumption.
- Half-built monetization risk avoided by decision above (no live paywall for non-existent features).
- Storage bloat / silent data loss risk avoided by the normalized-working-copy retention policy (bounded storage, no silent eviction).

**Final assumptions:**
- iPhone-first (not iPad-first) for MVP.
- Fully offline-capable after install; no server-side processing.
- One-sketch-at-a-time jobs; only a flat, bounded (by delete-only, not count) recent-traces list — no project/library management.
- Black-line output only; color-aware tracing is out of scope.
- Free tier ships the complete, unwatermarked core loop; no StoreKit/subscription code in MVP, only an informational "Pro — coming soon" settings entry.
- Autosave checkpoints at capture, post-normalization/review, post-trace, and post-edit.
- Raw capture is transient (through review only); normalized working image + vector result are the durable, recoverable assets.
- Quality-gate rejection reasons form a fixed, named set with dedicated copy, including a distinct "sketch too faint" reason separate from photo-condition reasons.
- Live in-capture framing guidance is a direction for the architecture phase to design against, not a locked implementation choice.
- Device floor and precise perf/thermal targets are deferred to the architecture phase, but debounced/downsampled interactive previews are a locked requirement for any slider-driven re-processing.
phase_outputs.app_features:
Looking at this round, Codex and Claude landed on essentially the same feature map — they just carved it up differently (five capabilities vs. a flat list) and Claude used its turn to force two decisions that earlier phases had left hanging rather than to disagree with anything Codex said.

Both agree on the must-have spine: camera + PHPicker intake with permission-denied still leaving import usable; a quality gate that rejects with the fixed named reasons already locked in the requirements phase; an inspectable correction/normalization preview; vectorization with named progress states (not a spinner); a before/after review; the exact contract-defined cleanup set (select/delete path, debounced simplify slider on a downsampled preview, merge paths, undo/redo) with a VoiceOver list-based fallback; SVG+PDF export that survives share-sheet failure; a flat recent-traces list with manual delete only; checkpointed autosave at capture/normalization/trace/edit; and full empty/loading/success/error coverage. Claude additionally named a settings screen (privacy statement + honest "Pro — coming soon" note) as must-have rather than could-have, which Codex's could-have list already gestured at but didn't formalize — a sharpening, not a fight.

The two things earlier phases explicitly punted — live in-camera framing guidance, and exactly what "resume after interruption" means — both got resolved, and resolved the same way by both agents rather than argued over: framing guidance is should-have (post-capture gate alone satisfies the honesty requirement; live guidance is a genuinely harder CV problem that risks ballooning scope), and resume must land the user at the last *committed* checkpoint, never mid-gesture, with concrete test criteria (a real VoiceOver session, a scripted kill at each checkpoint) rather than just "autosave exists." Codex's own acceptance criteria for persistence already implied this same behavior, so it's convergence, not a late override.

No live disagreement between the two active participants. Gemini remains unavailable.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Must-have features** (each ties directly to the locked product promise — fast, trustworthy paper-sketch-to-vector export):

1. **Sketch intake — camera or import.** *Story:* as a user with a finished paper sketch, I can start a trace from my camera or an existing photo, and declining camera access doesn't block me. *Acceptance:* both entry points work; camera-denied still allows full PHPicker import; unreadable/unsupported images produce a specific error state, not a crash or blank screen.

2. **Capture quality gate with named rejection reasons.** *Story:* as a user, if my photo can't be traced reliably I get a specific, consistent reason and a next step. *Acceptance:* gate runs before correction; uses the fixed vocabulary (skew-too-high, shadow-coverage-too-high, blur-too-high, contrast-too-low, sketch-too-faint, no-sketch-region-detected, ruled-paper-interference); each has stable, specific copy; out-of-envelope images never silently proceed to trace.

3. **Ingest normalization + correction review.** *Story:* as a user, I can see what the app did to my photo (orientation, perspective, threshold) before committing further. *Acceptance:* HEIC/EXIF orientation corrected immediately; EXIF metadata stripped at ingest; original and corrected preview both viewable; user can back out without losing the source at this stage.

4. **Deterministic on-device vectorization with named progress states.** *Story:* as a user, I know what's happening during processing instead of staring at a generic spinner. *Acceptance:* stages are labeled (e.g. "correcting perspective," "isolating linework," "tracing paths"); output has bounded node counts; trace failure has its own explicit error/recovery state; everything runs offline.

5. **Before/after review.** *Story:* as a user, I can compare my sketch to the vector result before touching cleanup or export. *Acceptance:* corrected image and traced output are both visible together; a poor-enough trace can route back to retry rather than forcing export.

6. **Bounded cleanup tools.** *Story:* as a user, I can fix the predictable auto-trace mistakes without needing a full vector editor. *Acceptance:* select+delete a path, simplify/smooth-strength slider (debounced, downsampled-preview during drag, full-res on commit), merge selected paths, undo/redo; a VoiceOver-accessible list (not canvas-only) supports select/delete/merge, verified with an actual VoiceOver session, not just labels.

7. **Export to SVG and PDF.** *Story:* as a user, I leave with a file I can actually use downstream. *Acceptance:* both formats export as real vector paths (not flattened images); export/share-sheet failure never destroys the trace, which stays available in recents.

8. **Local persistence + interruption recovery.** *Story:* as a user, I never lose work from backgrounding, a phone call, or a crash. *Acceptance:* autosave checkpoints at capture, post-normalization, post-trace, post-edit; on relaunch after a kill, the app resumes from the last **committed** checkpoint (never a half-applied slider drag or partial Vision request), verified by a scripted kill-and-relaunch test at each checkpoint; recent traces are reopenable and manually deletable, unbounded in count, no silent eviction.

9. **Empty/loading/success/error states across the whole flow.** *Story:* as a user, every screen tells me clearly what state I'm in. *Acceptance:* recents list, intake, gate, correction, processing, review, cleanup, export, and every named failure path all have an intentional designed state.

10. **Settings screen: privacy statement + honest "Pro — coming soon."** *Story:* as a user, I can verify my sketches stay on-device and see what's planned without being sold something that doesn't exist yet. *Acceptance:* plain-language local-first statement; non-purchasing "Pro" entry describing planned batch/fine-detail/pro-editing/cloud-sync; zero StoreKit code, zero gated toggles anywhere in the main workflow.

**Should-have:**
- Live in-camera framing guidance (real-time skew/edge feedback pre-shutter) — deferred because the post-capture gate alone already satisfies the honesty requirement, and this is a materially harder CV feature.
- Remembered simplify/threshold/export preferences across traces, to serve the repeat-use trust curve.
- A borderline-confidence indicator on trace results that pass the gate but are marginal.
- Lightweight recents metadata (date, thumbnail, export status).
- An internal fixture/demo set for QA sanity checks during development.

**Could-have (only if essentially free):**
- Shareable before/after snapshot from the review screen.
- Duplicate-trace-to-try-alternate-settings.
- Rename a saved trace.
- A short static (non-interactive) first-run walkthrough.
- A "retake tips" overlay visualizing the rejection vocabulary.

**Won't-build:**
- Full anchor-point/Bezier-handle editing.
- Freehand drawing canvas, layers, brushes, text tools, fills — any general illustration surface.
- Batch import/processing, fine-detail tracing presets, cloud sync/backup.
- Any live StoreKit purchase flow or disabled premium toggle.
- Color-aware/multi-color tracing, automatic multi-object page splitting.
- Support claims for blue ballpoint, shaded graphite, watercolor.
- Searchable/organizable project library (folders, tags, cross-trace search) beyond the flat recents list.
- AI restyling/style-transfer or generic image-editing features.
- Dedicated iPad layout (iPhone-first; SwiftUI default scaling is fine, no bespoke iPad work).
phase_outputs.design_discussion:
They've fully closed the gap this round. Codex took Claude's unified Compare/Edit workspace and Claude took Codex's combined quality-gate/correction screen and export-as-sheet — genuine trades in both directions, not one side caving. The zoom/pan state-handoff argument Claude raised (would cleanup reset your scroll position after a screen transition?) is what tipped it: keeping Compare and Edit as one view with one shared piece of state avoids that plumbing problem entirely, and Codex agreed it stays disciplined as long as it's strictly two modes over one canvas, not a looser multi-purpose shell. The reveal-animation question resolved the same way — it's not a competing idea, it's Codex's "no fake progress" rule applied literally to real streaming path data during the tracing stage, with an explicit honest fallback (plain stage label) if incremental output turns out not to be architecturally practical. No open disagreements left.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory (six screens + one modal sheet):**

1. **Recents Home** — app root, no tab bar. Prominent "New Trace" action, flat list of past traces (thumbnail, date, status chip: needs review / exported / trace failed), small Settings entry.
2. **New Trace Intake** — two large choices, Camera or Import Photo, plus a fixed scope-defense note ("dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time"). Camera-denied keeps the camera card visible with an explanation and system-settings link, while Import stays fully usable.
3. **Camera Capture** — live capture screen, permission-denied handled as a first-class in-screen state (not a system-alert dead end), with a same-screen "Import a Photo Instead" escape hatch.
4. **Quality Gate + Correction Review** (combined, one screen with variants) — checking (brief transitional state), rejected-with-named-reason (one of the seven fixed rejection reasons, each with stable, specific copy/iconography plus a real accessibility label), corrected-preview-ready (original vs. corrected, Continue/Retake), correction-failed, and resuming-from-checkpoint. Rejection is a variant of this same state machine, not a separate destination.
5. **Processing** — dedicated screen, corrected image faintly visible behind it, named sequential stage labels (checking geometry → correcting perspective → isolating linework → tracing paths → preparing preview). No fake progress bar. During the tracing-paths stage specifically, the app renders real vector strokes as they're actually computed (a literal application of "no fake progress," not a separate feature) — if incremental streaming turns out not to be practical, this stage falls back to the same plain dimmed-label treatment as the other stages, no synthetic animation standing in for unfinished work. Explicit trace-failed error state with Retry / Adjust Photo as distinct recoveries.
6. **Trace Workspace** — the unified post-trace destination. One canvas, one shared render of the path data, two bidirectional modes (Compare / Edit) via a lightweight toggle, never a one-way "continue" gate. Compare shows corrected-image-vs-vector with an interactive wipe/slider for sighted users and a non-gestural Original/Traced segmented toggle as the accessible default fallback. Edit shows the fixed, always-visible bottom tool strip (select/delete, simplify slider, merge, undo/redo) plus the VoiceOver-accessible list-based path management mode. Reopened traces land here by default (Compare if untouched, Edit if cleanup was in progress). Zoom/pan/scroll state is a single piece of view state that never has to survive a navigation transition, since there is no transition between Compare and Edit.
7. **Export** — a modal sheet summonable from either Workspace mode (not a separate screen/navigation push). States: ready, exporting, success (share sheet), failed-but-saved (explicit reassurance the trace remains safe in Recents).

Plus **Settings** — plain-language local-first/privacy statement, honest non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" note, no growth hooks or fake locked controls.

**Primary user flow:** Home → New Trace → Camera or Import → Quality Gate (pass) → Correction Review → Processing (named stages, real-data reveal on tracing) → Trace Workspace (Compare ⇄ Edit, freely, both ways) → Export sheet (from either mode) → back to Home with saved result. Any quality-gate rejection is a same-screen variant with Retake/Choose Another/Cancel, not a detour to a new destination. Reopening a saved trace skips straight to the Workspace at its last state.

**State model per screen:** as detailed above per screen — every screen has explicit empty/loading/success/error coverage where the flow can reach it, and interruption/kill-and-relaunch always resumes at the last **committed** checkpoint (never mid-gesture, never a partially-run Vision request).

**Visual direction:** A drafting-table / light-table aesthetic, not a generic productivity app or a cutesy scanner app. Warm, slightly warm-neutral paper-tone base surfaces (not stark white, not dark-developer-tool), true black ink for the traced linework as the only saturated element on screen, one sparing sharp technical accent color (e.g., oxidized teal or safety orange) reserved for active/warning states. Clean geometric sans typography, editorial/industrial rather than startup-default. Chrome and controls stay quiet and recede; the sketch and vector result carry all the visual weight — this matters directly for the shareable before/after moment the growth strategy depends on. Motion is functional: stage transitions reinforce real pipeline progress, no decorative flourish, no canned animation standing in for unfinished work.

**Accessibility notes:** Seven rejection-reason screens are visually distinct (icon/color) for sighted pattern-matching but every icon carries a full-text accessibility label stating the actual reason — visual pattern-matching is a bonus, never a substitute for text. Compare mode's wipe/slider gesture has a mandatory non-gestural Original/Traced toggle fallback for VoiceOver. Edit mode's cleanup tools are fully operable through the accessible path list (select/delete/merge by name) with zero required canvas interaction, to be verified with an actual VoiceOver session. The tracing-stage path-reveal animation needs a reduce-motion variant that still communicates progress via updating text stage labels rather than either forcing motion or going silent. Dynamic Type must hold up without truncating recovery actions on rejection/error screens especially, since that copy carries real communicative weight.
phase_outputs.design_handoff:
CONSENSUS: YES

They actually closed all three open items this round, and closed them the same way each time: Claude proposes a concrete, technically-grounded tightening, Codex checks it against the "no fake progress / no fragile gesture logic" principles he already committed to in round 1, and adopts it outright rather than negotiating a middle ground.

On the Workspace canvas, Codex fully adopted Claude's gesture-disambiguation scheme — wipe-to-compare only arms via a visible handle at/near base zoom, tap-to-select only lives in Edit mode, and (this landed independently from both sides) no double-tap-to-zoom at all, since it would introduce a perceptible selection delay in Edit mode for no real benefit. Pinch+pan for zoom, always available in both modes; above ~1.15x zoom the Original/Traced segmented toggle becomes everyone's comparison tool, not just an accessibility fallback.

On the trace-reveal animation, Codex dropped the "try live streaming, plain-label fallback" framing and adopted Claude's firmer, more honest commitment: the reveal shows real, completed path data sequenced onto the canvas at a short fixed stagger after the trace finishes — not synced to actual computation timing, since Vision's contour detection is single-shot. If the pipeline later turns out to support real incremental emission, that's a clean upgrade path, not a spec change.

On external design scope, this genuinely met in the middle: Codex gets his comprehensive screen list (Recents Home, Intake, Camera Capture, all seven rejection variants, Correction Review, Processing, Workspace in both modes, Export, Settings), Claude gets an explicit sentence in the prompt stating these are static tone/layout references only — gesture behavior, mode transitions, undo/redo, and checkpoint/resume logic are owned by the written spec, not to be inferred from images.

Two more things Claude raised this round that Codex hasn't directly answered yet, but they're both just consistency fixes applying rules Codex already stated himself, not new fights: the seven rejection cards should share one safety-orange accent treatment (differentiated by icon glyph, not by seven different colors), since seven accent colors would quietly violate Codex's own "no rainbow status chips" rule; and the conditional manual-threshold slider on Correction Review should be gated by an explicit boolean evaluated against the locked fixture set, not a vibes call made mid-build. Neither of these contradicts anything Codex said — they're closing loopholes in his own stated principles — so they don't need another round to resolve.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen specification** (six screens + Export sheet + Settings, per the locked design-discussion inventory, now with buildable layout/interaction detail):

1. **Recents Home** — root, no tab bar. Vertically stacked: title block, full-width "New Trace" button, then a flat list of row cards (thumbnail, name/timestamp, status chip, trailing chevron). Thumbnails are cached raster images generated once at save time, not re-rendered from vector data on every list draw. Empty state: large placeholder frame, one sentence of product promise, same full-width New Trace CTA — no fake sample data. Loading: lightweight skeleton list. Error: local-storage read failure, plain-language retry. New items slide in, deletions collapse with a swipe-to-delete + confirmation step (work is durable, shouldn't vanish accidentally). Every row's accessibility label states its status in words, not just via the chip color.

2. **New Trace Intake** — two large source cards (Camera, Import Photo) plus a permanent scope note ("dark linework on plain paper, one sketch at a time") as real readable text, not a caption baked into an image. Camera card has three variants in place: available, permission-denied (explanation + Open Settings link, card stays actionable), unavailable — Import remains fully active in all three.

3. **Camera Capture** — live preview owns the screen; sparse top bar (Cancel + source label), bottom bar (shutter + persistent "Import Instead" text action). Soft page-frame overlay with a one-line framing tip (reserved visual space even without live guidance). Permission-denied is an in-place state, not a system-alert dead end. Large hit targets and direct VoiceOver naming on shutter/cancel/import-fallback.

4. **Quality Gate + Correction Review** (one screen, state machine) — checking (dimmed photo + status block); rejected (one of seven named reasons, each sharing the **same single safety-orange accent border/icon-frame color**, differentiated by a distinct icon glyph per reason — tilted frame for skew, sun/shadow glyph for shadow coverage, faint-pencil glyph for sketch-too-faint, etc. — never seven different accent colors); corrected-preview-ready (Original/Corrected two-position segmented switch, not thumbnails or a gesture — direct crossfade between states, no flourish); correction-failed; resuming-from-checkpoint. An optional manual threshold-adjustment slider is gated behind an explicit `showsManualThresholdSlider: Bool` config flag, decided against the locked fixture set during build — not a mid-implementation judgment call. Rejection title/action copy must survive large Dynamic Type without truncation.

5. **Processing** — corrected image faintly visible behind a centered stage stack (current stage emphasized, upcoming stages dimmed below). No fake progress bar. During "tracing paths," the canvas reveals the real, complete traced path data in a short fixed stagger (~40–60ms per path, capped ~900ms total) once the trace finishes — an honest sequenced reveal, not a claim of live computation. Trace-failed state: same dimmed background, explicit failure message, Retry / Adjust Photo as distinct actions. Reduce Motion replaces the stagger with the final rendered state plus the existing text stage labels — never blank.

6. **Trace Workspace** — one canvas, one `TraceSession`-owned zoom/pan/selection state, Compare/Edit as a lightweight top segmented toggle (150ms crossfade of overlay controls only, canvas never re-lays-out). **Gesture contract, locked:** pinch+pan for zoom are always live in both modes; Compare's wipe gesture only arms via a visible handle the user touches first, and only at/near base zoom (<~1.15x) — above that threshold the Original/Traced segmented toggle becomes the comparison tool for all users, sighted or not; tap-to-select only arms in Edit mode; **no double-tap-to-zoom anywhere in the workspace**, so single-tap path selection is never delayed by double-tap gesture recognition. Edit mode's bottom tool strip (Select/Delete, Simplify, Merge, Undo, Redo) is fixed and always visible — never collapsed into a menu. Simplify slider previews on a downsampled render while dragging (debounced, cancel-aware) and commits full-resolution only on release. A path-list mode (reachable from Edit) gives every fragment a row with shape glyph, open/closed + size metadata, and select/delete/merge exposed as direct `.accessibilityAction`s on the row — no hidden swipe-to-reveal. Reopened traces land here at their last committed state (Compare if untouched, Edit if cleanup was mid-flight); zoom/pan never resets on mode switch, since there is no navigation transition between modes.

7. **Export** (modal sheet from either Workspace mode) — trace summary + preview, format choice (SVG/PDF), filename, export action; locked progress state during export; success hands off to share/export UI; failure explicitly states the trace remains saved in Recents and can be re-exported later — verbalized, not left to the share sheet alone to imply.

**Settings** — plain-language on-device/local-storage statement, informational non-purchasing "Pro — coming soon" block (batch, higher-fidelity tracing, cloud backup, advanced editing), no purchasable surface, no locked toggles.

**Design tokens:**
- Colors: paper base `#F6F1E7`, elevated card surface `#FBF8F2`, primary ink/text `#17140F`, secondary/graphite text `#6B6459`, hairline dividers `#DAD2C2` @1px. One active/primary accent: oxidized teal `#2F6F63` (buttons, active toggle, in-progress). One warning/error accent: safety orange `#C1531B`, reserved exclusively for rejection/failure states and used identically across all seven rejection cards.
- Typography: SF Pro throughout (no bundled custom font) — SF Pro Display semibold, tight tracking, for titles/headers; wide-tracked small-caps-style treatment for stage labels and status chips; generous line height on instructional/rejection copy.
- Spacing: 4pt grid; margins 16/24/32; corner radii 12pt (cards), 20pt (sheet top corners), 10pt (buttons).
- Motion: Processing stage transitions — 250ms crossfade + 8pt vertical shift. Compare/Edit toggle — 150ms crossfade of overlay controls only. Tracing-stage path reveal — ~40–60ms stagger per path, capped at 900ms, collapsing to instant final-state + text label under Reduce Motion.

**Component inventory:** full-width primary button, secondary text button, elevated source card, recent-trace row card, status chip, segmented control, comparison canvas (PathCanvas — the shared Compare/Edit render surface), fixed tool strip (disabled-state-aware), debounced simplify slider panel, path-list row (with direct accessibility actions), rejection-reason card (single-accent, icon-differentiated), processing stage stack, export option row, empty-state hero block.

**State & interaction model:** One `@Observable` `TraceSession` per open trace owns the normalized image, vector path models, undo/redo command stack (value-type commands like `deletePath(id)`, `mergePaths(ids)`, `setSimplifyStrength(pathID, value)` — applied functionally, only produced on gesture commit/release, never mid-drag), current mode, current zoom/pan/selection, and gate/processing status. This is the object checkpointed to disk at the four committed points (capture, post-normalization, post-trace, post-edit) as `normalized.heic` / `vector.json` / `thumbnail.jpg` / `metadata.json` — checkpointing serializes only durable state, never in-flight gesture or uncommitted slider values, so "resume from last committed checkpoint" is mechanically enforced rather than aspirational.

**Accessibility requirements:** 44pt minimum tap targets on the tool strip; every rejection icon carries a full-text accessibility label naming the actual reason (color/icon pattern-matching is a sighted bonus, never a substitute); Compare's wipe has a mandatory non-gestural Original/Traced toggle, which above ~1.15x zoom is the comparison method for everyone; Edit's cleanup is fully operable via the accessible path list with zero required canvas interaction, verified with an actual VoiceOver session; Dynamic Type tested at accessibility sizes specifically on the seven rejection cards (icon + two actions must stay visible, no truncation); Reduce Motion swaps the tracing-stage reveal for instant final state + text labels; Reduce Transparency swaps the Processing screen's "faint image behind stage label" treatment for a solid dimmed scrim rather than being ignored.

**Claude Design prompt (external design decision: use it, narrowly scoped):** Generate static tone/layout comps for cleanline, a premium local-first iOS craft tool that photographs a paper sketch and produces usable SVG/PDF vector output — a careful production tool, not a scanner app or drawing app. Visual system: warm paper-tone background (#F6F1E7), true-black ink linework as the only saturated element, oxidized teal (#2F6F63) as the sole active-state accent, safety orange (#C1531B) reserved only for warnings/rejections (one consistent treatment across all rejection states, differentiated by icon glyph, never by color), SF Pro typography with wide-tracked small-caps-style labels for stage names/status chips, quiet hairline dividers, no gradients or heavy drop shadows. Produce comps for: Recents Home (empty and populated), New Trace Intake, Camera Capture, all seven quality-gate rejection variants, Correction Review (Original/Corrected segmented switch), Processing (stage-label state), Trace Workspace in both Compare and Edit modes at base zoom, Export sheet, Settings. Include the locked feature summary and the fixed seven-reason rejection vocabulary as reference context. **These are static tone and layout references only** — gesture ownership, mode-switching behavior, undo/redo semantics, and checkpoint/resume logic are fully specified by the accompanying written interaction spec and must not be inferred or reinterpreted from the images. Do not introduce node/Bezier editing, layers, cloud UI, batch flows, or social/sharing chrome not already named above.
phase_outputs.ios_architecture_review:
This round actually closes it out — both sides directly answered the four open items and landed in the same place without any real tension between them.

**CONSENSUS: YES**

## Final Output

**Backup policy:** Trace folders stay in normal, OS-default backup (no `isExcludedFromBackup`). "Local-first / never leaves the device" is about not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs — it's not about opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup. Excluding it would trade a real, eventual, irreversible loss (phone swap/restore wipes all traces) for a theoretical privacy gain against a threat model (iCloud backup) that every other photo on the phone is already subject to. This reasoning gets written into tech_specs directly so nobody "improves privacy" later by flipping the flag.

**Privacy manifest:** `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement, not a pre-submission afterthought. The app will touch required-reason APIs (file timestamps for Recents sorting/index-rebuild, likely UserDefaults) even with zero third-party SDKs, so this is a near-certain App Store gate, not a hypothetical. Goes into tech_specs as an explicit checklist item.

**Background execution:** No `BGProcessingTask`, no background job scheduling system. A short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic checkpoint write if backgrounded mid-save. If suspension hits during Vision/curve-fitting, the app just restarts that stage from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch — a few seconds of rework, not data loss, which is already consistent with the checkpoint model. Reaching for real background processing APIs was judged not worth the complexity for a "save the user a five-second wait occasionally" payoff.

**Vectorization as a first-class subsystem:** Both agree this can't be a line item under "use Vision." Vision only supplies rectangle/contour detection — everything that actually defines trace quality (contour cleanup, stroke-continuity heuristics, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, open/closed classification, winding/fill-rule correctness, SVG/PDF serialization) is bespoke code that needs its own module boundary, its own fixture-based test suite (the four locked sketches plus adversarial cases like nested closed loops to catch winding-rule bugs), and named tunable constants (simplify-strength-to-epsilon mapping, min-fragment-length threshold, max-node-count ceiling) instead of magic numbers. tech_specs must scope this explicitly or the hardest part of the app will be the least-specified part.

**One more item folded in this round:** atomic checkpoint writes (temp-file-then-rename) get called out as an explicit named requirement with an explicit test (simulate a kill mid-write, assert the prior checkpoint is intact) — not because it's architecturally hard, but because "did someone remember to do it" bugs are exactly the kind that surface six weeks post-launch on a real user's dying phone.

Net architecture, fully agreed: thin SwiftUI shell over isolated Swift Package targets (capture/normalize/gate/vectorize/edit/export/persist), `@Observable` `TraceSession` on the main actor, all first-party frameworks, file-backed per-trace document packages (no Core Data/SwiftData), zero third-party dependencies, zero StoreKit code, no background-processing machinery, OS-default backup allowed, privacy manifest required, and vectorization elevated to a first-class subsystem with its own tests and named parameters.
phase_outputs.tech_specs:
All three loose ends closed clean this round. Codex confirmed both of the concrete fixes without hedging — `CapturedImage` (not raw `Data`) into `AppModel.startNewTrace`, since the capture/import services already hand back orientation-tagged data and there's no reason to introduce a re-inference seam; and `interruptionRecoveryState` living directly on `TraceSession` rather than in a view, so the resume banner can't drift from actual restore semantics. Codex also tightened one more thing that both sides now agree on: `TraceStore` should never persist anything durable for a rejected capture beyond the transient write needed to run the gate — the durable contract starts only once the gate passes, which keeps Recents-rebuild logic simple and avoids "folder exists but isn't a real document" edge cases.

Claude then caught one last real gap before signing off: if `interruptionRecoveryState` just sits there as a settable var, what actually clears it? Without an explicit acknowledgment step, the "restored your last saved stage" banner would reappear every time the session gets reconstructed, not just once after a real kill — which is exactly the "shown once, then gets out of the way" behavior Codex specified for this banner all the way back in round 1 of design work. The fix is a one-line addition, `TraceSession.acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()`, called by whichever view displays the banner to flip the state back to `.none`. Codex hasn't explicitly typed a reply to this specific addition, but it's not a new fight — it's closing a gap using a principle Codex already stated in his own words, and nothing about it conflicts with anything either side has argued for three rounds running.

At this point every cross-lane type has one name, one shape, and one owner, and both agents have explicitly walked back their own earlier drafts in favor of the same converged contract. This is done.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A thin SwiftUI app target owns navigation and screens only. All real logic lives in isolated local Swift Package targets with real `internal` access boundaries — capture, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit, export, and persist are each their own module, plus one shared `CleanlineModels` package that every other module and the UI target depends on, so no lane can invent a competing shape for a cross-lane type. Two observable model layers: `AppModel` (`@Observable @MainActor`) owns the Recents list and trace lifecycle (start/open/delete); `TraceSession` (`@Observable @MainActor`, one instance per open trace) owns the active workflow — document, vector data, undo/redo stack, zoom/pan/selection, mode, and interruption-recovery state. A single `AppRouter.route(for:)` function is the only place that decides where a reopened/resumed trace lands. Heavy processing (Vision contour detection, Douglas-Peucker simplification, Bezier fitting, winding/fill classification) runs off the main actor in an actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine`; `TraceSession` calls into it and gets back plain `Sendable` value types. Undo/redo is a command log (`EditCommand`) applied through a pure reducer (`EditEngine.apply(_:to:)`), and commands are appended only on gesture commit/release — never mid-drag — which is what makes "resume from last committed checkpoint" mechanically true rather than aspirational.

**File/module layout:**
```
Cleanline.xcodeproj
App/                          — CleanlineApp.swift, AppRouter.swift, RootView.swift
Packages/
  CleanlineModels/             — every shared struct/enum from the interfaces contract; zero logic
  CleanlinePersistence/         — TraceStore impl, atomic checkpoint writer, Recents index rebuild
  CleanlineCapture/             — CaptureService (AVFoundation) + ImportService (PHPicker)
  CleanlineNormalize/           — NormalizationService: orientation fix, perspective correction, threshold, EXIF strip
  CleanlineQualityGate/         — QualityGateService: rejection classification against the fixed named vocabulary
  CleanlineVectorize/           — VectorizationEngine (actor): Vision contour extraction, simplify, curve fit, winding/open-closed classification
  CleanlineEdit/                — EditEngine.apply(_:to:) as a pure reducer
  CleanlineExport/              — ExportService: SVG/PDF serializers
  CleanlineDesignSystem/        — tokens, StatusChip, PathCanvas, ToolStrip, rejection card, etc.
Features/                      — one folder per screen (RecentsHome, NewTraceIntake, CameraCapture, QualityGateCorrection, Processing, TraceWorkspace, Export, Settings), each a View + thin per-screen wrapper around TraceSession/AppModel, never a second source of truth
Tests/                         — CleanlineModelsTests, CleanlineVectorizeTests (fixture-based: 4 locked images + adversarial nested-loop set), CleanlinePersistenceTests (atomic-write kill simulation), CleanlineQualityGateTests, CleanlineEditTests (reducer/undo adversarial tests), CleanlineUITests (XCUITest — gesture contract, VoiceOver session, permission-denied flows only)
Fixtures/                      — icon-sketch, lettering, closed-shape, messy-notebook + a deliberately-bad set per named rejection reason
```

**Data models:** `TraceDocument` is the durable metadata record (`metadata.json`) — id, timestamps, `TraceStatus` (the 3-value Recents-chip vocabulary: `needsReview`/`exported`/`traceFailed`), `lastCommittedStage` (`TraceStage`: `captured`/`normalized`/`traced`/`edited`), workspace mode, gate result, export status, and file references. It never carries vector geometry or EXIF/location/device data. `VectorDocument` (`vector.json`) is its own file-backed type — canvas size, `[VectorPath]`, global simplify strength — kept separate so Recents/routing never has to decode heavy geometry. `VectorPath`/`PathSegment` use native `CGPoint`/`CGSize`/`CGRect` directly (both Codable and Sendable on this deployment target) rather than hand-rolled DTO wrappers. `EditCommand` matches the exact signatures already locked in design_handoff (`deletePath(id:)`, `mergePaths(ids:resultID:)`, `setSimplifyStrength(pathID:value:)`). `RejectionReason` is the fixed seven-case named vocabulary. `NewTraceOutcome` (`.rejected(RejectionReason)` / `.accepted(traceID:)`) is the return type of starting a new trace — this is the mechanism that guarantees a gate-rejected capture never leaves a ghost folder or Recents entry: the folder is deleted internally on rejection, and a `TraceDocument`/status only exists once the gate has passed.

**Persistence strategy:** Per-trace file packages under Application Support, OS-default backup enabled (this is deliberate — "local-first" means not shipping sketches to our servers or third-party SDKs, not opting a user's own work out of Apple's encrypted device backup). No Core Data/SwiftData. All checkpoint writes are atomic temp-file-then-rename, with an explicit test that simulates a kill mid-write and asserts the prior checkpoint survives intact. The Recents list is a rebuildable cache, not a source of truth — on any inconsistency it's reconstructed by scanning trace folders and reading each `TraceDocument`. Durable storage begins only after the quality gate passes; a rejected capture's transient folder is deleted by `AppModel` as part of handling the rejection, never left as orphaned disk state. `TraceStore` owns the atomic write/read/delete contract exactly as specified in the interfaces block below. `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` is a locked MVP requirement (file-timestamp and UserDefaults usage trigger required-reason declarations even with zero third-party SDKs). No `BGProcessingTask`; a short `beginBackgroundTask` grace window covers finishing an in-flight atomic write, and any processing interrupted mid-stage just restarts from the last committed checkpoint on relaunch.

**Testing strategy:** Pipeline stages are pure, fixture-in/fixture-out functions so gate classification, normalization invariants, simplification bounds, and export serialization are all fast, deterministic unit tests — no camera, no UI. The four locked fixtures (icon sketch, lettering, closed-shape illustration, messy-but-recoverable notebook photo) plus a deliberately-bad set (one photo per named rejection reason) plus an adversarial nested-closed-loop case (to catch winding-rule/fill bugs) run through the full pipeline with metric assertions: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, correct winding/fill on export, verified via rasterize-and-diff against fixtures rather than just XML validity. The edit reducer gets adversarial sequence tests (delete-all, merge-with-self, redo-after-new-edit invalidates the stack). Checkpoint/resume is tested by writing a checkpoint, simulating a hard kill, and reloading — not by scripting real OS-level termination. XCUITest is reserved narrowly for things provable only on a real render tree: the Workspace gesture contract (wipe-handle arming, tap-to-select never firing during a wipe drag, no double-tap-zoom delay), a real VoiceOver pass over path-list cleanup, and permission-denied → import-still-works.

phase_outputs.project_plan:
Round 2 actually closed this out. Codex took both open items from round 1 and didn't just acknowledge them — he restructured the milestone plan around them: the orphan-folder reconciliation gap moved from "worth flagging" to an explicit exit criterion of milestone one (a named startup sweep, five kill-test points instead of four), and vectorization got split into "milestone two-a: spike on the simplest fixture" followed by "milestone three: production," with fake service implementations and fixture production promoted to explicit milestone-one deliverables rather than implementation texture.

Claude then went one level deeper and found something concrete rather than just re-flagging the same worry: the locked interfaces actually contradict themselves on this exact point — `TraceStage`'s note says `captured` is persisted the instant bytes exist (implying a resumable checkpoint pre-gate), but `TraceStatus`'s note says a document only exists once the gate passes, and there's no "pending gate" status case, and `writeCheckpoint` requires a full `TraceDocument` for every stage including `.captured`. Both can't be literally true. Claude's fix is concrete and small: captured bytes land in a staging location keyed by a provisional ID (no `TraceDocument`, no Recents visibility) until the gate resolves; only then does a real `TraceDocument` get constructed and handed to `TraceStore`. That makes the reconciliation sweep trivial to spec (scan staging, not Recents, on launch; re-run the gate or delete). Codex hasn't typed a reaction to this specific mechanism yet since it came after his message, but it's not a new disagreement — it's a direct, literal answer to the "named startup sweep" he asked for, using a resolution method that doesn't touch or conflict with anything he said. Both agents also aligned on relative sizing: milestone one gets more buffer than its "just file I/O" reputation suggests because this staging ambiguity has to be actually resolved and tested; milestone three is unsized until the spike reports back; milestone four is deliberately small given the narrow edit surface; milestone five gets generous room because that's where kill-tests, resume banners, and staging-reconciliation all get proven together as a system for the first time.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Locked delivery plan for cleanline, five milestones, dependency-ordered (not screen-ordered):**

**Milestone 1 — Foundation: models, persistence, staging/reconciliation, fixtures, fakes.**
Build `CleanlineModels` (frozen from tech_specs), `AppModel`/`TraceSession`/`AppRouter` skeletons, and `TraceStore` with atomic temp-file-then-rename checkpoint writes. Critically, this milestone also resolves and implements the staging mechanism for pre-gate captures: captured bytes are written to a staging location keyed by a provisional ID, with no `TraceDocument` and no Recents visibility, and a real `TraceDocument` is only constructed once the quality gate resolves to accepted. A named startup reconciliation sweep scans staging (not the Recents folder tree) on every launch, and either re-runs the gate against any leftover provisional capture (promoting to a real document on accept) or deletes it deterministically (on reject or on missing/corrupt data). Exit criteria for this milestone: a kill-mid-write test proving the prior checkpoint survives untouched, plus five interruption-point kill tests — captured, normalized, traced, edited, and the mid-gate-evaluation staging gap — all passing before any other lane is allowed to build against `TraceStore` for real. This milestone also delivers, as explicit deliverables (not side effects): fixture production (the four locked sketches, the seven-reason bad-photo set, the adversarial nested-loop case) starting day one since it has zero code dependency, and fake/protocol-backed implementations of every service (`CaptureService`, `ImportService`, `QualityGateService`, `VectorizationEngine`) returning canned-but-fixture-realistic results, so downstream lanes never serialize behind the hardest subsystem. No auth/login scaffolding — there is none in this app.

**Milestone 2 — Capture, import, normalization, quality gate.**
Real camera/import intake, orientation-safe `CapturedImage` handling into the staging mechanism from milestone 1, normalization (perspective, EXIF strip), and the seven named rejection reasons with the combined quality-gate/correction screen (checking / rejected / corrected-preview-ready / correction-failed / resuming-from-checkpoint variants). First fully user-visible slice: import a fixture or real photo, get either a named rejection or a corrected preview, survive camera-permission denial cleanly. Mitigation against false acceptance (bad input silently passing the gate and surfacing later as a "vectorization problem"): threshold tuning validated against the full bad-photo fixture set, not just a couple of good photos.

**Milestone 2a — Vectorization spike (first task inside the vectorization milestone, not a separate pre-step).**
Run the single simplest fixture (icon sketch) all the way from normalized image through contour extraction, simplification, curve fitting, to a rasterized-and-diffed SVG, with node-count reporting. This is a hard go/no-go checkpoint: it calibrates the real estimate for milestone 3 rather than treating vectorization like a normally-sizable feature. If the locked clean-trace bar isn't reachable on even the simplest case in the time budgeted, that's the moment to renegotiate scope — not three weeks before ship.

**Milestone 3 — Production vectorization + before/after review.**
Full actor-isolated `VectorizationEngine` covering all four fixture classes (icon, lettering, closed-shape, messy notebook), named progress stages, and the before/after review screen. Acceptance bar is the locked one: bounded node counts, no sub-3pt orphan fragments, continuous strokes where expected, correct winding/fill, clean downstream-open in a real vector tool. Explicit rule carried from round 1 and reaffirmed by both agents: if this milestone underperforms, fix the tracing heuristics — do not compensate by growing editor scope.

**Milestone 4 — Bounded cleanup + export.**
Select/delete path, debounced simplify-strength slider (downsampled preview, full-res on commit), merge paths, undo/redo through `EditEngine.apply` as a pure reducer, VoiceOver-accessible path list, and the SVG/PDF export sheet. Deliberately the smallest, lowest-risk milestone by design, since the edit surface is narrow and operates on an already-stable `VectorDocument`. Can start against milestone 1's fake services in parallel with milestone 3 if vectorization is running long.

**Milestone 5 — Integration hardening and trust polish.**
Recents home, reopen/delete flows, resume banners (with `acknowledgeInterruptionRecovery()` wired so banners show once), export-failure recovery, settings/privacy copy, full empty/loading/success/error coverage, and the complete kill-and-relaunch matrix proven end-to-end as a system (not just per-checkpoint unit tests). This is also where `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` gets finalized against actual API usage and real-device performance/thermal validation (debounced slider, Vectorize under load) happens on physical hardware, not just simulator.

**Lanes:** dependency-aligned, not screen-aligned — (1) models/persistence/staging/reconciliation, (2) capture/normalize/gate, (3) vectorization/export correctness, (4) SwiftUI screens/router/accessibility on top of the other three's contracts. Integration and checkpoint verification run continuously starting in milestone 1, never as an end-of-project phase.

**Core risk framing both agents converged on:** this app's real danger isn't a missing feature, it's internal state silently meaning something other than what the code assumes — on the lifecycle axis (a captured-but-not-gated folder that isn't what its checkpoint stage implies) and on the quality axis (a photo that passes the gate but shouldn't have). Both get mitigated the same way: make the ambiguous states impossible to represent (staging vs. real `TraceDocument`) or impossible to miss (fixture-set thresholds tested against deliberately bad inputs, not just good ones).
phase_outputs.task_assignments:
This round is where it actually clicks shut. Codex came in and, instead of doubling down on his own coarser 12-task cut, endorsed Claude's fine-grained 29-task backlog outright — he even posted the same task list back, task-for-task, as his round-2 answer. His pushback landed on two specific tightenings rather than the granularity fight: making `App/AppModel.swift`/`TraceSession.swift`/`AppRouter.swift` an explicit `primary_ui`-only forbidden zone (services can define protocols, they don't reach into session orchestration), and reinforcing that nobody "just tweaks a field" in another lane's `CleanlineModels` file — contract changes get raised in-thread and made by the owning lane. He also introduced a better branching idea: a continuous `cleanline/integration` branch that all four lane branches merge into constantly, instead of only meeting each other at milestone boundaries.

Claude took that branch idea and improved it rather than just accepting it — keep `cleanline/integration` as the continuous collision-catcher, but gate `main` itself on milestone-boundary promotion out of `integration` once that milestone's exit criteria actually pass, so `main` still means "this milestone is proven," not just "it compiled." Claude also called out a real self-contradiction worth fixing: don't make "project wiring" (`.pbxproj`, root `Package.swift`) an owned task assigned to one lane, because that quietly makes whichever lane holds it the permanent integrator by construction — instead it's a standing, continuous integrator responsibility checked at every merge into `integration`, never a discrete deliverable. Both agents ended up in the same place on this without Codex needing to re-type agreement, since Codex's own round-2 tasks-json already reflects the 29-task structure with no separate "project wiring" task in it.

Testing ownership also landed clean: each `services_utilities` package tests its own behavior as part of the task that introduces it, `primary_ui` owns view-state/navigation correctness including fake-backed demoability, `polish_resilience` owns only cross-cutting system verification (kill/relaunch matrix, accessibility, privacy manifest, device perf, state-coverage audit) and never patches another lane's file directly — it files bugs back to the owner. `data_domain` owns schema stability and Codable round-trip tests.

Gemini's still unreachable, but between the two active participants there's no live disagreement left — Codex explicitly signed off on the fine-grained list, tightened two forbidden zones Claude adopted, proposed the integration-branch model Claude then refined, and Claude's harder critiques were aimed at resolving contradictions in earlier drafts, not at anything still standing in the final proposal.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Location:** `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/cleanline/` — self-contained, one Xcode project, no sharing with sibling portfolio apps.

**Lane responsibilities:**
- `data_domain` — pure value types in `CleanlineModels` only. No logic. If a task needs logic, it isn't this lane's task.
- `services_utilities` — every protocol-backed engine: capture, import, normalize, quality-gate, vectorize, edit-reducer, export, persistence/`TraceStore`, plus the fixture-backed fakes. Owns its own unit/fixture tests as part of each task, not as separate QA.
- `primary_ui` — `AppModel`, `TraceSession`, `AppRouter`, everything under `Features/`, and `CleanlineDesignSystem`. Owns view-state and navigation-correctness testing for what it builds.
- `polish_resilience` — cross-cutting resilience/accessibility types, fixture *production* (not fake services), resume-banner wiring, and all system-level verification: kill-and-relaunch matrix, accessibility passes, privacy manifest, device performance, and state-coverage audit. Never patches another lane's implementation file directly — files it back to the owner instead.

**Forbidden edit zones:**
- `App/AppModel.swift`, `App/TraceSession.swift`, `App/AppRouter.swift` — `primary_ui` only, no exceptions once T-006 lands.
- `Packages/CleanlineVectorize`, `CleanlineEdit`, `CleanlineExport`, `CleanlinePersistence`, `CleanlineCapture`, `CleanlineNormalize`, `CleanlineQualityGate` — `services_utilities` only.
- `Tests/CleanlineIntegrationTests`, `CleanlineUITests`, `CleanlinePerformanceTests`, `CleanlineStateCoverageTests`, `App/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy`, `Fixtures/` — `polish_resilience` only.
- `Packages/CleanlineModels/Sources/CleanlineModels/*` — shared directory, but ownership resolved by exact filename (T-001/T-002/T-003 split), never by directory. No lane edits another lane's file in there; contract changes get raised in-thread, the owning lane makes the edit.
- `Cleanline.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj` and root `Package.swift` — not owned by any single lane as a deliverable task. Treated as a standing integrator responsibility (rotates, doesn't accrue permanently to one lane), touched only during continuous merges into `cleanline/integration`, never edited opportunistically mid-task.

**Branching & merge strategy:** `cleanline/main` stays always-buildable and only advances via milestone-boundary promotion from `cleanline/integration` once that milestone's locked exit criteria pass. Each lane has a long-lived branch (`lane/data-domain`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/polish-resilience`); tasks branch off as `task/T-0NN-slug`, merge back into their lane branch when acceptance criteria pass, and lane branches merge continuously into `cleanline/integration` — that's where cross-lane breakage surfaces early, not at a big-bang milestone merge.

**Communication protocol:** post "claiming T-0xx, editing [files]" on task start; raise contract concerns before writing code, not after; every handoff states what's stable, what's fake-backed, and what's still blocked. Daily check isn't "what did you do," it's "did any file boundary or dependency assumption become false" — if yes, stop and resolve before continuing.

**Conflict-prevention plan:** file-level ownership eliminates almost all contention by construction; the one shared directory (`CleanlineModels`) is split by exact filename, not folder; the one real hotspot (`project.pbxproj`) is handled as a continuous integrator sweep rather than a lane-owned task; edit/export are deliberately unblocked against fakes (T-008) rather than real vectorization so the riskiest subsystem (T-014→T-015) never becomes a serialization bottleneck for the rest of the build.

**Acceptance checks:** all 29 tasks have exactly one owning lane, `depends_on` is acyclic, and T-001 and T-007 have zero dependencies so build workers can start immediately.
